title: Getting started
guided:
# Getting started

Welcome. This page walks you through what Console is, how it runs on your machine, how to open your first project, and how to find your way around. You don't need to be an experienced developer to follow it. Where a software-engineering idea comes up, there's an info callout that explains it honestly, so you finish this page knowing a little more than when you started.

## What Console is

Console is a desktop app that sits on top of a code project you have on your computer and helps you understand it and ship it. You point it at a folder, and it reads the project the way an experienced engineer would on their first day: what language and frameworks it uses, how the pieces fit together, what's healthy and what's broken, and what you'd need to do to get it live on the internet.

It does a few distinct things:

- **Reads your project** and writes you a plain report: the stack, the structure, a health score, and what to fix first.
- **Maps the architecture** as a diagram of which parts of the code depend on which other parts.
- **Connects to the services** your project uses or could use, such as GitHub (where code lives), Vercel (where you deploy), Sentry (which catches errors after launch), and others.
- **Advises you** through a built-in AI assistant that can read your actual code to ground its answers.
- **Deploys** to Vercel with no configuration, so "make it live" is a button rather than an afternoon.

> [!INFO] What "deploy" means
> To **deploy** is to take the code on your computer and put a running copy of it on a server somewhere on the internet, so other people can use it at a real web address. Until you deploy, your project only runs on `localhost` ŌĆö your own machine. Deploying is the step that turns "it works for me" into "anyone can visit it."

> [!INFO] What "a repo" is
> A **repository** (repo for short) is just the folder that holds your project, tracked by a tool called Git. Git records every change you make as a "commit," so you have a full history and can undo mistakes. When Console asks you to "open a project," it's asking for the folder that is your repo.

## Local-first: it runs on your machine, not in the cloud

Console is **local-first**. That's an important promise, so it's worth being precise about what it means.

- The app runs **on your computer**. It reads the files in the folder you open, directly from your disk.
- Console does **not** upload your code to a Console server. There is no Console cloud that stores a copy of your project.
- Everything you see in a panel is **derived live from files already in your repo** ŌĆö your source files, your `.github/workflows`, your release checklists, your run records. Console reflects the truth on disk; it doesn't keep a second copy.

> [!INFO] Why "local-first" matters
> A lot of developer tools work by uploading your project to their servers and showing you a website. That's convenient but it means your code leaves your machine. **Local-first** means the opposite: the tool comes to your code, not your code to the tool. Your source never has to leave your computer for Console to do its job, which matters a great deal when the code is private or unfinished.

A couple of consequences fall out of this design, and they're good ones to understand early:

- **An empty panel is usually a signal, not a bug.** If the Architecture panel is blank, it generally means Console didn't find the files it draws from yet ŌĆö not that it crashed. Add the file, and the panel fills in on the next read.
- **Your keys stay private.** When you connect a service that needs a secret token, that secret is held by Console's background process (the part of the app that talks to your operating system), never handed back to the visible part of the app, and never sent to the AI. The screen only ever sees a yes/no "Connected" status and a redacted preview.

> [!INFO] What "a token" or "a key" is
> Many services give you a long secret string ŌĆö a **token** or **API key** ŌĆö that proves you're allowed to use them, a bit like a password that programs use instead of you typing one. Because anyone holding it can act as you, it must be kept secret. Console treats these tokens carefully: it stores them in your operating system's secure keychain and never shows them back on screen.

## Opening a project

The first thing Console needs is a project folder to look at.

The very first time you launch Console, you'll see a short **onboarding flow** (covered below). One of its steps asks you to pick your project folder. After that first run, you can open or switch projects at any time from the top bar.

To open or switch a project later:

1. Look at the **project chip** in the top bar ŌĆö the little button with a folder icon. If you have a project open, it shows the folder's name; if not, it says "No project, click to pick."
2. Click it. A menu drops down with:
   - **Recent projects** ŌĆö up to six folders you've opened before, each showing its name and full path. Click one to switch to it.
   - **New project in a new window** ŌĆö opens a fresh Console window. Use this when you want two projects open side by side; each window remembers its own active project.
   - **Open a project in this windowŌĆ”** ŌĆö opens a folder picker and switches the current window to whatever folder you choose.
   - **Open in file explorer** ŌĆö reveals the current project folder in your operating system's file browser (this is greyed out until you have a project open).

> [!INFO] Why several windows?
> Console scopes the "active project" to each **window**, while the recent-projects list is shared across all of them. So you can have your main app open in one window and a side project in another, working on both at once, without them stepping on each other. A project you open in one window still shows up in another window's "Recent" list.

When you pick a folder, Console reads it and the panels fill in. Naming follows the folder: the operating-system window title becomes "`your-project` - Console" so you can tell windows apart on the taskbar.

## The first-run onboarding

The first time you launch (or any time you choose "Run onboarding again" from the command palette ŌĆö see below), Console walks you through a short setup wizard. You don't have to get everything right; most steps are skippable, and you can change every choice later in Settings. The stages are, roughly, in order:

1. **Pick a pace.** A welcome screen ŌĆö "You built it. Let's ship it." ŌĆö offers two buttons: **Jump in** (the Operator view) or **Guide me through it** (the Guided view). This sets your *mode*, explained in the next section. Don't overthink it; you can switch any time.
2. **A quick tour** of the main features (skippable).
3. **Sign in** and **set up AI** ŌĆö both optional, both skippable. You can add an AI provider now or later.
4. **Connect your project** ŌĆö pick the folder. This one is required, because everything else reads from it.
5. **Study** ŌĆö Console reads the project and builds its profile.
6. **Report** ŌĆö it shows you what it found, and you confirm "Looks good."
7. **Goals** ŌĆö tell it what you're trying to do.
8. **Services** ŌĆö optionally connect things like GitHub now (skippable, "Finish").
9. **Done** ŌĆö you land on the Overview.

## The two modes: Guided and Operator

Console has two *modes*, and the whole app changes shape depending on which one you're in. They show the same real data ŌĆö they differ in how much they explain and how densely they pack it.

- **Guided** is the teaching view. It uses plain-language names, a shorter and journey-ordered menu, more breathing room, and explanatory notes. It's the view you're reading these docs in. Start here if you're new to building software or new to Console.
- **Operator** is the dense view for experienced engineers. Same data, fewer words, a fuller menu with technical names, tighter spacing. Switch to it once you know your way around and want to move fast.

You're not locked in. To switch:

- Click the **mode pill** in the top bar (it shows "Guided" or "Operator"). One click flips it.
- Or open the command palette (next section) and choose "Switch mode."
- Or go to **Settings ŌåÆ Display ŌåÆ Mode**.

> [!INFO] Why two modes?
> Good tools meet you where you are. A beginner and a ten-year veteran need different things from the same screen: one wants the concept explained, the other wants the number and nothing else. Rather than compromise on a middle that serves neither, Console ships both and lets you choose. Switching is instant and the whole UI re-renders, so feel free to flip back and forth as you learn.

## Light and dark theme

Independently of mode, you can pick a **theme** ŌĆö how the app looks.

- Click the **sun/moon button** in the top bar to flip between light and dark instantly.
- In **Settings ŌåÆ Display ŌåÆ Theme** you get a third option, **System**, which makes Console follow whatever your operating system is set to (so it goes dark when your OS goes dark).

Your choice is remembered between launches.

## The command palette (Ctrl+K)

The fastest way to get anywhere in Console is the **command palette**. Press **Ctrl+K** (or click the `Ctrl+K` button in the top bar) and a search box opens in the middle of the screen.

Start typing what you want. The palette searches three kinds of things:

- **Panels** ŌĆö jump straight to any screen (Overview, Workboard, Architecture, and so on), even ones hidden from your current menu.
- **Actions** ŌĆö do something without hunting for a button: toggle the theme, switch mode, connect GitHub or Vercel, deploy, open the assistant, run onboarding again, copy the eval command, open your project folder, and more.
- **Docs** ŌĆö open any page of these docs.

Press **Enter** to run the highlighted result, or **Esc** to close. The palette is smart about matching: it finds results by the words in their name or description, then re-ranks them by meaning, so a rough description of what you want usually surfaces the right thing.

> [!INFO] Why a command palette?
> As an app grows, hunting through menus gets slow. A **command palette** is a single search box that reaches everything ŌĆö every screen and every action ŌĆö by name. It's a pattern borrowed from code editors and modern tools because, once you know it exists, it's almost always faster than the mouse. If you only learn one shortcut in Console, make it Ctrl+K.

There's also a set of quick keyboard jumps if you like them: press **`g`** then a letter to jump to a panel (for example `g` then `o` goes to Overview, `g` then `s` to Services). Press **`?`** anywhere to see the full list. These are entirely optional ŌĆö Ctrl+K does everything they do.

## The overall layout

Once you're past onboarding, every Console window has the same frame. Knowing the four regions means you always know where you are.

**1. The top bar** runs across the top. Left to right it holds:
- The **Console** wordmark and "by Refringence."
- The **project chip** (open and switch projects ŌĆö covered above).
- On the right: a **mode pill** (Guided/Operator), an **Assistant** button (opens the chat, covered below), the **Ctrl+K** button, and the **theme** toggle. If an app update has downloaded, a "Restart to update" button appears here too.

**2. The sidebar** runs down the left. It's your menu of panels, grouped sensibly for your mode. In Guided mode the groups are plain-language: **Your project** (Overview, Project report, Your code, What to fix), **Ship it** (Connect & deploy, Release, Monitor), and **Help** (Tutorials, Docs, and "Read project files like a book"), plus a **More** disclosure for the rest, and Settings always at the bottom. You can collapse the whole sidebar to a thin strip of icons using the **Collapse** control at its bottom ŌĆö the icons stay, and hovering one shows its name.

> [!INFO] What these panels are, briefly
> Each sidebar entry is a different lens on your project. **Overview** is the dashboard. **Project report** is the deep read. **Your code** browses the files. **What to fix** is your task board. **Connect & deploy** wires up services. **Release** answers "is it ready to ship?" **Monitor** watches for errors after you go live. You'll meet each in its own doc page; for now, just know the sidebar is how you move between them.

**3. The main area** in the middle is the panel you've selected. This is where the actual work and information live, and it's the part that changes as you click around the sidebar.

**4. The assistant dock** opens on the right when you click the **Assistant** button (or choose "Open assistant" from the palette). It's a chat panel where you can ask questions; if you've connected an AI provider and have a project open, the assistant can read your real code to answer accurately. Opening it widens the window so it doesn't squeeze the main area. Close it with the ├Ś in its header.

> [!INFO] "Bring your own key"
> Console doesn't bundle an AI for you; instead you connect your own account with one of several providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, a local Ollama model, or Kimi) in **Settings ŌåÆ AI**. This is called **bring-your-own-key**. It means you control which model you use and what it costs, and your key stays on your machine. A locally-running Ollama model needs no key at all.

That's the whole frame: top bar, sidebar, main area, and the optional assistant dock. With those four regions and Ctrl+K, you can reach everything Console does. The next pages walk through each panel in turn.

# Getting started

A precise reference to Console's shell: what the app is, where its data comes from, how to open and scope projects, the persona/theme/density/layout axes, the command palette and keymap, and the window frame. Source of truth: `console-shell/src/views/_shell/`, `console-shell/src/lib/`, and `console-shell/src/views/welcome/`.

## What Console is

Local-first developer command-center, Electron main + React 19 / Vite / Tailwind v4 / Radix / cmdk renderer. It reads a local repo and derives every panel from files on disk; it does not maintain a server-side copy of project state. The renderer talks to the main process through a single typed IPC surface, `window.refringenceConsole`, wrapped by `lib/bridge.ts`. In browser dev (no main process) bridge calls fall back to throwing stubs. Raw secrets/keys/tokens are held main-side and never returned to the renderer ŌĆö the renderer receives boolean status, redacted previews, and metadata only.

## Local-first data model

- Every "data read" in a panel is a bridge call, usually wrapped in a TanStack-Query hook under `lib/queries/*`.
- Derived sources include: repo files, GitHub issues via the `gh` CLI, `.github/workflows`, release checklists under `docs/release-checklists/<version>.yaml`, run records under `.refringence-qa/`, project-scoped state under `.refringence-console/` (prompts, architecture overlay).
- An empty panel signals a missing underlying file/connection, not a crash. Panels re-read on next fetch; freshly added files/connections surface without restart.
- Secret handling: provider keys and service tokens are encrypted in the OS keychain main-side. Service `.env` values are read transiently main-side to test/store, then dropped ŌĆö never shown back, never sent to AI.

## Personas (Guided / Operator)

`lib/persona.ts`, `lib/usePersonaMode.ts`. localStorage key `refringence-console-persona`, values `newbie` | `seasoned` (internal enum; user-facing labels `PERSONA_LABEL = { newbie: 'Guided', seasoned: 'Operator' }`, the raw words never render).

- **No persona stored ŌåÆ route to `/welcome`** (onboarding). Once stored, `usePersonaMode` returns the value; UI defaults treat a missing value as `newbie` for label/render fallbacks.
- Persona drives: explainer hovers, default sidebar collapse, density default, palette placeholder, onboarding-banner visibility, and per-panel Guided/Operator variants (panels branch `if (isNewbie) return <XNewbie/>; return <XSeasoned/>`).
- Flip broadcasts `console-persona-change`; all mounted views re-render instantly. Flip surfaces: TopBar persona pill (`topbar-persona`), Sidebar persona chip (`sidebar-persona-chip`), CommandPalette "Switch mode", Settings ŌåÆ Display ŌåÆ Mode.

## Theme

localStorage key `refringence-console-theme`. TopBar toggle (`topbar-theme-toggle`) cycles **light Ōåö dark only**; Settings ŌåÆ Display ŌåÆ Theme adds **System** (`prefers-color-scheme`). Toggling writes the key, toggles `.dark` on `<html>`, and dispatches `console-theme-change` for cross-component sync. `WindowControls` and all theme-aware components listen on that event. TopBar reads stored value as `'dark' | 'light'` (anything not `dark` ŌåÆ `light`).

## Density

`lib/density.ts`, key `refringence-console-density`, values `compact` | `roomy`, default `compact` (Guided surfaces `roomy`). Editable in Settings ŌåÆ Display ŌåÆ Density.

## Console layout presets

`lib/consoleLayout.ts`, key `refringence-console-layout-v1`. Two bits: `railCollapsed` (sidebar shows icon rail only) + `chatOpen` (right chat dock open). This module is the single owner of the rail bit. Presets:

| Preset | railCollapsed | chatOpen |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Standard (default) | false | false |
| Focus | true | false |
| Chat | true | true |

`useConsoleLayout` exposes `setPreset`, `toggleRail`, `setRailCollapsed`, `toggleChat`, plus `detectPreset` (returns `custom` when bits match no preset). Cross-instance/cross-window sync via `console-layout-change` custom event + `storage` event. Set from CommandPalette ("Layout: Standard/Focus/Chat", "Toggle sidebar rail") and the Sidebar Collapse control.

## Opening and scoping projects

`lib/activeProject.ts`. The **active project is scoped per window** via the `?wid=` query the main process appends (survives reloads); keys `refringence-console-active-project:<wid>` and `ŌĆ”-pickedAt:<wid>`. The **recent list is global**: key `refringence-console-recent-projects-v1`, cap 6. Changes broadcast `console-active-project-change` (+ `storage` event for cross-window).

TopBar project chip (`topbar-project-chip`) dropdown:

- **Recent projects** ŌĆö up to 6, name + full path; select ŌåÆ `setProject(path)`.
- **New project in a new window** (`topbar-new-window`) ŌåÆ `bridge.window.newWindow()` (fresh window starts at onboarding).
- **Open a project in this windowŌĆ”** ŌåÆ `bridge.project.pickFolder()` ŌåÆ on non-cancel, `setProject(res.path)`.
- **Open in file explorer** ŌåÆ `bridge.openPath(project.path)`; disabled when no active project.

On project change the OS window title is set via `bridge.window.setTitle` to `"<basename> - Console"` (or `"Console"` when none).

## First-run onboarding

`views/welcome/OnboardingWizard.tsx`, state machine `lib/onboarding/machine.ts`. Window-scoped via `?wid=`; frameless with its own drag strip + `WindowControls` (no TopBar). Stages and gating:

1. **persona** ŌĆö `PersonaLanding`, "You built it. Let's ship it." Buttons: **Jump in** (`persona-seasoned`) ŌåÆ writes `seasoned`; **Guide me through it** (`persona-newbie`) ŌåÆ writes `newbie`. Then ŌåÆ tour.
2. **tour** ŌĆö skippable carousel (`TourStep`) ŌåÆ signin.
3. **signin** (`SignInStep`, skippable) ŌåÆ **ai** (`AiSetupStep`, skippable) ŌåÆ **connect** (`ConnectRepoStep`, **required**: Next disabled until `projectRoot` set; writes active project) ŌåÆ **study** (`StudyStep`, footer hidden; `bridge.intel.mountStart`) ŌåÆ **report** (`ReportStep`, Next label "Looks good") ŌåÆ **intent** (`IntentStep`) ŌåÆ **services** (`ServicesStep`, skippable, Next label "Finish") ŌåÆ **done** (`DoneStep`, footer hidden).

Footer (when shown): Back (`onb-back`) / Skip (`onb-skip`, on signin/ai/services) / Continue (`onb-next`). Finish writes both onboarded flags (`writeOnboarded` global + `writeOnboardedForWindow`) ŌåÆ navigate `/overview`. CommandPalette "Run onboarding again" clears `refringence-console-onboarded` + the per-window flag ŌåÆ `/welcome`.

## Command palette (Ctrl+K)

`views/_shell/CommandPalette.tsx`, Radix Dialog + `cmdk` (testid `command-palette`). Opened by the `console-open-palette` window event, dispatched by TopBar's `topbar-cmdk` button and by `CommandPaletteHotkey` (registers Ctrl+K / Cmd+K to toggle). Input placeholder: "Jump to a panel, run an action, or open a docŌĆ”". Footer: Esc to close, Enter to select.

Matching: `shouldFilter={false}`. Own substring match on `label + description`, then re-rank by semantic similarity (`rankBySemanticSimilarity`, `lib/cmdk/semantic-rank`), debounced 150ms; empty query ŌåÆ alphabetical. Three groups:

- **Panels** ŌĆö flattened from `GROUPS` (the canonical Operator set in `Sidebar.tsx`). Each row shows its group badge; a **Hidden** badge appears when the path is in the saved sidebar `hidden` set.
- **Actions** ŌĆö Toggle theme; Switch mode (Guided/Operator); Run onboarding again; Connect GitHub / Connect Vercel / Connect repo (ŌåÆ `/services`); Run eval (ŌåÆ `/observability`); Deploy (ŌåÆ `/release`); Open assistant (`toggleChat`); Open prompt library (ŌåÆ `/prompts`); Toggle sidebar rail; Layout: Standard / Focus / Chat; Copy eval command (`npm run eval:promptfoo` ŌåÆ clipboard + toast); Open project folder (`bridge.openPath` active project, toast on no-project or failure); Open repo on GitHub (`github.com/Refringence-AI/console`); Keyboard shortcuts (opens help dialog).
- **Docs** ŌĆö from `CONSOLE_DOC_PAGES` (`console-docs-index.ts`).

Selecting a panel/doc navigates; an action runs its `run()`. The palette also hosts `useKeymap({ enabled: !open })` and the Shortcuts help dialog (`shortcuts-help`).

## Keyboard shortcuts

`lib/useKeymap.ts`. Global handler; yields while target is INPUT/TEXTAREA/SELECT/contentEditable, while any modifier (meta/ctrl/alt) is held, or while the palette is open. g-prefix chord buffer clears after 1500ms.

g-prefix navigation chords (`NAV_CHORDS`):

| Chord | Route | Chord | Route |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `g o` | /overview | `g l` | /release |
| `g w` | /issues (Workboard) | `g y` | /observability |
| `g r` | /repo | `g c` | /activity |
| `g a` | /arch | `g t` | /tutorials |
| `g p` | /pipeline | `g d` | /docs |
| `g s` | /services | `g ,` | /settings |
| `g b` | /library | | |

Other: **`?`** opens the shortcuts help dialog; **Ctrl+K / Cmd+K** toggles the palette. In ChatComposer: Enter sends, Shift+Enter newline. In Prompts (Operator) detail: Ōåæ/Ōåō navigate, ŌīśC copy filled.

## Window frame

`views/_shell/ConsoleShell.tsx`: `TopBar` (full width) over `Sidebar | <Outlet/> | ConsoleChatDock`. Mounts `CommandPalette` + `CommandPaletteHotkey`; "Skip to content" a11y link; each route wrapped in `PanelErrorBoundary` keyed by pathname. Frameless window ŌĆö `WindowControls` (minimize/maximize/close) rendered in React.

### TopBar (`top-bar`)

Left: `console.svg` + "CONSOLE" wordmark + "by Refringence"; then the project chip. Right cluster (`ml-auto`): `UpdatePill` (`topbar-update`, shown only when `useUpdate().status === 'downloaded'`, click ŌåÆ `update.install()`); persona pill (`topbar-persona`); Assistant toggle (`topbar-ai`, `aria-pressed=chatOpen`, on open calls `bridge.window.growForDock(360)`); Ctrl+K button (`topbar-cmdk`); theme toggle (`topbar-theme-toggle`). Whole bar is an OS drag region except interactive controls.

### Sidebar (`sidebar`, `data-rail-collapsed`)

Persona-aware IA. Width animates between `FULL_WIDTH=224` and `RAIL_WIDTH=56`; rail keeps icons + tooltips, active row keeps a 2px accent bar (`nav-active-bar-<panel>`). Nav testids are route-canonical (`NAV_TESTID`) regardless of display label.

- **Operator** renders `GROUPS`: **Project** (Overview, Report, Prompts, Workboard `/issues`, Repo, Architecture, Pipeline, Services, Library, Release, Observability, Activity) and **Help** (Tutorials, Docs, Settings). Order / hide / group-collapse come from saved sidebar layout (`lib/sidebarLayout.ts`, key `ŌĆ”sidebar-layout-v1`). `SOFT_HIDDEN = {/activity, /library, /docs, /tutorials}` default off the rail (palette-only) until the layout is explicitly customized. LOCKED-never-hideable: Overview, Release, Pipeline.
- **Guided** renders `GUIDED_GROUPS` (plain-language, same routes): **Your project** (Overview, "Project report", "Your code" `/repo`, "What to fix" `/issues`); **Ship it** ("Connect & deploy" `/services`, Release, "Monitor" `/observability`); **Help** (Tutorials, Docs, "Read project files like a book" `/library`). `GUIDED_MORE` disclosure (Architecture, Pipeline, Activity, Prompts) + Settings always shown.
- Proactive nav badges: danger dot on `/observability` when `useObsCounters().errors_last_24h > 0`; warning dot on `/release` when latest release summary is blocked/red. One OS notification (`bridge.notify`) per mount when 24h errors cross 0ŌåÆpositive.
- Footer: Collapse toggle (`sidebar-rail-toggle`, drives `toggleRail`), persona chip (`sidebar-persona-chip`), version chip "v0.1" (`sidebar-version-chip`).

### ConsoleChatDock

Right-hand 360px AnimatePresence column, open state = `consoleLayout.chatOpen`. Header "Assistant" + close (`console-chat-dock-close`). Embeds `<ChatPanel embedded/>`. The assistant is the dock, not a nav panel; passing `projectRoot` to `bridge.ai.chat.start` gives it read-only tools + project context.

## At a glance

| Surface | Storage key | Owner |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Persona | `refringence-console-persona` | `lib/persona.ts` |
| Theme | `refringence-console-theme` | TopBar / Settings |
| Density | `refringence-console-density` | `lib/density.ts` |
| Layout (rail + chat) | `refringence-console-layout-v1` | `lib/consoleLayout.ts` |
| Sidebar layout | `ŌĆ”sidebar-layout-v1` | `lib/sidebarLayout.ts` |
| Active project (per window) | `ŌĆ”active-project:<wid>` | `lib/activeProject.ts` |
| Recent projects (global) | `ŌĆ”recent-projects-v1` | `lib/activeProject.ts` |
| AI tier | `refringence-console-ai-tier` | `lib/ai/registry.ts` |
| Onboarded flags | `refringence-console-onboarded` (+ per-window) | `lib/onboarded*.ts` |

title: Overview
guided:
# Overview

The Overview panel is the first thing Console shows you after you open a project. It is your project's front page: one screen that answers the single most useful question a builder can ask ŌĆö *"What is the state of my project right now, and what should I do next?"*

Everything on this page is read live from your actual project (your git history, your config files, the services you have connected). Nothing here is invented or filled in with placeholder data. If a number looks empty, it is because that thing genuinely has not happened yet.

> [!INFO] **What "live state" means.** Console does not store a copy of your project and show you a stale snapshot. Each time this panel loads, it re-reads the real files and re-runs the checks. A few numbers refresh on their own roughly once a minute. So when you fix something and come back, the page reflects it.

The page is laid out **most-urgent-first**, top to bottom. The closer to the top something is, the more it wants your attention.

---

## The top line: "Needs attention" or "All systems healthy"

The very first thing under the title is a single strip. It is the page's headline.

- **Green strip ŌĆö "All systems healthy. Nothing needs your attention right now."** Everything Console checks is in good shape. You can relax or keep building.
- **Amber/red strip ŌĆö "Needs attention:"** followed by a short list of dots. Each dot is one real problem, and clicking it jumps you straight to the panel where you can deal with it.

The problems that can appear here:

- **"N gates failing"** ŌĆö one of your release checks failed. Jumps to **Release**.
- **"N gates blocked"** ŌĆö a release check is waiting on you to do something. Jumps to **Release**.
- **"N errors in 24h"** ŌĆö your running app reported errors in the last day. Jumps to **Observability**.
- **"N eval failures"** ŌĆö your AI-quality tests failed. Jumps to **Observability**.

> [!INFO] **Why "most urgent first" is a real engineering habit.** Good dashboards do not show you everything at equal weight ŌĆö they triage. A pilot's cockpit puts the "engine on fire" light where you cannot miss it and the cabin temperature in a corner. This strip is your "engine on fire" light. When it is green, you have permission to stop scanning the rest of the page for hidden trouble.

---

## "Is it ready to ship?" ŌĆö the ship card and the gate ring

Below the headline strip sit two cards side by side. Together they answer the one question that matters before a release: *can I ship this?*

### The ship card (left)

A plain-English verdict in a big sentence, plus one button:

- **"Ready to ship."** ŌĆö every release check passed. The button reads **Ship release** and takes you to the Release panel to cut it.
- **"Almost ready to ship."** ŌĆö just a couple of checks left. The button reads **Open Release**.
- **"Not ready to ship."** / **"Checks pending."** ŌĆö there is failing or unfinished work. The button reads **Open Release**.

The card never repeats the exact problem count (the strip at the top already said that). Instead it gives you the verdict and a forward-looking next move.

### The release-gate ring (right)

A donut chart that breaks your release checks into three coloured slices, each with a matching labelled row beside it:

- **Passing** (green) ŌĆö checks that are done and good.
- **Blocked** (amber) ŌĆö checks waiting on your input. (Failing checks are folded in here too, because both mean "you need to act.")
- **Pending** (grey) ŌĆö checks not yet run or reviewed.

The middle of the ring reads something like **"7/12 passing"** so you can see progress at a glance. Clicking anywhere on this card opens the **Release** panel.

> [!INFO] **What a "release gate" actually is.** A *gate* is a checkpoint that a release must pass before it is allowed out the door ŌĆö "tests pass", "no known security holes", "the changelog is updated", and so on. The set of gates is your *release checklist*. Professional teams gate releases so that shipping is a deliberate decision backed by evidence, not a hopeful "looks fine to me." Console reads your checklist (a file in `docs/release-checklists/`) and shows you, honestly, how many gates are green.
>
> A **health score** or a **pass ratio** like "7 of 12" is engineering's favourite shape for this: it turns a yes/no question ("am I done?") into a progress bar you can chip away at.

---

## Vitals ŌĆö the four number cards

Next is a row of small cards, each a single important number. Think of them as your project's pulse, blood pressure, and temperature. Each card is clickable and takes you to the panel with the full story.

### Tests

How many test runs finished in the last 24 hours, and whether any of them had errors.

> [!INFO] **What "tests" are.** A *test* is a small piece of code whose only job is to check that your real code behaves correctly ŌĆö it runs your function with a known input and asserts the output is what you expected. *Running your tests* means executing all of them at once and counting how many passed. A project with passing tests is one you can change without holding your breath, because the tests will shout if you broke something. This card counts how often that safety net has run recently.

### Build

Whether your latest **CI** run passed, failed, or is still running. If your project has no CI set up, this reads **"idle"** with **"No CI runs found"** ŌĆö which is not a failure, just an absence.

> [!INFO] **What "CI" (Continuous Integration) is.** CI is a robot that watches your code repository. Every time you push a change, it automatically downloads your project on a fresh machine, installs everything, builds it, and runs your tests ŌĆö then reports pass or fail. The point is to catch "it works on my machine but nowhere else" problems immediately, and to keep the main branch always in a working state. The config lives in a `.github/workflows/` folder; Console reads the result of the most recent run.

### Cost today

How much you have spent on AI through Console today, in dollars. If you have not used any paid AI yet, it reads **"No AI spend tracked yet."**

> [!INFO] **Why a tool tracks AI cost.** AI models charge by the amount of text they read and write. It is easy to lose track and get a surprising bill. Showing the running daily total ŌĆö the way a taxi meter shows the fare as you ride ŌĆö keeps the cost visible while it is still small enough to act on.

### Evals

Your AI-quality test results, shown as a pass-rate percentage with a "X pass ┬Ę Y fail" breakdown, plus a count of components in your SBOM if one exists. If you have never run them, it reads **"No runs"**.

> [!INFO] **What an "eval" is.** An *eval* (short for evaluation) is a test, but for AI behaviour instead of ordinary code. Normal tests check exact answers; AI gives fuzzy, varied answers, so an eval runs a set of prompts through your AI and grades whether the responses are good enough against expected outcomes. It is how you stop your AI feature from silently getting worse. **SBOM** stands for *Software Bill of Materials* ŌĆö a complete list of every third-party package your project depends on, like an ingredients label on food. It matters for security: when a popular package is found to have a hole, you check your SBOM to see if you are affected.

### A note on empty cards

When you have run **no** tests **and** no evals, Console does not show you two sad blank cards. It collapses them into one friendly card ŌĆö **"No test or eval runs yet"** with a **Run a test** button that takes you to Observability to start your first run. This is deliberate: a clean, honest "you have not done this yet, here is how to start" beats two hollow zeroes.

### Deployment (only when connected)

A fifth card appears **only if you have connected Vercel** (a hosting service) and deployed at least once. It shows the status of your last deployment (e.g. **READY**) and the live URL, which you can click to open your site in a browser.

> [!INFO] **What "deploying" means.** *Deployment* is the act of taking the code on your computer and putting a running copy on the public internet so other people can visit it. "Zero-config" deployment, which Console supports through Vercel, means Console figures out the build settings for you so you do not have to learn them first. This card is your receipt: it tells you the latest version is live and gives you the link.

---

## "Next" ŌĆö what to do now

Below the vitals is a short list (one to three items) of concrete next steps, computed from the real state of your project. Each is a single clickable line that takes you where the work happens.

These are not chatbot answers and not generic advice ŌĆö they are rules reading your actual signals. For example: if a release gate is blocked, it suggests opening Release; if you have never run evals, it suggests running them. If everything is genuinely fine, this section quietly says so.

> [!INFO] **Why a "next step" list helps you learn.** The hardest part of a new project is not doing the work ŌĆö it is knowing *which* work to do first. By always surfacing the single highest-value next action, Console teaches you the order experienced engineers work in: clear blockers, keep your safety nets fresh, then build. Follow the suggestions for a few weeks and the priorities start to feel obvious on their own.

---

## "Recent activity" ŌĆö your project's history

At the bottom is a feed of your most recent git commits: the actual changes saved to your project, newest first, with a short description, a relative time ("3 hours ago"), and a short code (the commit hash). **View all** opens the full history in the Activity panel.

If this is empty, it usually means the folder is not a git repository yet, or git is not installed.

> [!INFO] **What a "commit" is.** *Git* is the system that records the history of your project. A *commit* is a single saved snapshot ŌĆö a checkpoint with a message describing what changed ("fix login bug", "add dark mode"). The string of commits is your project's complete, undo-able timeline. Reading this feed is like flipping through a logbook: it tells you what has been happening and reassures you that the project is alive and moving.

---

## The Guided Overview, specifically

You are reading the **Guided** version of this panel (set by your persona toggle in the top bar). It is intentionally calmer and more narrative than the Operator version:

- A friendly headline ŌĆö **"How your project is doing"** ŌĆö and one plain-English sentence describing what your project *is* (e.g. "A Node.js web app. Start with `npm start`.").
- A single bold **Next step** line ŌĆö the one thing most worth doing now.
- An **"Is it ready to ship?"** card with the big gate number and a plain explanation.
- **The numbers that matter**: AI spend today, Progress (commits landed), and Tested (test runs in 24h) ŌĆö and if all three are zero, one quiet line instead of three empty cards.
- A **"Lately"** activity list (your last few commits).
- An **Explore** row pointing you at Workboard, Read the repo, and Tutorials.

It deliberately leaves out the denser details (SBOM counts, gate-by-gate breakdowns, raw line counts) ŌĆö those mean little before you know the project. Flip the persona toggle to **Operator** any time to see the full cockpit.

# Overview

Route `/overview`. Landing surface. Persona-branched at the top of `OverviewPanel`: `usePersonaMode().isNewbie` ŌåÆ `OverviewNewbie`, else `OverviewSeasoned`. This reference documents the **Operator** (`seasoned`) cockpit; the Guided variant is summarized at the end.

`data-testid="overview-panel"`. Vertically scrolling single column. Layout order, top to bottom: PanelHeader ŌåÆ AlertStrip ŌåÆ focal row (ShipCard + ReleaseGatesCard) ŌåÆ Vitals grid ŌåÆ Next (WhatsNextTile) ŌåÆ Recent activity ŌåÆ 32px tail spacer.

## Data sources

All reads are TanStack-Query hooks over `bridge.*`, keyed on the active project path (`useActiveProject().project.path`); each is window-scoped via the active-project query.

| Hook | Bridge call | Feeds | Cadence |
|---|---|---|---|
| `useMetricsSummary` | `metrics.summary(root)` | Build, Cost today, Evals, SBOM | `staleTime 30s`, `refetchInterval 60s` |
| `useReleaseList` | `release.list(root)` | resolves `[0].version` | `staleTime 30s` |
| `useReleaseSummary(version)` | `release.summary(root)` | ShipCard, ReleaseGatesCard, AlertStrip, WhatsNextTile | `staleTime 30s` |
| `useObsCounters` | `obs.counters(root)` | Tests vital, AlertStrip errors, WhatsNextTile | ŌĆö |
| `useRepoSummary` | `repo.summary(root)` | `total_loc` for shape subtitle | ŌĆö |
| `useRecentCommits(6)` | `activity.recentCommits` | Recent activity feed | ŌĆö |
| `useProjectShape(path)` | `repoIntrospect.shape` | header subtitle (`shapeSubtitle`) | ŌĆö |
| `useConnections` | `connections.list` | `vercel.connected` gate | ŌĆö |
| `useVercelDeployments(enabled)` | `connections.vercel.deployments` | DeploymentCell (enabled only when Vercel connected) | ŌĆö |
| `getLastDeploy(path)` | `lib/deployStore` (local, per-project) | scopes DeploymentCell to this project | synchronous |

`ReleaseSummary` shape: `{ version, overall_status: GateStatus, green, amber, red, blocked, gate_count }` where `GateStatus = 'green'|'amber'|'red'|'blocked'`. `MetricsSummary` relevant fields: `ci{configured,status:'passing'|'failing'|'running'|'none',conclusion,workflow,ranAt}`, `sbom{present,components,spec_version,size_bytes}`, `promptfoo{present,last_run,passed,failed,errors}`, `cost_today_usd`, `cycle_log{commits_landed,cycles_completed}`. `ObsCounters`: `{runs, runs_last_24h, errors, errors_last_24h}`.

## PanelHeader

Icon `LayoutDashboard`, title **"Overview"**, `testid="overview-panel-header"`. Subtitle = the project **shape line** from `shapeSubtitle(shape.data, repo.data?.total_loc)` (`data-testid="overview-shape-line"`), format `Type ┬Ę startCommand ┬Ę N packages ┬Ę N LOC` (each segment conditional). Fallback when shape unresolved: **"Live state of the project, most urgent first."**

## AlertStrip

`px-6 pt-4`. Aggregated banner, built in this push order from real signals:

1. `release.red > 0` ŌåÆ `"{n} gate(s) failing"`, tone `rose`, ŌåÆ `/release`.
2. `release.overall_status === 'blocked' && release.blocked > 0` ŌåÆ `"{n} gate(s) blocked"`, tone `amber`, ŌåÆ `/release`.
3. `obs.errors_last_24h > 0` ŌåÆ `"{n} error(s) in 24h"`, tone `rose`, ŌåÆ `/observability`.
4. `metrics.promptfoo.present && metrics.promptfoo.failed > 0` ŌåÆ `"{n} eval failure(s)"`, tone `rose`, ŌåÆ `/observability`.

States:
- **Loading** (`!release.data && release.isLoading`): 36px pulse skeleton.
- **Empty** (no alerts): success-tinted row, `CircleCheck` + **"All systems healthy."** + "Nothing needs your attention right now."
- **Populated**: warning-tinted row, `TriangleAlert` + **"Needs attention:"** + alerts joined by `┬Ę` separators. Each alert = tone dot (rose=`bg-danger`, amber=`bg-warning`) + label, wrapped in a `Link` to its `to` (hover underline).

The strip's first alert route (`alerts[0]?.to`) is passed to WhatsNextTile as `suppressTopTo` to de-dupe the headline against the Next list.

## Focal row

`px-6 pt-5`, `grid lg:grid-cols-2 gap-4`. ShipCard + ReleaseGatesCard. Both render loading skeletons while `!release.data && release.isLoading`.

### ShipCard

Verdict + single primary action. Verdict computed by `shipVerdict(release)` over `open = amber + red + blocked`:

- `open === 0` ŌåÆ **"Ready to ship."** / "Every release gate is passing. You are clear to cut a release." / `ready=true`.
- `green >= gate_count - open && open <= 2` ŌåÆ **"Almost ready to ship."** / "Clear the last few gates from the release checklist, then ship." / `ready=false`.
- else ŌåÆ headline **"Not ready to ship."** (when `red > 0`) or **"Checks pending."** (otherwise) / "Open the release checklist to see what each gate needs." / `ready=false`.

The detail line is intentionally forward-looking and never restates the alert count. Button: `variant="default"` (neutral-inverted), `size="lg"`, no chevron; label **"Ship release"** when `ready`, else **"Open Release"**; both `Link to="/release"`.

### ReleaseGatesCard

Entire card is a `Link to="/release"`. `Donut` (size 84, stroke 9), `ariaLabel="{green} of {gate_count} gates passing"`, three segments:

- `green` ŌåÆ `emerald`
- `blocked = red + blocked` ŌåÆ `amber` (failing gates fold into blocked; no fourth arc)
- `amber` ŌåÆ `slate`

Donut center: **`{green}/{gate_count}`** + caption **"passing"**. Beside it: `SectionLabel` "Release gates", `{green} of {gate_count} passing`, then three `LegendRow`s mapped 1:1 to segments ŌĆö `passing` (`bg-success`, count `green`), `blocked` (`bg-warning`, count `red+blocked`), `pending` (`bg-muted-foreground`, count `amber`).

## Vitals

`Section title="Vitals"`, grid `sm:grid-cols-2 xl:grid-cols-4`, gap 3. Each cell is a `VitalCell` = `Link` to a route with hover border. Cells render `CellSkeleton` (48px pulse) until their query resolves.

**Combined-empty collapse**: `testsAndEvalsEmpty = obs.data.runs_last_24h === 0 && !metrics.data.promptfoo.present` (both loaded). When true, the Tests cell and the Evals cell are replaced by a single dashed `EmptyTestsEvalsPrompt` spanning `sm:col-span-2`: label "Tests + Evals", "No test or eval runs yet", "See past runs and start the first one in Observability.", **Run a test** button ŌåÆ `/observability`. It carries the `overview-eval-passrate` and `overview-sbom-count` testids so queries still resolve; the standalone Evals cell is suppressed in this state.

### Tests ŌĆö `VitalCell to="/observability"`
Icon `FlaskConical`. Value = `obs.runs_last_24h`.
- `> 0`: `NumberFlow` value + hint `"all passing in 24h"` (when `errors_last_24h === 0`) or `"{n} error(s) in 24h"`.
- `=== 0`: muted `0` + hint "No test runs in 24h".

### Build ŌĆö `VitalCell to="/pipeline"`
Icon `Hammer`. Value = `ciLabel(metrics.ci.status)` where `'none'` maps to **"idle"** (a CI-less repo is not a failure); other values render verbatim (`passing`/`failing`/`running`). Hint: `metrics.ci.workflow ?? 'latest CI run'` when `ci.configured`, else **"No CI runs found"**.

### Cost today ŌĆö `VitalCell to="/observability"`
Icon `Coins`. Value = `metrics.cost_today_usd` via `NumberFlow` formatted as USD currency. Hint: "No AI spend tracked yet" when `0`, else "AI spend today".

### Evals ŌĆö `VitalCell to="/observability"` (suppressed when `testsAndEvalsEmpty`)
Icon `CheckCircle2`. `data-testid="overview-eval-passrate"`.
- `promptfoo.present`: `evalPassRate = round(passed / (passed+failed+errors) * 100)` rendered as `{n}%`; hint `"{passed} pass ┬Ę {failed} fail"` (+ `┬Ę {errors} err` when `errors > 0`) + `overview-sbom-count` span showing `┬Ę {components} SBOM` when `sbom.present`.
- not present: muted **"No runs"**, hint "No eval runs yet"; hidden `overview-sbom-count` span carries the component count (or 0).

### Deployment ŌĆö `DeploymentCell`, mounted only when `vercelConnected`
`Link to="/services"`, `data-testid="overview-deployment-cell"`, icon `CloudUpload`. Scoping: `getLastDeploy(path)` (per-project local store) ŌåÆ matched against `useVercelDeployments` data by `id` or `url`; if not found in the live list a synthetic `{state:'READY', ...stored}` is shown. A never-deployed project shows nothing here (the account's global latest deploy is deliberately NOT surfaced).
- Status `Badge`: variant from `deploymentStatusVariant(state)` ŌĆö `READY`ŌåÆsuccess; `BUILDING`/`QUEUED`/`INITIALIZING`ŌåÆwarning; `ERROR`/`CANCELED`ŌåÆdanger; else secondary. Label = raw uppercased state.
- URL: a button (stops nav propagation) ŌåÆ `bridge.openExternal('https://'+url)` with trailing `ExternalLink`; "No URL yet" when absent. "No deployments yet." when `deployment` undefined.

## Next

`Section title="Next"`. Renders `WhatsNextTile` with `metrics`, `release`, `obs`, `suppressTopTo={alerts[0]?.to}`, `hideHeader` (Operator owns the section header). `data-testid="ov-whats-next"`.

Suggestions from `suggestNext(state)` (rule engine `lib/ai/rules.ts`, pure/no-network). `toOverviewState` maps panel data: release counts (forcing Ōēź1 on blocked/red when `overall_status` matches), promptfoo last-run/failed/errors + `daysSince`, sbom present/components, and `obs.errors_last_24h ŌåÆ issues.openCritical` / `obs.runs ŌåÆ issues.openTotal`. Output: filtered (drops `all-clear` unless it is the only one), sorted by severity (`critical < warning < info`), the top row dropped when `sorted[0].to === suppressTopTo`, then sliced to 3.

Each row: severity dot (`critical`=`bg-danger`, `warning`=`bg-warning`, else muted) + `cleanLabel(label)` + trailing `ChevronRight` when the suggestion has a route; row is a `Link to={s.to}` when routed, else a static div. Loading ŌåÆ 3 pulse bars; empty ŌåÆ "Nothing to recommend right now." HARD RULE enforced in source: no chatbot widget, no textarea, no "Ask AI" here.

Relevant rules (`suggestNextRule`): `release-blocked` (critical ŌåÆ `/release`), `release-red` (critical ŌåÆ `/release`), `evals-stale` (>7d warning / >14d critical ŌåÆ `/observability`), `evals-never` (warning ŌåÆ `/observability`), `evals-failed` (warning ŌåÆ `/observability`), `issues-critical` (from obs errors, critical ŌåÆ `/issues`), `sbom-missing` (warning ŌåÆ `/release`), `all-clear` (info, no route).

## Recent activity

`Section title="Recent activity"`, action **"View all"** `Link to="/activity"` (the one accent affordance in the section). `Card` p-0. Source: `useRecentCommits(6)` ŌĆö real chronological git history.
- Loading: three pulse bars.
- Populated: `<ul>` of rows, each ŌĆö `GitCommit` icon, `humanizeCommitSubject(c.subject)` (neutral body text, hover underline; not an accent link), mono short hash, right-aligned `relativeTime`.
- Empty: "No commits found. This isn't a git checkout, or git isn't on PATH."

Note: the 90-day commit heat-strip is intentionally omitted ŌĆö the activity bridge caps history at 50 commits and exposes no per-day count series, so a real strip cannot be drawn (per source comment).

## Navigation map (Operator)

| Surface | Target |
|---|---|
| AlertStrip "gates failing/blocked" | `/release` |
| AlertStrip "errors in 24h" | `/observability` |
| AlertStrip "eval failures" | `/observability` |
| ShipCard button | `/release` |
| ReleaseGatesCard | `/release` |
| Tests / Cost / Evals vitals | `/observability` |
| Build vital | `/pipeline` |
| DeploymentCell card | `/services`; URL button ŌåÆ external browser |
| EmptyTestsEvalsPrompt button | `/observability` |
| WhatsNextTile rows | per-suggestion route |
| Recent activity "View all" | `/activity` |

No panel-local keyboard shortcuts; global g-prefix `g o` navigates here. No edge interactions beyond hover/click. Empty-state behavior is exhaustive above (combined Tests+Evals collapse, CI idle, no-deploy suppression, no-commits message).

## Guided variant (`OverviewNewbie`, `data-testid="overview-newbie"`)

Single centered column (`max-w-[760px]`). Same data hooks, different framing:
- Header **"How your project is doing"** + one `ProjectLede` line = standing lede + `describeShape(shape, total_loc)` (e.g. "A Node.js web app. Start with `npm start`."). Folder-not-picked fallback prompts to pick from the top bar.
- `NextStepCallout`: single top suggestion from `suggestNext` (ranked by severity), severity dot + label + hover arrow ŌåÆ suggestion route (default `/repo` when none). `release-blocked` rendered amber (awaiting input), not rose.
- `ReleaseStatusCard` (`newbie-release-status`, ŌåÆ `/release`): "Is it ready to ship?" + version badge; big number = `gate_count` when green / `amber` when pending / `blocked` otherwise; status `Badge` (greenŌåÆReady, amberŌåÆPending, redŌåÆFailing, blockedŌåÆBlocked[warning]); plain-English `liveBlurb`.
- `NumbersThatMatter` (`newbie`): three MetricCards ŌåÆ `/observability` ŌĆö **AI spend today** (`cost_today_usd`), **Progress** (`cycle_log.commits_landed`), **Tested** (`runs_last_24h`); each shows a zero-caption instead of a headline zero. When all three loaded and zero ŌåÆ single muted line "No automation activity in the last 24h." (`newbie-numbers-quiet`).
- `RecentActivityCard` (`newbie-recent-activity`): `useRecentCommits(3)`, "Lately" list; empty ŌåÆ "No commits in the last 24hŌĆ”".
- `Explore` row: Workboard (`/issues`), Read the repo (`/library`), Tutorials (`/tutorials`).
- Deliberately omits SBOM counts, gate-by-gate breakdown, LOC.

# Report

> [!INFO] **What this panel is**
> The **Report** panel (in Guided mode it is labelled **Project report**) reads the project you have open and writes up what it is, what it is built with, what it connects to, and how healthy it is. Almost all of it comes from *reading your files directly* - no internet, no AI required. Think of it as the page a careful new teammate would write after spending an afternoon walking your codebase.

Open it from the sidebar (**Project report**, under *Your project*) or jump there with the command palette (`Ctrl+K`).

---

## What you are looking at

When you open the Report, Console does a **deterministic scan** of your project. "Deterministic" just means *predictable*: the same files always produce the same answer. There is no guessing and no model involved - it walks your folders, opens the files it understands, counts things, and looks for tell-tale signs of how the project is set up.

> [!INFO] **Why "deterministic" matters**
> A lot of developer tools dress up a guess as a fact. Console keeps the two separate. Everything in the top of the Report is *measured* from your files, so you can trust it and re-run it. The only part that involves an AI model is the optional narrative at the end, and it is clearly labelled as such.

The subtitle under the title summarises the whole project in one line, for example:

```
TypeScript ┬Ę 48,210 LOC ┬Ę 3 services
```

That is your main language, your total **lines of code**, and how many outside **services** Console found wired in.

> [!INFO] **What "LOC" means**
> LOC = *lines of code*. It is the rough size of your project measured in lines. It is not a quality measure - more lines is not better - it is just a sense of scale. A weekend project might be a few thousand LOC; a mature app, hundreds of thousands. Console counts lines only in files written in a programming language (it skips images, lock files, and so on).

---

## The identity card

The first card is the project's **identity**: a health ring on the left, then the title, a one-line description (taken from your README or your `package.json`), the notable frameworks it found, your licence, your current git branch, how active the project is, and its size on disk.

> [!INFO] **What a licence is, and why Console shows it**
> A **licence** is the legal text that says what other people are allowed to do with your code - copy it, sell it, change it. If there is no licence file, the law's default is "nobody may reuse this", which is usually *not* what an open project wants. Console reads your `LICENSE` file and tries to name it (MIT, Apache-2.0, GPL-3.0, and so on). If it says "no LICENSE", that is worth fixing before you share the project.

> [!INFO] **What a git branch is**
> **Git** is the system that records the history of your code as a series of saved snapshots called *commits*. A **branch** is one line of that history you are currently working on - usually `main`. Console shows the branch so you know which version of the project it is describing.

The health ring shows a number out of 100 and a word: **Healthy** (80+), **Needs work** (50-79), or **At risk** (below 50). We will come back to how that number is built.

---

## Languages and size

In the Tech-stack area you will see a thin coloured **language bar**. Each colour is one programming language, sized by how much of your code is written in it. Hover any segment to see the exact percentage.

> [!INFO] **Why one project has several languages**
> Most real projects mix languages. A web app might be mostly TypeScript for the logic, with some CSS for styling and a little Python for a script. The bar shows that mix at a glance. The biggest segment is your **primary language**, the one Console names in the subtitle.

Console figures this out by opening every code file it recognises, counting its lines, and tallying them per language. It deliberately *ignores* configuration files (like `.json` and `.yaml`) and documentation (`.md`) when measuring "what this is written in", so a few large config files don't drown out your actual code.

The at-a-glance strip near the top gives you four plain numbers: **Files**, **Lines of code**, **Packages**, and **Commits**.

> [!INFO] **What a "package" is**
> A **package** is a self-contained unit of code with its own definition file (like a `package.json`). A small project is one package. A larger project might be split into several - a front-end package, a back-end package, a shared-utilities package. When a project holds many packages in one repository, that arrangement is called a **monorepo**, and Console will say so.

---

## How to run it, and where to start

Two cards answer the questions every newcomer asks first.

- **How to run** lists the commands that start, build, test, and deploy the project, pulled straight out of your `package.json` scripts (and any `Makefile`). If there is an obvious start command, Console highlights it.
- **Start here** is a short, numbered reading order: the entry point, the files the most other code depends on, the file that changes most often, and the largest file. It is Console's suggested path through an unfamiliar codebase.

> [!INFO] **Why "most depended-upon" is a good place to start**
> Console builds a **dependency graph** of your project (more on that next). If lots of files rely on one particular file, that file is probably important - changing it affects everything downstream. Reading the heavily-relied-upon files first is the fastest way to understand how a project hangs together.

---

## The dependency graph

A **dependency graph** is a map of *what relies on what*. When one part of your code imports or uses another part, that is a dependency - an arrow from the user to the thing being used.

> [!INFO] **Why this map is useful**
> Software is mostly about how the pieces connect, not the pieces themselves. The dependency graph turns those invisible connections into something you can see and reason about. Console builds it by reading your `import`/`require` statements and following the trail between packages. (You can explore the full visual version in the **Architecture** panel; the Report uses the same graph quietly, under the hood.)

The Report uses this graph in three places:

1. **Start here** - to find the most-depended-upon files.
2. **Risk hotspots** (Operator view) - files that are large, change often, *and* have lots of code depending on them. That combination is where bugs tend to hide.
3. **The health score** - if the graph contains a **cycle** (package A depends on B, and B depends back on A), that is a design smell, and it pulls the score down.

> [!INFO] **What a dependency "cycle" is and why it is bad**
> A cycle is a loop in the map: A needs B, B needs A. It means the two pieces can't be understood, built, or tested in isolation - they are tangled together. A few cycles are common; many of them make a codebase hard to change safely. Console counts them and warns you.

---

## Detected services, with evidence

A **service** is an outside product your project talks to - GitHub for code hosting, Vercel for deploys, Sentry for error tracking, Stripe for payments, a database, an AI provider, and so on.

Console finds these *without you connecting anything*, by looking for clues your project already contains:

- a matching dependency installed (for example `@sentry/node`),
- a known config file present (like `vercel.json`),
- a server named in your `.mcp.json`,
- or an environment-variable **name** that matches a known pattern (like `STRIPE_SECRET_KEY`).

> [!INFO] **What an environment variable is - and why Console only reads its name**
> Secrets like API keys are not written into your code (that would be unsafe). Instead they live in a `.env` file as **environment variables** - `NAME=value` pairs the app reads at runtime. Console reads only the **names** on the left of the `=`, never the values on the right. Seeing a variable called `STRIPE_SECRET_KEY` is enough to say "this project uses Stripe" without ever touching the secret itself.

Each detected service shows the **evidence** that found it, and a confidence badge:

- **in use** (high confidence) - a real dependency, config file, or MCP server was found. Strong proof.
- **maybe** (medium confidence) - only an environment-variable *name* matched. A name in `.env.example` doesn't prove the service is actually wired up, so Console hedges.

In Guided mode the services are shown as a calm single line ("GitHub, Vercel, Sentry."). You connect them for real over in the **Services** panel.

---

## The health score (0-100)

The number in the ring is a **health score**: a rough, honest read of how well-tended the project is. It is *not* a grade of your code's cleverness - it measures the engineering hygiene that makes a project safe to work on and ship.

> [!INFO] **Why a health score exists**
> Good practices are easy to skip when you are moving fast - tests, a licence, a lockfile, continuous integration. Each one you skip is a small future risk. The health score gathers those into one number so you can see, at a glance, what your project is missing. It is a *checklist with teeth*, not a vanity metric.

Console builds the score by starting at zero and **adding points for good practices it finds**, then **subtracting for real risks**. The good practices and their weights:

| Check | Points | What it means |
|---|---|---|
| README (with real content) | 16 | A file that explains the project to a human |
| Tests present | 16 | Automated checks that catch breakage |
| Licence file | 12 | Clear reuse terms (see above) |
| CI configured | 12 | See the callout below |
| Lockfile | 8 | Pins exact dependency versions so builds are repeatable |
| Is a git repository | 8 | The project's history is tracked |
| Test depth (decent ratio) | +8 | Tests are a healthy fraction of the code, not token |
| `.gitignore` | 6 | Keeps junk out of version control |
| Docs beyond the README | 6 | Extra documentation |
| Two or more main authors | +4 | The project isn't dependent on one person |

And the risks that **subtract**:

- **Dependency cycles** pull points off (the more loops, the bigger the hit).
- A **thin test ratio**, a **bus factor of 1**, or a **dormant/abandoned** project each raise a warning (and miss the points a healthy version would earn).

> [!INFO] **What "CI" is**
> **CI** stands for *continuous integration*. It is an automation that runs your tests (and other checks) automatically every time you push code, so a mistake is caught by a robot in minutes rather than by a user in production. Console looks for a CI setup like a `.github/workflows` folder. Having CI is one of the strongest signals that a project is maintained seriously.

> [!INFO] **What "bus factor" means**
> The **bus factor** is a slightly grim industry term: *how many people would have to be hit by a bus before the project is in trouble?* A bus factor of 1 means a single person wrote almost everything - if they leave, nobody else understands it. Console reads your git history to estimate this. It is not about your code's quality; it is about how spread-out the knowledge is.

The score is capped between 0 and 100. Below the ring, each contributing check is listed with a tick, an info dot, or a warning, and a one-line explanation - so the number is never a mystery. In Guided mode, Console shows **only the checks that need your attention**, to keep the page calm.

---

## Reading the project "as a book"

Console also lays out the project as a readable document: the at-a-glance numbers, a plain "Built with ..." sentence, the services as a line, and only the health issues that actually need fixing. The goal is one calm story you can read top to bottom, not a wall of dials.

If you ever want to read the underlying files themselves, the **Library** panel ("Read project files like a book") opens them one at a time, formatted nicely.

---

## The optional AI narrative

Everything above is measured. The Report can *also* ask a connected AI model to read the project and write a richer story - but only if you ask it to, and only if you have set up an AI provider.

In the **What this is about** card you will see one of two things:

- If no AI is connected: a note explaining that the summary above came from your files, and a **Read deeper with AI** button.
- If AI is connected: a one-line **tagline**, a **narrative** paragraph, a **What to fix first** list (each item with the file it points at), and a **read by `<model name>`** badge so you know exactly which model wrote it.

> [!INFO] **Why the AI part is separate and optional**
> An AI model can write a fluent summary, but it can also be confidently wrong. By keeping the measured facts and the AI narrative in separate parts of the page - and labelling which model produced the narrative - Console lets you treat each appropriately. The facts you can rely on; the narrative is a helpful read, clearly attributed. The AI is also handed your real file structure, so its "what to fix first" suggestions point at actual paths in your project.

> [!INFO] **"Bring your own key"**
> Console does not ship its own AI. You connect a provider you control (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, a local Ollama, and so on) in **Settings ŌåÆ AI**. Your key is held by Console's background process and never shown back to the screen. If nothing is connected, **Read deeper with AI** will tell you so - the measured Report still works completely without it.

Once you have an AI narrative, **Re-read with AI** regenerates it.

---

## Caching and re-studying

The first scan of a large project takes a few seconds. Console then **caches** the result (keyed to a signature of your file tree) so re-opening the Report is instant. If your files change, the signature changes and Console re-scans automatically.

> [!INFO] **What "caching" means**
> A **cache** is a saved copy of a slow-to-compute answer, kept so you don't have to recompute it. Console saves the Report into a hidden `.refringence-console/` folder in your project. Because it is keyed to your file tree, an out-of-date cache is detected and rebuilt on its own - you don't have to think about it.

The footer tells you exactly what just happened, e.g. *"Read 1,240 files in 820 ms, all from the project itself."* Two buttons let you force a fresh pass:

- **Re-study** - rebuild the whole measured Report from scratch (ignore the cache).
- **Re-read with AI** - regenerate just the AI narrative.

---

## Quick recap

- The Report **reads your files** and reports facts: languages, size, packages, run commands, services, and health.
- The **health score** rewards good engineering hygiene and penalises real risks - it is a checklist, not a vanity number.
- **Detected services** are found from real evidence, labelled **in use** or **maybe**.
- The **AI narrative** is optional, clearly separated, and attributed to the model that wrote it.
- Everything is cached and re-runnable; nothing leaves your machine unless *you* connect an AI provider.
