# Report

Route `/report`. Sidebar label **Report** (Operator) / **Project report** (Guided). Title `Project report`, icon `ScanSearch`, testid `panel-report`. Source: `views/intel/ProjectReport.tsx`, branching `mode = isNewbie ? 'guided' : 'operator'` inside `ReportBody`. Backed by the **Project Intelligence Engine** (`console-electron/src/main/intel/`): a deterministic Layer-1 profiler plus an optional Layer-2 AI enrichment.

## Data source

| Hook | Bridge call | Returns |
|---|---|---|
| `useProjectProfile(root)` | `bridge.intel.profile(root)` | `ProjectProfile \| null` |
| `useReprofile(root)` | `bridge.intel.profile(root, { force: true })` | rebuilt `ProjectProfile`, written into the query cache |
| `useEnrich(root)` | `bridge.intel.enrich(root, { model? })` | `{ ok, intel?: ProjectIntel, error? }`; on `ok` merges `intel` into `profile.ai` |

`useProjectProfile` keys `['intel','profile',root]`, `staleTime` 10 min, `retry: false`, `enabled` only when `root.length > 0`. `root = activeProject.path ?? ''`.

Subtitle (memoised on `profileQuery.data`): `` `${stack.primaryLanguage} ┬Ę ${metrics.totalLoc.toLocaleString()} LOC ┬Ę ${N} service(s)` ``; before data loads: `"A deep read of the project you have open"`.

### Render states
- **No project** (`!projectRoot`): `EmptyState` icon `FolderGit2`, "No project open".
- **Loading**: skeletons (one h-28, four h-24 tiles, one h-40) + "Studying the projectŌĆ”".
- **Null profile** (resolved but failed): `EmptyState` icon `ScanSearch`, "Could not study this project" + **Try again** (`profileQuery.refetch()`).
- **Loaded**: `ReportBody`.

## The deterministic scan (`intel/profiler.ts ŌåÆ buildProfile`)

One bounded tree walk (`scanProject`) + a set of derive-* passes. No AI, no network. Every sub-step degrades to an empty/honest value rather than throwing. Staged build emits 8 mount steps (used by onboarding's streamed mount, not the `/report` fast path): `scan ŌåÆ deps ŌåÆ meta ŌåÆ stack ŌåÆ services ŌåÆ graph ŌåÆ git ŌåÆ health`.

**Walk caps & skips:**
- `MAX_FILES = 40000`, `MAX_DEPTH = 24`; hitting either sets `metrics.truncated = true`.
- Per-file LOC/TODO read only when size `<= MAX_LOC_BYTES` (1.5 MB).
- `SKIP_DIRS`: `node_modules, .git, dist, build, .refringence-qa, .refringence-console, __pycache__, .pio, .cache, .venv, tools-src, vcpkg, src-qt, out, .next, .parcel-cache, target, vendor, .turbo, .svelte-kit, coverage, .nuxt, .output`.
- Dotfiles skipped except allow-list `.github, .gitignore, .cursor, .mcp.json, .npmrc` and any `.env*`.

## Languages / LOC (`metrics`, `stack.languages`)

`CODE_LANG` maps ~50 extensions to a language (TS/JS variants, Python, Rust, Go, Java/Kotlin, C/C++, C#, Ruby, PHP, Swift, Scala, Dart, Elixir, Shell, PowerShell, Lua, Julia, R, Vue, Svelte, HTML, CSS/SCSS/etc., SQL, Solidity, shaders). LOC = `body.split('\n').length` per matched file. Config exts (`.json/.yaml/.yml/.toml/.ini/.xml/.env`) counted as `configFiles` only; `.md/.mdx/.rst` counted as `docsCount`/`docsLoc`; neither contributes to the language histogram. Test vs source LOC split by `TEST_PATH_RE`.

`LanguageStat = { language, files, loc, bytes, share }`, `share = loc / totalLoc`, sorted desc; `primaryLanguage = languages[0] ?? 'Unknown'`. `metrics = { fileCount, totalBytes, totalLoc, sizeLabel, truncated, ratios }`. `sizeLabel` is human bytes (B/KB/MB/GB). `ratios = { sourceLoc, testLoc, docsLoc, configFiles, testToSource, docsToSource }` (ratios rounded to 2 dp).

**UI:** `LanguageBar` shows top 6 languages, segment width `max(1, share*100)%`, fixed muted palette `#60a5fa #38bdf8 #2dd4bf #fbbf24 #94a3b8 #64748b`, hover title = exact %. `metrics.truncated` is carried but the Report does not surface a banner for it.

## Stack detection (`deriveStack`, deps name-only)

Deps collected name-only from every `package.json` (monorepo-aware, `findPackageJsons` cap 60, depth Ōēż6), `requirements.txt`, `[project]`/deps in `pyproject.toml` (first 8 KB), `[dependencies]` in `Cargo.toml`. Versions resolved by `collectDepVersions` (declared ranges, overridden by `package-lock.json` installed versions).

- **frontend / backend**: anchored regex over lowercased dep names (Next.js, React, Vue/Nuxt, Svelte/SvelteKit, Angular, SolidJS, Astro, Remix, React Native, Tailwind; Express, Fastify, NestJS, Koa, Hono, Flask, Django, FastAPI, Rails, Gin, Axum/Actix).
- **runtimes**: manifest presence **or** Ōēź8% LOC share (Node.js, Deno, Bun, Python, Go, Rust) + Electron/Tauri from deps.
- **buildTools**: Vite, Webpack, Rollup, esbuild, Turborepo, Nx, Cargo, Make, electron-builder.
- **packageManager**: from lockfile precedence (pnpm > yarn > bun > npm > poetry > cargo > go modules).
- **notableFrameworks**: `FrameworkVersion[]` from a curated `NOTABLE_FRAMEWORKS` table joined to the resolved version map; UI chip shows `name + major`.

**UI (Operator only)** ŌĆö *Tech stack* card: `LanguageBar` + columns Frontend / Backend / Runtimes / Build tools / Package manager; second row (when present) Data layer (`detail.dataLayer.orm + engines`), API style (`detail.apiStyle`), Testing + quality (`detail.testing.frameworks/linters/formatters/typecheck`). Empty columns render "None detected". **Guided** collapses this to one "Built with" sentence (`frontend Ōł¬ backend Ōł¬ buildTools`, runtimes) + the bar.

`detail.dataLayer` (Prisma/Drizzle/TypeORM/Mongoose/Sequelize/Knex/Kysely/SQLAlchemy/Alembic/Diesel/sqlx; Postgres/MySQL/SQLite/Redis/MongoDB), `detail.testing`, `detail.apiStyle` (tRPC/GraphQL/gRPC/OpenAPI/WebSocket/REST) all derived from dep names (+ relFiles for OpenAPI).

## Dependency graph

`depGraph` is computed via the **shared arch-graph cache** ŌĆö `getGraph(root, { includeExternal: false, allLanguages: false }, false)` ŌĆö the same options as the Architecture panel's default, so the profiler warms the cache the panel reads. On failure it degrades to `{ nodes:[], edges:[], cycles:[], truncated:false, fileCount:0 }`.

`DependencyGraph = { nodes: DependencyNode[], edges: DependencyEdge[], cycles: string[][], truncated, fileCount }`. PackageŌåÆpackage edges from JS/TS imports (Python/Rust/Go/Java/Kotlin only when `allLanguages` ŌĆö off here). The graph is **not rendered** in the Report; it feeds three derived products:

- **`detail.readingOrder`** (`ReadingStep[]`, ŌåÆ *Start here*): entry point (shell/app package), top-3 most depended-upon nodes (in-degree), `hotFiles[0]` ("changes most often"), largest node by LOC; deduped, cap 8.
- **`detail.hotspots`** (`Hotspot[]`, ŌåÆ Operator *Build + risk*): non-external nodes scored `round(log2(loc+1) ┬Ę (1+churn/200) ┬Ę (1+inDeg) ┬Ę 10)/10`, top 6. Title attr shows `loc / churn / depended-on`.
- **`health.cycles`** signal: `graph.cycles.length` subtracts `min(10, cycles*3)` from the score and adds a `risk` signal.

`detail.commands` (`RunCommand[]`) parsed from every `package.json` `scripts` block (cap 80) + `Makefile` targets, grouped run/build/test/quality/data/deploy/other by name regex. `detail.workflows`, `detail.containers`, `detail.envGroups`, `detail.todoCount`, `detail.release` (latestTag/tagCount/hasChangelog from git + scan) round out the detail block.

## Detected services with evidence (`intel/serviceCatalog.ts`)

`detectServices({ envNames, deps, configFiles, mcpNames })` is a pure function over a curated `SERVICE_CATALOG` (~21 defs). It **never reads env values, only names**. Each `ServiceDef` declares `envKeyPatterns` (anchored, whole-name), `depPatterns` (substring), `configFiles`, `mcpNames`, plus `category / powers / pricing / docsUrl`.

Catalog (id ŌåÆ category ŌåÆ powers): githubŌåÆrepoŌåÆ*Repo + Workboard*; vercel/netlifyŌåÆhosting-frontendŌåÆ*Release*; railway/fly/renderŌåÆhosting-backendŌåÆ*Release*; supabase/upstashŌåÆdatabase; sentryŌåÆobservabilityŌåÆ*Observability (errors)*; langsmithŌåÆobservabilityŌåÆ*Evals*; posthog/plausibleŌåÆanalyticsŌåÆ*Overview counters*; stripeŌåÆpayment; slackŌåÆcommsŌåÆ*Workboard*; openai/anthropic/google-ai/openrouter/elevenlabsŌåÆai-modelŌåÆ*AI provider*; exaŌåÆsearch.

**Confidence rule:** any of `mcp / config / dep` evidence ŌćÆ `high` ("in use"); env-name-only ŌćÆ `medium` ("maybe"). `via` ordered strongest-first `mcp ŌåÆ config ŌåÆ dep ŌåÆ env`; `evidence` strings (e.g. `dep: @sentry/node`, `env: STRIPE_SECRET_KEY`) capped at 6. Sort: GitHub first ŌåÆ panel-powering ŌåÆ high-confidence ŌåÆ alphabetical.

**`DetectedService = { id, name, category, confidence, via, evidence[], powers, pricing, docsUrl }`.**

**UI:** Operator *Services wired* card ŌĆö count badge + `ServiceCard` per service: name, `in use`/`maybe` badge (success/warning), category label, `┬Ę powers <x>` when set, evidence joined `┬Ę`. Empty: "No external services detected. This looks like a services-light project." **Guided** *Services* card collapses to `services.map(name).join(', ')` or a connect-from-Services prompt. (Connecting for real lives in `/services`; detection here is read-only.)

## Health score 0-100 (`deriveHealth`)

Discriminator, not a presence checklist. Starts at 0, **adds** weights for practices, **subtracts** for risks, clamps `[0,100]`. Each check pushes a `HealthSignal { id, label, severity: good|info|warn|risk, detail }`.

| id | label | weight | bad severity |
|---|---|---|---|
| `readme` | README (`present && wordCount>60`) | 16 | warn |
| `license` | License | 12 | warn |
| `tests` | Tests (`inv.hasTests`) | 16 | **risk** |
| `ci` | CI | 12 | info |
| `gitignore` | .gitignore | 6 | warn |
| `lockfile` | Lockfile | 8 | warn |
| `docs` | Docs (beyond README) | 6 | info |
| `git` | Version control (`.git` present) | 8 | **risk** |

Conditional signals:
- `test-ratio` (only if `hasTests`): `+8` when `ratios.testToSource Ōēź 0.1` (good), else `warn` "coverage likely thin".
- `bus-factor`: `+4` when `busFactor Ōēź 2` (good); `warn` when `busFactor === 1 && commitCount > 20`.
- `cycles`: `-min(10, cycles*3)`, `risk`, when `graph.cycles.length > 0`.
- `activity`: `warn` when git activity is `dormant`/`abandoned` (no points either way).

`hasTests = testing.frameworks.length>0 || scan.testLoc>0 || cap.hasTests` (monorepo-aware, nested tests count). `hasEvals` from eval frameworks / promptfoo / capability. Activity bucket from last-commit recency: `<30d active, <90d slowing, <365d dormant, else abandoned`. Bus factor = authors needed to reach 50% of commits (`git shortlog -sn`).

**UI band (`healthTone`):** Ōēź80 `Healthy`/success, Ōēź50 `Needs work`/warning, else `At risk`/danger. `HealthRing` is an SVG donut (r=26, stroke = tone colour) with the score centred. Operator *Project health* card lists every signal (icon by severity: `good`ŌåÆCheckCircle2, `info`ŌåÆInfo, `warn`ŌåÆAlertTriangle, `risk`ŌåÆXCircle) with a header line `<CI provider|no CI> ┬Ę N docs ┬Ę README (M sections)|no README`. Guided *Health* card shows the `score/100 ┬Ę label` badge and **only** `severity !== 'good'` signals (or "Every basic check passes. Nice.").

## Optional AI narrative (`ProjectIntel`, Layer 2)

`profile.ai: ProjectIntel | null`. Triggered only by **Read deeper with AI** / **Re-read with AI** via `useEnrich` ŌåÆ `bridge.intel.enrich` ŌåÆ `enrichProfile(profile, model?, nowIso)`. On success `intel` is merged into `profile.ai` and persisted; returns `{ ok:false, error }` when no provider is connected (rendered as a connect prompt). Provider is bring-your-own-key (Settings ŌåÆ AI); keys live main-side, never returned to renderer.

`ProjectIntel = { narrative, tagline, systemDiagram, suggestions[{title,detail,priority}], packageNotes[{path,oneLiner}], changeFirst[{title,rationale,evidencePath}], runGuide, model, generatedAt }`.

**`AboutSection` ("What this is about"):**
- No `ai`: explanatory note + **Read deeper with AI** button (outline; pulse icon while pending). `enrichError` shown in warning text.
- With `ai`: `tagline` (bold), `narrative`, then **What to fix first** (`changeFirst`, each with `evidencePath` basename) ŌĆö falling back to **Suggestions** (`suggestions`, priority badge high/medium/low ŌåÆ danger/warning/info) when `changeFirst` empty; `read by <model>` badge in the header; **Re-read with AI** (ghost) button.

`ai` also feeds: `runGuide` ŌåÆ *How to run* card prose; `packageNotes` ŌåÆ per-package one-liners in *Packages* and *Start here*; `systemDiagram` ŌåÆ Architecture **Systems** view (not the Report). Enrichment is preserved across deterministic/version rebuilds (`getProfile`/`runMount` carry `cached.profile.ai` forward) so it is never silently wiped.

## Operator-only sections (beyond shared cards)

Shared by both personas: identity card, `AboutSection`, *How to run*, *Packages* (`PackagesCard`, kind badge app/lib/test/tooling/docs/unknown, AI one-liner or description or `role ┬Ę frameworks`), *Start here*. Operator adds:
- **Stat tiles** (4): Files (`fileCount`, hint `sizeLabel`), Lines of code (`totalLoc`, hint primary language), Packages (`shape.packageCount || packages.length || 1`, hint monorepo/single), Commits (`git.commitCount` or n/a, hint contributors).
- **Tech stack** card (above).
- **Services wired** + **AI tooling** (`aiTooling`: MCP servers from `.mcp.json`/`.cursor`/`.vscode`, AI SDKs, eval frameworks, agent configs `.claude`/`CLAUDE.md`/`AGENTS.md`/`.cursor`/copilot).
- **Project health** card.
- **Build + risk** (`InfraCard`, rendered only when any of hotspots/workflows/dockerfiles/todoCount present): Risk hotspots (top 3), CI workflows (file ┬Ę N jobs ┬Ę deploys), Containers (deduped base images), Markers (`todoCount` TODO/FIXME ┬Ę tags ┬Ę CHANGELOG).

## Footer / caching / re-study

Footer: `Read <fileCount> files in <durationMs> ms, all from the project itself.` + **Re-study** (outline, `useReprofile`, spinner while pending).

**Cache** (`ipc/intel.ts`): written to `<root>/.refringence-console/intel-profile.json` as `{ v: PROFILE_VERSION (=4), profile }`, keyed to `cacheSignature(root)` (mtime-based). `getProfile` returns cache only when `version === 4 && signature` matches and `force !== true`; otherwise rebuilds and carries `cached.profile.ai` forward. `PROFILE_VERSION` bump invalidates all caches (signature alone would miss a profiler code change). `resolveRoot` traversal-guards the path; every handler returns `null`/`{ok:false}` rather than throwing.

## Keyboard / navigation

No panel-local chord. Reached via sidebar, command palette (Panels group), or onboarding's **report** step (`ReportStep`, which renders this profile and offers "Looks good"). Re-running onboarding re-mounts the streamed profiler via `bridge.intel.mountStart`.

# Workboard

The Workboard is where the open work for your project lives. It pulls the issues from your GitHub repository and arranges them so you can see, at a glance, what is blocking a release and what can wait. You can also flip it to read issues that came in over Slack.

> [!INFO] What is an "issue"?
> In software, an *issue* is a single tracked piece of work or a problem: a bug to fix, a feature to build, a question to answer. GitHub gives every repository an issue tracker, and each issue gets a number (like `#42`), a title, a description, optional labels, and a comment thread. A *workboard* (or *issue board*) is just a visual way to look at all those issues at once instead of reading a flat list. Think of it as a wall of sticky notes, grouped by how urgent each one is.

You reach it from the sidebar (in Guided mode it is called **What to fix**) or with the keyboard chord `g` then `w`.

---

## Where the issues come from

Console does not store your issues. It reads them live from GitHub every time you open the panel, using a small command-line program called the **GitHub CLI** (the `gh` command).

> [!INFO] What is the GitHub CLI?
> `gh` is GitHub's official command-line tool. It talks to GitHub for you and, importantly, it reuses the login you already set up once with `gh auth login` ŌĆö so Console never has to ask you for a password or token of its own. When Console needs your issues, it runs `gh issue list` behind the scenes and reads back the result. This is why everything you see here matches github.com exactly: it *is* GitHub, just shown inside Console.

Console figures out *which* repository to read by looking at the project folder you opened. Every git project remembers a remote address (its "origin") that points at GitHub; Console reads that address and asks GitHub for the issues of that exact repo. So the Workboard always shows your project's issues, never someone else's.

> [!INFO] What is a "remote" / "origin"?
> When you clone a project from GitHub, your local copy keeps a bookmark back to the GitHub copy. That bookmark is called a *remote*, and the default one is named `origin`. It is just a URL like `github.com/you/your-project`. Console reads that URL to know which repo to load.

### If you see a warning banner

Two yellow/red banners can appear at the top:

- **"gh CLI not available"** ŌĆö the `gh` program is not installed on your computer. The banner links to **cli.github.com**; install it, then come back. Nothing is broken; Console simply has no way to reach GitHub yet.
- **"gh not authenticated"** ŌĆö `gh` is installed but you have not logged in. Open a terminal and run `gh auth login`, follow the prompts, and reload the panel.

> [!INFO] Why a separate login step?
> Console deliberately does *not* invent its own GitHub login. Reusing `gh`'s login means there is no extra password or access token for you to manage, store, or accidentally leak. One login, used everywhere.

---

## The two views: board and table

At the top right you will find a **GitHub / Slack** source toggle and, for GitHub, a **Kanban / Table** view toggle and a search box. Start with GitHub.

### Kanban board (the default)

The board lays your issues out in six columns, ordered by urgency:

**Critical ┬Ę High ┬Ę Medium ┬Ę Low ┬Ę Phase trackers ┬Ę Other**

> [!INFO] What is a Kanban board?
> *Kanban* (a Japanese word for "signboard") is a way of managing work where every task is a card and every card sits in a column that represents its status or priority. You move a card from one column to another as its situation changes. It makes the shape of your work visible: a tall "Critical" column instantly tells you that you have a problem. Console uses the columns for *severity* (how bad / how urgent), not for progress.

Each column shows a count and a coloured dot so you can read severity without reading words:

- **Critical** ŌĆö a solid red dot. Things that block shipping. Look here first.
- **High** ŌĆö a solid amber dot. Important, but not blocking a release today.
- **Medium** ŌĆö a hollow ring. Worth doing, no rush.
- **Low** ŌĆö a solid green dot. Minor.
- **Phase trackers** ŌĆö a small square. These are big "umbrella" issues that track a whole phase of work rather than one fix.
- **Other** ŌĆö anything Console could not sort into a severity.

The two low-traffic columns on the right (**Phase trackers** and **Other**) start *collapsed* into a narrow strip to keep the board calm. Hover over one, or start dragging a card, and it opens up.

> [!INFO] How does Console decide the severity?
> It reads the issue's *labels*. A label is just a tag you attach to an issue on GitHub ŌĆö like `bug`, `documentation`, or `severity:critical`. Console looks for labels named `severity:critical`, `severity:high`, `severity:medium`, and `severity:low` and drops the card into the matching column. Issues whose labels start with `phase:` go to **Phase trackers**. Anything with no severity label lands in **Other**. So the board is only as organised as your labels are ŌĆö which is the whole point of the next section.

### Table view

Click **Table** to see the same issues as a plain spreadsheet: number, title, labels, author, comment count, and when it was last updated. This is handy when you just want to scan a lot of issues quickly or sort them in your head by date. Clicking any row opens the issue, exactly like clicking a card.

---

## Ranking and re-prioritising: drag a card

Here is the part that actually changes things. On the board you can **drag a card from one column into another** to change its severity.

Say a bug you marked "Low" turns out to be blocking your release. Grab its card (use the small grip handle that appears on hover at the left of the card) and drop it into the **Critical** column. Empty columns even say *"Drop here to mark as Critical"* so you know where to aim.

> [!INFO] What just happened underneath?
> When you drop a card into a new severity column, Console does the same thing you would do by hand on github.com: it *removes* the old `severity:low` label and *adds* the new `severity:critical` label, using `gh issue edit`. The change is real and permanent ŌĆö refresh github.com and you will see it. This is called *relabelling*. The board is not a private scratchpad; it is a direct, two-way window onto your repo's labels.

> [!INFO] What does "optimistic" mean here?
> The card jumps to its new column *instantly*, before GitHub has actually confirmed the change. That snappy, no-waiting feel is called an *optimistic update* ŌĆö the app assumes the change will succeed and shows it immediately. If GitHub then rejects it (you are offline, you lack permission, the label does not exist), Console quietly slides the card back to where it was and shows a small error message at the bottom of the screen. So you never have to wonder whether it really worked: success stays put, failure undoes itself.

If you would rather not drag, every card has a **Ōŗ« menu** (top-right corner on hover) with **Move to ŌåÆ Critical / High / Medium / Low / Other**. Same effect, no dragging.

A couple of honest details:

- Moving a card *into or out of* **Phase trackers** or **Other** does not change any `severity:` label (those columns are not severities), so Console tells you "Moved, no severity label changed." The card stays where you put it for this session, but nothing is written to GitHub.
- You can also drag a card up or down *within* a column to reorder it. That reordering is just for your eyes during this session ŌĆö GitHub has no concept of "card order", so it is not saved.

> [!INFO] What does "drag-rank" really buy me?
> Two things. First, triage: pulling the genuinely urgent issues up into Critical/High makes the rest of Console honest ŌĆö the Overview and Release panels count "Critical" issues, so your labels drive the whole app's sense of "are we ready to ship?". Second, shared truth: because the change lands on GitHub, your teammates see the new priority too. You are not maintaining a second, private to-do list that drifts from reality.

---

## Opening an issue

Click anywhere on a card (or a table row) and a panel slides in from the right with the full issue:

- the number, who opened it, and when;
- the title;
- all of its labels;
- the full description, with formatting, code blocks, and links rendered properly;
- every comment, with author and timestamp;
- a **View on GitHub** button to jump to the issue in your browser.

> [!INFO] Why read it inside Console instead of on GitHub?
> Console fetches the issue with `gh issue view`, which means you can read the whole thread ŌĆö description and comments ŌĆö without leaving the app or losing your place on the board. When you *do* want to act on it (close it, comment, edit), **View on GitHub** takes you to the real page where GitHub's own tools live. Console reads and relabels; GitHub remains the place you write.

The description and comments are written in **Markdown**, and Console renders it for you.

> [!INFO] What is Markdown?
> Markdown is a simple way to format plain text: `**bold**`, `# headings`, `- bullet lists`, and so on. GitHub issues are written in it. Console turns that raw text into formatted, readable output ŌĆö the same way GitHub does ŌĆö so you do not have to read the raw symbols.

---

## Searching and filtering

In GitHub view, the search box at the top filters the board and table live as you type. It matches on three things at once:

- the issue **title**,
- the issue **number** (type `42` to find `#42`),
- and any **label** name.

The subtitle next to the panel title keeps a running count ŌĆö "12 of 37 open" ŌĆö so you always know how much you are looking at versus how much exists. The Workboard fetches up to 100 open issues at a time.

---

## Slack: issues that arrived as messages

Flip the source toggle from **GitHub** to **Slack** and the board re-reads from your Slack workspace instead.

> [!INFO] Why would issues come from Slack?
> In a lot of teams, the first report of a bug is not a tidy GitHub issue ŌĆö it is someone typing "the login page is broken again" into a Slack channel. Console can watch the channels you choose and surface those messages here as a second source of "work to look at", so nothing important gets lost in the chat scroll.

The Slack view groups messages by **team**: **Tech** (engineering reports ŌĆö bugs, blockers, regressions), **Non-tech** (product, design, ops), and **Test** (QA findings). Each row shows the channel it came from, the message title, a severity dot, the date, and an **Open** button that jumps straight to that message in Slack.

If Slack is not connected yet, you will see a calm empty state with an **Open Services** button. Head there, paste a Slack bot token, and tag each channel as Tech, Non-tech, or Test. After that, reports flow into this view.

---

## Guided vs Operator

You are reading the Guided layout, which simplifies the board into three plain buckets ŌĆö **Critical**, **High**, and **Other** ŌĆö with a one-line explanation under each and a big, calm row for every issue. There is no dragging here; the goal is to read and understand your work, not to micro-manage labels.

When you are ready for the full six-column board with drag-to-reprioritise, the per-card move menu, and the table view, switch to **Operator** mode (there is a **Switch to Operator view** link at the bottom of the board, or use the persona pill in the top bar).

---

## Quick reference

| You want toŌĆ” | Do this |
|---|---|
| Open the Workboard | Sidebar **What to fix**, or press `g` then `w` |
| Read an issue in full | Click the card or row |
| Change an issue's priority | Drag its card to another column (Operator mode) |
| Jump to GitHub | Open an issue, then **View on GitHub** |
| Find one issue | Type its title, number, or label in the search box |
| See Slack reports | Toggle the source from **GitHub** to **Slack** |

# Workboard

Route `/issues`. Sidebar label: **Workboard** (Operator) / **What to fix** (Guided). Nav chord `g w`. Source file: `views/issues/IssuesPanel.tsx`, with `WorkboardNewbie.tsx` (Guided) and `SlackBoard.tsx` (shared Slack source). Panel `data-testid="issues-panel"`.

A live read of the open project's GitHub issues, rendered as a six-column severity kanban (Operator) or three-bucket list (Guided), plus a Slack source. All GitHub data is fetched on demand through the `gh` CLI subprocess in the Electron main process ŌĆö Console persists no issue state of its own. Cross-column drag writes back to GitHub via `gh issue edit`.

## Persona branch

`IssuesPanel()` reads `usePersonaMode()`: `isNewbie` ŌåÆ `<WorkboardNewbie/>`, else `<IssuesPanelSeasoned/>`. Both share the Slack source (`SlackBoard`) and the right-side `IssueDetailSheet` (each persona has its own copy; identical IPC source, Guided's omits the labels row).

## Data sources

All via `lib/queries/issues.ts` over `bridge.issues.*` (`lib/bridge.ts`), backed by `console-electron/src/main/ipc/issues.ts`.

| Hook | Bridge / IPC | gh command | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| `useIssuesHealth()` | `issues.health()` ŌåÆ `console:issues.health` | `gh --version`, `gh auth status` | `staleTime` 5 min. Returns `{ghAvailable, ghVersion, repo, authStatus:'ok'\|'no-auth'\|'unknown', error?}`. `repo` is the literal string `(from the open project)` unless `REFRINGENCE_CONSOLE_REPO` is set. |
| `useIssuesList({state,limit,ŌĆ”})` | `issues.list(opts)` ŌåÆ `console:issues.list` | `gh issue list --repo <nwo> --state <s> --limit <n> --json number,title,state,url,createdAt,updatedAt,author,assignees,labels,milestone,comments [--label <l>]` | Panel calls with `{state:'open', limit:100}`. `staleTime` 60s. `enabled` only when a `projectRoot` or explicit `repo` exists. |
| `useIssueDetail(num)` | `issues.detail(num, projectRoot)` ŌåÆ `console:issues.detail` | `gh issue view <num> --repo <nwo> --json ŌĆ”,body` | `staleTime` 60s. `enabled` when `num !== null && num > 0`. Returns `IssueDetail` (`IssueRow` + `body` + `comments[]`) or `null`. |
| `useIssueRelabel()` | `issues.relabel({number,addLabels,removeLabels})` ŌåÆ `console:issues.relabel` | `gh issue edit <num> --repo <nwo> [--add-label L]ŌĆ” [--remove-label L]ŌĆ”` | Mutation. `onSuccess` invalidates `['issues','list']`. Returns `{ok, error?}`. No-op guard returns `{ok:true}` when both label arrays empty. |

**Repo derivation** (`deriveRepo`): runs `git -C <projectRoot> remote get-url origin`, parses owner/name via `/github\.com[/:]([^/]+)\/(.+?)(?:\.git)?$/i`. Resolution order for every call: explicit `opts.repo` ŌåÆ derived from origin ŌåÆ `REFRINGENCE_CONSOLE_REPO` env ŌåÆ if none, `list`/`detail` return `[]`/`null`, `relabel` returns `{ok:false, error:'No GitHub repo for this project (no origin remote).'}`. `projectRoot` is injected from `useActiveProject().project.path` by the query hooks.

**gh runner**: `spawn('gh', args, {shell:false})`, timeouts ŌĆö version/auth 5s, list/detail 30s, relabel 20s. Non-zero exit ŌåÆ `list`/`detail` log + return empty; `relabel` returns first 3 stderr/stdout lines joined by ` / `. `MAX_ISSUES_PER_FETCH = 100` (clamps `limit`).

## Header (`PanelHeader`)

- Icon `KanbanSquare`, title **Workboard**, `testid="workboard-panel-header"`.
- Subtitle (GitHub source): repo chip (`issues-repo`, `health.data.repo`) + `"{filtered.length} of {issues.data.length} open"`. Slack source: `"Pulled from Slack"`.
- Controls: `SourceToggle` (always). GitHub only: filter input + Kanban/Table `ViewToggle`.

### SourceToggle (`SlackBoard.tsx`)
`data-testid="workboard-source-toggle"`, buttons `workboard-source-github` / `workboard-source-slack`, `role="tablist"`, `aria-pressed`. State `WorkboardSource = 'github'|'slack'`, local to the panel, default `'github'`.

### Filter input (GitHub only)
`data-testid="issues-filter"`, `type="search"`, placeholder `"title, #num, label"`. `useMemo` over `issues.data`: case-insensitive substring match against `title`, `String(number)`, or any `label.name`. Empty filter ŌåÆ all rows. Drives the header count.

### ViewToggle (GitHub only)
`issues-view-kanban` / `issues-view-table`, `aria-pressed`. State `ViewMode = 'kanban'|'table'`, default `'kanban'`.

## Banners & states (GitHub source)

- `gh` unavailable (`health.isSuccess && !ghAvailable`): error banner (`issues-banner-error`) with install link to `https://cli.github.com` (via `bridge.openExternal`) + `gh auth login` hint.
- Authed-but-not-logged-in (`ghAvailable && authStatus !== 'ok'`): warn banner (`issues-banner-warn`), run `gh auth login`.
- `issues.isLoading` ŌåÆ `KanbanSkeleton` (six-column silhouette, fixed greyed counts critical2/high3/medium2/low2/phase3/other2, `issues-kanban-skeleton`).
- `issues.isError` ŌåÆ `Empty tone="error"`: `"Failed to fetch issues via gh: {error}"`.

## Severity model

`severity(issue)` reads lowercased label names:
- `severity:critical` ŌåÆ `critical`
- `severity:high` ŌåÆ `high`
- `severity:medium` ŌåÆ `medium`
- `severity:low` ŌåÆ `low`
- any label `startsWith('phase:')` ŌåÆ `phase`
- else ŌåÆ `other`

`severityLabel(s)` maps back to the `severity:*` string for `critical/high/medium/low`, and `null` for `phase`/`other` (so moves to/from those columns write no label).

`SEVERITY_PRIORITY = ['critical','high','medium','low','phase','other']`. Dots (`SEVERITY_DOT`): critical solid `bg-danger`, high solid `bg-warning`, medium hollow neutral ring, low solid `bg-success`, phase `rounded-[2px]` muted square, other solid muted dot.

## Kanban view (`KanbanView`)

`data-testid="issues-kanban"`. `@dnd-kit/core` + `@dnd-kit/sortable`, `collisionDetection={pointerWithin}`.

### Columns (`COLUMNS`, all six always rendered)
| id | label | collapsed `short` | lean |
|---|---|---|---|
| critical | Critical | ŌĆö | no |
| high | High | ŌĆö | no |
| medium | Medium | ŌĆö | no |
| low | Low | ŌĆö | no |
| phase | Phase trackers | Phase | yes |
| other | Other | ŌĆö | yes |

Widths: severity columns `w-[244px] shrink-0`; lean-expanded `w-[240px]`; lean-collapsed `w-[140px]`. Each `Column` is a `useDroppable({id:'col:<id>'})` with its own `SortableContext`. Headers: dot + label + count. Empty columns render dashed `emptyDropCopy(id)` ("Drop here to mark as Critical/High/Medium/Low", "Drop here for phase trackers", "Drop here for Other"). `isOver` highlights `border-ring bg-card/70`.

**Lean collapse**: `phase`/`other` collapse to the 140px compact column when `!hovered && !dragActive && !isOver`; remain valid droppables while collapsed (ref retained). Hover or any active drag expands them. Collapsed header uses `short ?? label`; tall spacer keeps drop zone full-height.

**Overflow**: horizontal scroll container, `snap-x snap-mandatory`. `ResizeObserver` + scroll listener set `hasOverflow` when `scrollWidth - clientWidth > 8` and not at end ŌåÆ right-edge mask gradient + `Drag right ŌåÆ` hint pill (`issues-kanban-scroll-hint`).

### Grouping & sync
`groups: Record<SeverityKey, number[]>` is local optimistic state (per-column ordered issue numbers), seeded by `initialGroups(issues)`. `useSyncGroups` re-seeds from upstream whenever the set of issue numbers changes shape (filter change / refetch) ŌĆö compares membership, not order, so optimistic reorders survive.

### Cards (`SortableIssueCard` / `CardBody`)
`useSortable({id: issue.number})`. `data-testid="issue-card-<num>"`. Structure:
- Drag handle (`GripVertical`), appears on hover/focus, `aria-label` documents arrow-key column movement (keyboard sensor + `sortableKeyboardCoordinates`).
- Full-card open button (`aria-label="Open issue #<num>"`) ŌåÆ `onOpenIssue(number)`.
- Body: `cleanIssueTitle(title)` (`line-clamp-2`, `text-body-strong`), then `#<num>` / author / comment count (`MessageCircle` when `commentCount>0`). `area:*` labels render as up-to-4 chips (prefix stripped, `title` = label description).
- `Ōŗ«` menu (`issue-card-menu-<num>`, Radix `DropdownMenu`): "Move to" ŌåÆ `MOVE_TARGETS` = Critical/High/Medium/Low/Other (items `issue-card-move-<num>-<id>`; current column disabled). Selecting calls `moveToColumn`.

`DragOverlay` renders `DragGhost` (`CardBody` in a `bg-popover` ring) with a 180ms spring drop animation; the source card goes `opacity-30`.

### Drag lifecycle
- `onDragStart` ŌåÆ `activeId = active.id`.
- `onDragOver` ŌåÆ if over a different column (drop-zone id `col:<id>`, or the column of the hovered card), optimistically move the number to the end of the target column.
- `onDragEnd`:
  - **Same column**: `arrayMove` reorder (session-only; never persisted to GitHub).
  - **Cross column**: `commitRelabel(num, targetCol)`.

### `commitRelabel` (shared by drag + menu)
Computes `original = severityLabel(severity(row))`, `target = severityLabel(targetCol)`. `addLabels = target ? [target] : []`; `removeLabels = original && original!==target ? [original] : []`. If both empty (move to/from phase/other) ŌåÆ no IPC, `toast('Moved #<num>, no severity label changed')`, visual move stays. Else `relabel.mutate`:
- success + `res.ok` ŌåÆ `toast.success('Relabeled #<num> to <label>')`.
- `res.ok === false` or `onError` ŌåÆ `revertMove(num, originalSeverity)` + `toast.error("Couldn't relabel #<num>: <error>")`.

`moveToColumn` (menu): optimistically removes the number from all columns, appends to target, then `commitRelabel`. `revertMove` re-buckets to the original severity.

> Note: same-column reorder and any phaseŌåöother move are **not** written to GitHub ŌĆö GitHub has no card ordering, and phase/other are not `severity:*` labels.

## Table view (`TableView`)

`data-testid="issues-table"`, `min-w-[720px]`. Sticky header. Columns: **#** (mono), **Title** (`cleanIssueTitle`, truncated `max-w-[420px]`), **Labels** (first 4 as chips), **Author** (`?? '-'`), **Comments** (right-aligned `commentCount`), **Updated** (`updatedAt` ŌåÆ `toLocaleDateString`). Rows `issue-row-<num>`, click ŌåÆ `onOpenIssue`. Operates on the same `filtered` array.

## Issue detail sheet (`IssueDetailSheet`)

Radix `Dialog`, right-anchored `max-w-[640px]`, `data-testid="issue-detail-sheet"`. Open when `openIssue !== null`. Fetches via `useIssueDetail`.
- Header: `#<num>` ┬Ę `opened by <author|unknown>` ┬Ę `createdAt` (locale date) ┬Ę title (`cleanIssueTitle`). Loading ŌåÆ "Loading issue..."; null ŌåÆ "Issue not found. It may have been closed or transferred."
- Body (Operator): labels row (all labels as chips, `title`=description), then `body` rendered via `marked.parse(...,{async:false})` into `prose-issue` (`issue-body`), else "No description." Comments section (`issue-comments`): count header + per-comment card (author, `Calendar` + `createdAt` locale string, markdown body).
- Footer: **View on GitHub** (`Button variant="outline"`) ŌåÆ `bridge.openExternal(detail.url)`.

Guided sheet is identical minus the top-level labels row.

## Guided board (`WorkboardNewbie`)

`data-testid="workboard-newbie"`. Header subtitle: `"{n} open right now"` / `"Loading..."` (GitHub) or `"Pulled from Slack"`. Same `SourceToggle`. No filter, no view toggle, no drag, no relabel.

Three buckets via `groupBySeverity`: **Critical** (`severity:critical`), **High** (`severity:high`), **Other** (everything else, sorted by `updatedAt` desc). Each `Bucket` (`Card`): dot + title + item count + one-line blurb; rows are full-width click targets (`workboard-newbie-row-<num>`: `#num` + `cleanIssueTitle` + hover chevron) opening the detail sheet. `BUCKET_ROW_CAP = 6` with a "Show N more" / "Show less" expander (`workboard-newbie-bucket-expander-<title>`). Loading ŌåÆ `BucketSkeleton` ├Ś3. Footer link **Switch to Operator view** (`workboard-newbie-switch-power`) ŌåÆ `setPersona('seasoned')`.

## Slack source (`SlackBoard`)

`data-testid="slack-board"`, `density` prop `'compact'` (Operator) / `'roomy'` (Guided, adds blurbs + wider gaps). Reads `useConnections()` (`connections.data.slack.connected`) and `useSlackIssues(connected)`.
- Not connected ŌåÆ `EmptyState` (`slack-board-empty`) "Connect Slack to pull issues" ŌåÆ **Open Services** (`slack-board-connect`, navigates `/services`).
- Connected ŌåÆ groups by `SlackTeam` via `TEAM_META`: **Tech** (`bg-danger`), **Non-tech** (`bg-warning`), **Test** (`bg-success`). Each `TeamSection` (`slack-team-<id>`): header dot + label + item count, blurb only when roomy. Rows (`slack-issue-<id>`): severity dot (`critical`ŌåÆdanger else warning, when present), channel (`MessageSquare` + name), title (truncated), date (from Slack `ts`, integer seconds ├Ś 1000), **Open** ŌåÆ `bridge.openExternal(permalink)`. Loading ŌåÆ `TeamSkeleton`.

## Keyboard / a11y

- `g w` opens the panel (`lib/useKeymap.ts`).
- Card drag handle: pointer (4px activation distance) or keyboard sensor (arrow keys move between columns; `aria-label` on the grip documents it).
- Detail sheet: Radix focus trap, Esc closes, `aria-label="Close"` on the X.
- "Skip to content" + per-route `PanelErrorBoundary` from the shell.

## Edge cases

- No `origin` remote / non-GitHub remote ŌåÆ empty board (list returns `[]`); relabel surfaces the "no origin remote" error.
- gh subprocess timeout (30s list / 20s relabel) ŌåÆ treated as failure (empty result / revert + toast).
- Optimistic moves: success persists, any failure reverts and toasts the first 3 lines of gh stderr.
- Moving to/from **Phase trackers** / **Other**: visual-only, no GitHub write, neutral toast.
- Same-column drag reorder: session-only, never persisted.
- Up to 100 open issues fetched per load (`--limit 100`, hard-clamped).
- `REFRINGENCE_CONSOLE_REPO` env var overrides repo derivation globally (escape hatch; no baked-in default repo).

# Architecture

The Architecture panel draws a map of your project: a box for each package, and lines between the boxes showing which package uses which. It reads this straight from your code ŌĆö nothing is connected, nothing is sent anywhere. Open it from the sidebar, or press `g a`.

> [!INFO] What is a "package" here?
> Console treats each **top-level folder** in your repo as a package ŌĆö for example `console-shell`, `docs`, `scripts`. A package is just a unit of code that lives together and usually does one job (the UI, the docs, the build scripts). For a few common container folders ŌĆö `packages/`, `apps/`, `services/`, `libs/`, `bundled-tools/` ŌĆö it goes one level deeper, so `packages/design-tokens` and `bundled-tools/my-tool` each get their own box instead of all collapsing into one.

## The two views: Code and Systems

At the top right there's a toggle: **Code** and **Systems**.

- **Code** is the real, measured map. Console reads your files and draws the packages and the import lines between them. No AI involved ŌĆö it's just what your code actually says.
- **Systems** is an AI-drawn map of the *idea* of your project ("the frontend", "the auth service", "the database layer") mapped onto real folders. It needs an AI provider connected and only appears once you generate it. The rest of this page is about the **Code** view, which is the heart of the panel.

> [!INFO] Why have both?
> The Code view answers "what literally imports what." The Systems view answers "what are the big moving parts, conceptually." A beginner usually wants the Code view first ŌĆö it can't be wrong, because it's measured, not guessed.

## What the graph is reading

When you open the Code view, Console walks every source file in your project and looks at the **import lines** at the top of each file ŌĆö the `import ... from '...'` and `require('...')` statements.

> [!INFO] What is an import?
> When one file needs code from another file, it says so at the top with an import. `import { Button } from '../ui/Button'` means "this file uses the `Button` from that other file." Imports are how a codebase wires itself together ŌĆö they're the single best evidence of what depends on what. A **dependency graph** is just a picture of all those "uses" arrows, rolled up from individual files to whole packages.

By default it reads **JavaScript and TypeScript** only (`.ts`, `.tsx`, `.js`, `.jsx`, and friends), because that's the clean, common case. If your project mixes languages, there's an **All languages** button (more on that below) that also reads Python, Rust, Go, Java, and Kotlin imports.

It skips the folders that aren't your code ŌĆö `node_modules`, `.git`, `dist`, `build`, `target`, `.venv`, and similar ŌĆö so the map shows *your* architecture, not your tooling.

Each box also shows two numbers: **LOC** and **files**.

> [!INFO] What is "LOC"?
> LOC means **lines of code** ŌĆö literally how many lines of source live in that package. It's a rough size signal. A package with 12,000 LOC is doing a lot more (and is riskier to change) than one with 200. Console counts lines while it reads each file, so the number is real, not estimated.

## Reading the boxes: tiers and the one blue colour

Each box sits in a **tier**, shown by a small coloured dot and listed in the **legend** in the bottom-left corner. The tiers are:

- **Shell** ŌĆö the outer process / entry point (an Electron main process, a server, a backend).
- **Presentation** ŌĆö the UI: screens, components, the things a user sees.
- **Domain** ŌĆö the middle of the app: logic that both uses other packages and is used by them.
- **Data** ŌĆö packages that get used but don't use much themselves (token sets, data layers, leaf libraries).
- **Infra** ŌĆö build scripts, config, tooling, design tokens.
- **Test** ŌĆö test suites, e2e, evals, fixtures.
- **External** ŌĆö a third-party dependency from outside your repo (only shown if you turn it on).

> [!INFO] How does Console decide a package's tier?
> Two ways, in order. First it looks at the **name** ŌĆö a folder called `scripts` is Infra, one called `tests` is Test, one with `ui`/`web`/`frontend` in the name is Presentation, one with `electron`/`server`/`backend` is Shell. If the name gives no hint, it uses the **shape of the connections**: a package that's used by others but uses nothing is treated as a leaf (**Data**); one that only points outward is a top layer (**Presentation**); one in the middle is **Domain**. This is a sensible guess, not a law ŌĆö and you can override it (see "Make it yours").
>
> Notice the colours stay restrained: one blue accent plus neutral greys and a couple of status colours. That's deliberate ŌĆö the legend should help you read structure, not turn into a rainbow.

The legend only lists tiers that are actually on screen, so you won't see an "External" row unless you've turned external dependencies on.

## The lines (and what a thick line means)

A line from box A to box B means **A imports from B** ŌĆö A depends on B. The lines flow generally **top-to-bottom**: things that depend on a lot sit higher, things that are depended-on sit lower.

Lines vary in thickness. A **thicker line means more imports** between those two packages ŌĆö they're tightly coupled. A thin line is a light, occasional dependency.

When nothing is selected, every line rests at a calm neutral grey so you can take in the whole shape. **Click a box** and Console lights up just that box and its direct neighbours in blue, fading everything else back ŌĆö so you can isolate "what touches this one thing." Click empty space (or the box again) to clear it.

> [!INFO] Why highlight neighbours instead of the whole chain?
> When you're trying to understand one package, the question is almost always "what does *this* talk to directly?" Showing one hop in each direction answers that without drowning you in the entire graph. It's the same instinct as clicking a person in an org chart to see their manager and reports.

## Cycles ŌĆö and why they're worth a warning

If Console finds a **dependency cycle**, a warning card appears in the bottom-right corner ("N cycles") listing the loop, like `a -> b -> c -> a`.

> [!INFO] What is a dependency cycle, and why is it bad?
> A cycle is when packages depend on each other in a loop: A needs B, B needs C, and C needs A again. The problem is there's no clean starting point ŌĆö you can't fully build, test, or reason about A without already having C, which needs A. Cycles make code harder to change, harder to test in isolation, and harder to split apart later. They're one of the classic "this will hurt you eventually" signals in software architecture, which is why Console calls them out specifically.
>
> Console finds them using a standard algorithm (Tarjan's strongly-connected-components) ŌĆö it's a well-known, reliable way to spot every loop in a graph, so if there's a cycle, it will be found.

A small number of cycles isn't an emergency, but each one is a thread worth pulling: it usually means two packages should be merged, or a shared piece should be pulled out into a third package they both depend on.

## When there are no lines

Sometimes you'll see boxes but no lines, with a small note explaining why. That's honest, not broken: it means your packages genuinely **don't import one another** (they might each stand alone, or they might talk through a published package name rather than a direct file path that the default scan follows). If that happens, the **All languages** and **External deps** toggles often reveal connections the default JS/TS-only scan didn't look for.

## The toolbar (top-right of the canvas)

These buttons let you widen or reshape the map:

- **`N` standalone / Hide standalone** ŌĆö by default the map hides packages that have no connections at all (so a repo of independent folders doesn't render as a wall of lonely boxes). This button reveals them when you want the complete picture.
- **All languages** ŌĆö also read Python, Rust, Go, Java, and Kotlin imports, not just JS/TS. Turn this on for mixed-language projects.
- **External deps** ŌĆö add boxes for third-party libraries your code imports (the stuff from `node_modules`, `pip`, etc.). Off by default to keep the focus on *your* code.
- **Recompute** ŌĆö re-read the project from scratch. Console caches the map for speed, so use this if you've edited code and want to be sure the map is fresh.
- **Reset layout** ŌĆö throw away any manual box-dragging and let Console auto-arrange again.
- **Edit layout** ŌĆö let you drag boxes around (see below).

> [!INFO] What does "Recompute" actually do?
> Reading every file takes a moment, so Console saves the result and reuses it next time you open the panel. It notices when files change and refreshes on its own ŌĆö but **Recompute** forces a fresh read right now, bypassing the saved copy. Think of it as "reload."

## Making it yours

In the **Edit layout** mode you can drag boxes to arrange the map the way *you* think about the project. Click any box (whether editing or not) to open a small side panel where you can:

- **Re-assign its tier** ŌĆö if Console's guess is wrong, set the right one by hand.
- **Add a note** ŌĆö a sentence on why this package matters, for the next person (or future you).
- **Hide it** ŌĆö drop noisy packages out of the map.

> [!INFO] Where does my arrangement go? Will a recompute wipe it?
> No. Your positions, tier overrides, notes, and hidden boxes are saved in a file inside your project (`.refringence-console/architecture.json`), completely separate from the auto-drawn map. When Console re-reads your code, it lays your saved choices back on top ŌĆö so your curation survives a recompute. You can even commit that file so your whole team sees the same map. (The auto-map itself is cached separately in `.refringence-console/arch-graph-cache.json`, which is just a speed cache and safe to delete.)

## The list view

Below the graph there's a collapsible **List view** ŌĆö the same packages as a plain, scrollable list with short plain-English descriptions of what each one does. Hover a row to highlight what it imports and what imports it. It's the same information as the graph, in reading order, for when you'd rather scan text than a diagram. Clicking a row takes you to the **Repo** panel for that package.

---

**In short:** the Architecture graph reads your real import statements, rolls them up into a tiered package map, draws the dependencies between them, flags any cycles, and lets you curate the result without losing it. Start in the Code view, click a box to focus it, and watch for the cycle warning in the corner.

# Architecture

Route `/arch` ┬Ę nav chord `g a` ┬Ę sidebar icon `Workflow`. Persona-branched at `ArchPanel`: `isNewbie` ŌåÆ `ArchitectureNewbie`, else `ArchSeasoned`. Source: `views/arch/{ArchPanel,ArchitectureGraph,SystemsView,ArchitectureNewbie}.tsx`. Extraction + persistence: `console-electron/src/main/ipc/architecture-graph.ts`.

Header view toggle (`arch-view-toggle`): **Code** (`Boxes`) / **Systems** (`Network`), local state `viewMode: 'code' | 'systems'`, default `code`. Subtitle swaps per mode. Body capped at `max-w-[1100px]`.

## Data sources (bridge / queries)

| Hook | Bridge call | Returns |
|---|---|---|
| `useArchitectureGraph(root, includeExternal, allLanguages)` | `bridge.arch.graph(root, {includeExternal, allLanguages})` | `DependencyGraph`; `queryKey ['arch','graph',root,includeExternal,allLanguages]`; `staleTime 5min`; `enabled: !!root` |
| `useRecomputeArchitecture(root, includeExternal, allLanguages)` | `bridge.arch.recompute(root, opts)` | mutation; `onSuccess` seeds the graph queryKey with the fresh result (no refetch) |
| `useArchOverlay(root)` | `bridge.arch.overlayRead(root)` | `ArchOverlay \| null`; `queryKey ['arch','overlay',root]`; `staleTime 5min` |
| `useSaveArchOverlay(root)` | `bridge.arch.overlayWrite(root, overlay)` | mutation; `onMutate` optimistically sets the overlay cache (no bounce) |
| `useRepoSummary()` | `bridge.repo.summary` | drives the List view + Newbie buckets (`total_packages/total_files/total_loc`, `packages[]`) |
| `useProjectProfile(root)` / `useEnrich(root)` | `bridge.intel.*` | Systems view (`profile.ai.systemDiagram`) |

`includeExternal` and `allLanguages` are passed as `mode === 'operator' && flag` ŌĆö i.e. **forced false in guided mode**, so the newbie graph is always internal-only JS/TS. Both flags are part of the query key and are cached separately main-side.

`DependencyGraph = { nodes: DependencyNode[]; edges: DependencyEdge[]; cycles: string[][]; truncated: boolean; fileCount: number }`. `DependencyNode = { id; label; tier: ArchTier; loc; fileCount; external? }`. `DependencyEdge = { source; target; weight }`. `ArchTier = 'shell'|'presentation'|'domain'|'data'|'infra'|'test'|'external'`. `ArchOverlay = { positions: Record<id,{x,y}>; tierOverrides: Record<id,string>; notes: Record<id,string>; hidden: string[] }`.

## Main-process extractor (`architecture-graph.ts`)

**Package mapping.** A file's package id = its first path segment under the repo root, except for `TWO_SEGMENT_CONTAINERS` (`bundled-tools`, `packages`, `apps`, `services`, `libs`, `app`, `plugins`, `modules`, `examples`) which keep two segments (`bundled-tools/my-tool`). `pkgForRelPath()`.

**Walk** (`walkRepo`). Iterative DFS. `SKIP_DIRS` = `node_modules, .git, dist, build, .refringence-qa, .refringence-console, __pycache__, .pio, .cache, .venv, tools-src, vcpkg, src-qt, out, .next, .parcel-cache, target`; also skips any dotfile/dotdir. Caps: `MAX_DEPTH=24`, `MAX_FILES=30000`, per-file `MAX_FILE_BYTES=1_000_000` (oversize files skipped). Hitting a cap sets `truncated=true`. LOC = `readFileSync(...).split('\n').length` per file, summed per package. Config files collected during the walk for resolution: `tsconfig.json`, `go.mod`, `Cargo.toml`, `package.json`, `requirements.txt`, `pyproject.toml`.

**Languages.** Default (`allLanguages` off) recognises **JS/TS only**: `.ts .tsx .js .jsx .mjs .cjs`. With `allLanguages` on, also `.py/.pyi`, `.rs`, `.go`, `.java/.kt/.kts`. Unknown extensions are skipped (never counted).

**Import extraction (regex, one pass/file).**
- **JS/TS**: `import ŌĆ” from 'ŌĆ”'`, `export ŌĆ” from 'ŌĆ”'`, `require('ŌĆ”')`, dynamic `import('ŌĆ”')`. Relative specifiers resolved against `dirname` probing `'' .ts .tsx .js .jsx .mjs .cjs` then `index.*`; bare specifiers resolved through **tsconfig `paths` aliases** (`baseUrl` + longest-prefix-first, wildcard substitution); anything still unresolved and non-relative ŌåÆ external (`bareModuleName`, scope-preserving).
- **Python**: `from a.b import c`, `import a.b.c` (comma-split). Dotted module probed as `a/b/c.py|.pyi` and `a/b/c/__init__.py|.pyi`; leading-dot relative resolved against the importer's dir.
- **Rust**: `use crate::ŌĆ”` and `mod foo;` stay intra-crate (no edge). Cross-crate `other::x` maps to `other/src/lib.rs|main.rs` if walked; `cargoCrateDir` guards against same-crate false positives. `self/super/std/core` dropped.
- **Go**: grouped + single `import` specs; intra-module when prefixed by a `go.mod` `module` path (stripped to subdir, matched by `dirname`); third-party (dotted host) ŌåÆ external via `goExternalName`; stdlib dropped.
- **JVM**: `import a.b.C;` resolved to the repo package whose file declared `package a.b` (longest-prefix match over `jvmPackageToPkg`).

**Edges.** `buildEdges` accumulates `"source target" ŌåÆ weight` from imports (self-edges dropped). `addManifestEdges` recovers workspace edges the import scan misses: reads each internal `package.json` `dependencies/devDependencies/peerDependencies`, resolving `file:`/`link:`/`workspace:` specs to a sibling dir and otherwise matching a declared dep **name** to an internal package's `name`; same for `pyproject.toml`/`Cargo.toml` declared deps (PEP-621/poetry/cargo tables). This is what gives an npm-`--prefix`/workspaces graph any edges at all. `seedManifestPackages` adds zero-source nodes for manifest-only packages (e.g. an empty `design-tokens`).

**Tiers** (`tierFor`). Name signal first (`tierByName`, checked in order): `RE_TEST` (qa/tests/e2e/specs/fixtures/__tests__/evals/benchmarks) ŌåÆ `test`; `RE_INFRA` (scripts/infra/config/setup/install/build/tooling/tokens/design-tokens/ci/devops) ŌåÆ `infra`; `RE_SHELL` (electron/main/server/host/daemon/backend/gateway/api) ŌåÆ `shell`; `RE_PRESENTATION` (shell/renderer/chrome/web/ui/frontend/client/app/site/view/pages/components/docs) ŌåÆ `presentation`. No name hit ŌåÆ **topology**: `inbound 0 & outbound>0` ŌåÆ presentation; `inbound>0 & outbound>0` ŌåÆ domain; `inbound>0 & outbound 0` ŌåÆ data; else domain. External nodes (only with `includeExternal`) are tier `external`, `external: true`, `loc` = import-site count, synthesised from imported specs **plus** declared-but-unimported manifest deps (`collectDeclaredExternals`).

**Cycles.** Iterative **Tarjan SCC** over internal (non-external) nodes only; SCCs of size > 1 are returned as `cycles: string[][]`. Self-edges never emitted, so practically: multi-node SCCs.

**Caching.** Graph cached at `<root>/.refringence-console/arch-graph-cache.json`, keyed on `{version: GRAPH_VERSION(=3), signature, includeExternal, allLanguages}`. `cacheSignature` is **directory-only** (roll-up of dir `mtimeMs` + dir count + entry count; no per-file stat) ŌĆö a content edit bumps the containing dir mtime, an add/delete changes entry count. Cache hit requires version + signature + both flags to match. `recompute` forces `force=true`, bypassing the read. `bumping GRAPH_VERSION` invalidates older caches on unchanged trees.

**Hardening.** `resolveProjectRoot` resolves + `statSync.isDirectory()` guards; `isInside` blocks path traversal on the overlay write target. Every handler is total ŌĆö returns `EMPTY_GRAPH`/`null` on any error rather than throwing into the renderer. Overlay write returns `{ok:false,error}` on failure.

## Code view ŌĆö `ArchSeasoned`

Header `Dependency graph` label + `total_packages ┬Ę total_files ┬Ę total_loc` (from `useRepoSummary`). States: `noProject` ŌåÆ `NoProjectEmptyState` (`arch-pick-folder` ŌåÆ `bridge.project.pickFolder`); `isLoading` ŌåÆ `LoadingSkeleton` (`arch-loading`); `isError` ŌåÆ `arch-error-state` card with **Retry** (`repo.refetch`).

### ArchitectureGraph (`mode="operator"`)

`@xyflow/react` `ReactFlow` on a `h-[640px]` canvas (`architecture-graph`). Local state: `includeExternal`, `allLanguages`, `selectedId`, `editLayout`, `showStandalone`, `elkPositions`, `flowNodes`.

**Node visibility.** `hidden` = overlay `hidden` set. `connectedIds` = every node in any edge. Default `visibleNodes` = non-hidden Ōł® connected; `showStandalone` (or zero connected) ŌåÆ all non-hidden. `standaloneCount` = non-hidden, non-connected count (drives the toggle label). `visibleEdges` = edges with neither endpoint hidden.

**Layout (ELK).** `layoutWithElk` runs `elkjs` `elk.layered`, `direction DOWN`, `nodeNodeBetweenLayers 64`, `spacing.nodeNode 40`, fixed node box `NODE_W=240 ├Ś NODE_H=58`. Re-runs on `[graph, visibleNodes.length, hidden]`. ELK failure ŌåÆ 4-column grid fallback. **Overlay `positions[id]` win over ELK** per node, so a curated layout sticks. `FitOnInit` calls `fitView({padding:0.2})` only once `useNodesInitialized()` is true (and re-fits when visible node count changes) ŌĆö fixes the right-edge clipping from fitting before measurement.

**Node card** (`ArchNodeCard`): tier dot + tint + border from `TIER_STYLE` (one blue accent `accent-solid` for shell, `info` presentation, `foreground` domain, `warning` data, `muted` infra, `success` test, dashed `border` external), `font-mono` label, `loc ┬Ę files`. `target` handle Top, `source` handle Bottom. Dimmed to `opacity-25` when a selection excludes it; `ring-2 ring-accent-solid` when selected.

**Edges** (`flowEdges`): `strokeWidth = min(1.2 + weight*0.5, 4)`. No selection ŌåÆ all neutral `var(--muted-foreground)` at `opacity 0.6`. Selection (`neighbourIds`, the selected node ┬▒ one hop both directions) ŌåÆ in-subgraph edges `var(--accent-solid)` `opacity 0.95`, others `opacity 0.1`.

**Interaction.** Click node toggles `selectedId` (re-click clears); pane click clears. Pan/zoom/scroll/pinch/dblclick enabled (operator is not fixed viewport). `Controls` shown (no interactive lock toggle). `Background gap=20`. `proOptions.hideAttribution`.

**Operator toolbar** (top-right, gated `mode==='operator'`):
- `arch-standalone-toggle` ŌĆö only when `standaloneCount>0`; label `N standalone` / `Hide standalone`.
- `arch-all-languages-toggle` (`Languages` icon) ŌĆö toggles `allLanguages` (re-keys the query; also extracts Py/Rust/Go/JVM).
- `arch-external-toggle` (`Package` icon) ŌĆö toggles `includeExternal`.
- `arch-recompute` (`RefreshCw`, spins while `recompute.isPending`) ŌĆö `recompute.mutate()`.

**Banners / cards.**
- `Legend` (`arch-legend`, bottom-left) ŌĆö only `presentTiers` (tiers actually on screen), ordered `TIER_ORDER`.
- `CycleWarning` (`arch-cycle-warning`, bottom-right) ŌĆö when `graph.cycles.length>0`; warning badge `N cycles`, first 4 cycles as `a -> b -> c` (`shortName`).
- `arch-truncated-banner` (top-left) ŌĆö when `graph.truncated`; "Showing X packages; tree truncated at `fileCount` files."
- `arch-no-edges-hint` (top-left) ŌĆö when not truncated, `connectedIds.size===0`, `visibleNodes>0`; explains the missing lines and (operator) points at All languages / External deps.
- `architecture-graph-empty` ŌĆö when `graph.nodes.length===0`; "Showing the list view instead."
- `arch-graph-loading` ŌĆö `GraphSkeleton` while `graphQuery.isLoading`.

### Footer note + List view

Note under the graph: edges extracted live; Edit layout to drag; per-node tier/notes/hide save to `.refringence-console/architecture.json`.

**List view** (`arch-list-toggle` chevron, collapsed by default). Packages from `repo.data.packages` sorted by `total_loc` desc. Source of descriptions is the **hand-authored `PACKAGE_META`** in `ArchPanel.tsx` (NOT the live graph) ŌĆö `role` string + curated `importsFrom`; `importedBy` derived at runtime in `buildEdgeMap` (only edges where both ends exist in the repo's package set are kept). Each `PackageRow`: `name` (mono) + `LOC ┬Ę files`, role line, **Imports** / **Imported by** edge chips (`shortName` strips `bundled-tools/`, `packages/`). Hover highlights the row + its import/imported-by neighbours (others dimmed `opacity-40`, active gets a 2px `accent-solid` left bar). Whole row is a `<Link to="/repo">`.

## Systems view ŌĆö `SystemsView` (`mode === 'systems'`)

AI-generated **semantic** map (distinct from the measured Code graph). Reads `useProjectProfile(root).data.ai.systemDiagram` (`SystemDiagram = { nodes: SystemNode[]; edges }`; `SystemNode = { id, label, kind, paths[], summary? }`). No diagram ŌåÆ `EmptyState` with **Generate with AI** (`useEnrich(root).mutate()`, "Reading the projectŌĆ”" pending); surfaces `enrich` error text.

Rendered with ReactFlow + ELK (`elk.layered DOWN`, `nodeNodeBetweenLayers 72`, `nodeNode 44`, box `230├Ś64`). Nodes **not draggable** (no overlay). `KIND_STYLE` maps `frontend/backend/data/service/infra/shared/docs/tests` to the same one-accent palette (unknown kind ŌåÆ `shared`). Card shows `kind ┬Ę N paths`. Click toggles selection; same neighbour-highlight edge model as Code view; edges may carry a `label`. Bottom-left card: `N systems ┬Ę M links ┬Ę AI-generated`. Selection sidebar (`systems-sidebar`): label, `kind` badge, `summary`, and a **Maps to (N)** list of real repo paths ŌĆö each a button calling `bridge.openPath(`${root}/${rel}`)` to open real code (paths validated main-side).

## Guided variants

**`ArchitectureNewbie`** (`arch-newbie`): single column, `max-w-[820px]`. Embeds `ArchitectureGraph mode="guided"` (`arch-newbie-graph`) ŌĆö **fixed viewport** (no pan/zoom, no `Controls`, no toolbar, no operator side panel), `includeExternal`/`allLanguages` forced off, nodes never draggable. Clicking a node opens `GuidedSidebar` (`arch-guided-sidebar`): prose ("`label` sits in the `Tier` tier; N files, M LOC"), optional overlay note, and **It depends on** / **It is used by** chip lists derived from `graph.edges`. Below the graph: packages bucketed by `ROLE_RULES` into **Shell / UI / Data / Tools / Workspace** (`classify`, plus static `docs`/`scripts` workspace entries) with one-sentence explainers; no LOC, no arrows. Footer card "Want the full map?" ŌåÆ `setPersona('seasoned')`.

`ArchitectureGraph` `mode==='guided'` differences vs operator: `fixedViewport=true` (pan/zoom/scroll/pinch/dblclick disabled, `Controls` hidden), toolbar/operator panel suppressed, drag persistence inert.

## Keyboard / a11y

- `g a` ŌåÆ `/arch` (global chord, `useKeymap`).
- View toggle buttons `arch-view-{code,systems}`; graph container `architecture-graph` / `systems-view`.
- Route wrapped in `PanelErrorBoundary`. Node handles + dots are `aria-hidden`; close buttons are `aria-label`ed.

## Persisted files (in the open project)

- `.refringence-console/arch-graph-cache.json` ŌĆö auto-graph speed cache (dir-mtime signature + flags + `GRAPH_VERSION`). Disposable.
- `.refringence-console/architecture.json` ŌĆö user overlay: `positions`, `tierOverrides`, `notes`, `hidden`. Read/written independently of the cache so curation survives `Recompute`. (Both live under the gitignored `.refringence-console/` dir by default.)

# Pipeline and Release

This page covers the two panels that answer one question: **is your project safe to put in front of real users yet, and what happens when you push code?**

- **Pipeline** shows the automated checks that run every time you push code (this is called **CI**).
- **Release** shows a checklist of safety gates that decide whether a build is **ready to ship**, plus a one-click way to **deploy to Vercel**.

You reach them from the sidebar: **Ship it ŌåÆ Connect & deploy / Release / Monitor**, with **Pipeline** living under the **More** disclosure. The keyboard shortcuts are `g p` (Pipeline) and `g l` (Release).

---

## Pipeline

> [!INFO] **What is CI?**
> CI stands for *Continuous Integration*. The idea: every time you push code to GitHub, a robot automatically runs a set of checks on it ŌĆö building the project, running the tests, checking the formatting ŌĆö *before* the code is considered "good." Instead of you remembering to run things by hand, the robot does it on every change and tells you red or green. Console's Pipeline panel reads those robot instructions and draws them for you.

### Where the data comes from

Console does not invent the steps. It reads the **workflow files** in your repo at `.github/workflows/`.

> [!INFO] **What is a workflow file?**
> A workflow file is a small text file (ending in `.yml` or `.yaml`) that lives in the hidden `.github/workflows/` folder of your project. It tells GitHub: "when *this* happens (a push, a pull request, a schedule), run *these* jobs." One file = one workflow. A repo can have several ŌĆö one for tests, one for linting, one for deploying, and so on.

If you have not picked a project yet, the panel asks you to **Pick a folder**. If your project has no `.github/workflows/` folder, you will see *"No workflows found"* ŌĆö that simply means nobody has set up CI for this repo yet, and that is completely normal for a young project.

### What each workflow card tells you

In Guided mode, Pipeline shows **one card per workflow** (up to six), written in plain English. Each card has three parts:

1. **A title** ŌĆö the workflow's name, cleaned up to read nicely (e.g. `ci.yml` becomes "CI").
2. **A plain-English sentence** describing *when* it runs and *what* it does. For example: *"On every push and on pull requests, it builds and tests the repo across Lint and Test."* Console writes this sentence from the workflow's real triggers and real job names, so two different workflows never read the same.
3. **A status pill** (only when there has been a run): **Passing**, **Failing**, or **Running**.

> [!INFO] **Reading the trigger phrase**
> The "when" half of the sentence is the most useful part to learn from. It is built from the workflow's real triggers:
> - *on every push* ŌĆö runs whenever anyone pushes code.
> - *on pull requests* ŌĆö runs when someone proposes a change for review (a "pull request").
> - *on a schedule* ŌĆö runs on a timer (e.g. nightly), even if nobody pushed.
> - *when a release is published* ŌĆö runs only when you cut a release.
> - *when started by hand* ŌĆö someone can press a button to run it (this is the trigger Console uses too ŌĆö see below).
> - *when another workflow calls it* ŌĆö it is a reusable piece used by other workflows.

> [!INFO] **What does "the status pill" actually mean?**
> The pill reflects the **most recent run** of that workflow on GitHub:
> - **Passing** (green) ŌĆö the last run finished and everything succeeded.
> - **Failing** (red) ŌĆö the last run finished but something broke. Open the workflow on GitHub to read the logs and see which step failed.
> - **Running** (amber) ŌĆö a run is happening right now.
>
> If there is **no pill at all**, it means Console could not confidently classify the latest run (or there hasn't been one). Console deliberately shows *nothing* rather than a fake grey "idle" dot ŌĆö a blank is honest; a made-up status is not.

The status comes from GitHub itself, read through the **GitHub CLI** (`gh`). Console refreshes it about once a minute, so the pills stay close to live without you reloading.

> [!INFO] **The "as of" time**
> Because the status is fetched and then cached for a short while, the Operator view stamps it with an *"as of 14:32"* time so you always know how fresh the reading is. If the GitHub CLI is not installed or you are not signed in, Console will tell you *"Live run status unavailable"* and just show the workflow shapes without live pills.

### Seeing more detail

Each card has a **See details** link that flips you into the **Operator** Pipeline view. There, the same workflows are drawn as a left-to-right **stage graph**:

- A **Source** node on the left (this represents your `git push`).
- One node per **job**, arranged in the order they actually run.
- An optional **Deploy** node on the right ŌĆö it only appears if Console finds a `vercel.json` or `netlify.toml`, which signals the project deploys to Vercel or Netlify.

> [!INFO] **What is a "job" and what does the arrangement mean?**
> A workflow is made of **jobs** ŌĆö independent units of work (e.g. "install dependencies", "run tests", "build"). Some jobs depend on others: the "test" job might wait for the "build" job to finish first. The graph reads those dependencies and places jobs in columns so that anything to the *right* runs *after* the thing it points back to. Reading the graph left-to-right is reading the order your checks happen in.

### Running a workflow yourself

In the Operator view, every workflow has a **Trigger** button. Pressing it asks GitHub to start that workflow right now (the same as the *"when started by hand"* trigger).

- This only works if you have the **GitHub CLI** installed and your repo actually has CI workflows; otherwise the button is greyed out with a tooltip telling you why.
- Console fires the request once and shows you a toast like *"CI dispatched."* It will not let you double-fire the same workflow while a trigger is already in flight.
- A moment later, the workflow's status pill should flip to **Running** as GitHub picks up the job.

> [!INFO] **Why trigger by hand?**
> Most of the time CI runs on its own when you push. But sometimes you want to re-run checks without making a new commit ŌĆö for example, a test failed for a flaky reason and you just want to try again, or you fixed something on GitHub's side. The Trigger button is that "run it again now" button.

---

## Release

> [!INFO] **What is a "release" and why does it need gates?**
> A **release** is a specific, named version of your project that you've decided is good enough to give to users (e.g. `v0.1`). Before you hand something to real people, you usually want a short checklist: are the tests passing? Is there a security scan? Did someone review it? Each item on that checklist is called a **gate** ŌĆö because it *gates* (blocks) the release until it's satisfied. Console's Release panel is that checklist, made visible.

### Where the data comes from

Release reads a checklist file from your repo at `docs/release-checklists/<version>.yaml`. Each version (like `v0.1`) is one file, and each file lists its gates. If you have never created one, you'll see *"No release pipeline yet"* with a note telling you where to add it.

> [!INFO] **You write the gates; Console reads them**
> Console does not decide what "ready" means for your project ŌĆö you do, by listing gates in that YAML file. Console's job is to read the file, total up the statuses, and present it calmly so you always know where you stand.

### The readiness ring and the ship verdict

At the top, Release shows a **donut ring** and a **plain-English verdict**.

> [!INFO] **What is the readiness ring (the donut)?**
> The ring is a *part-of-whole* picture. The whole circle is "all your gates." The colored slices show how many are **passing** (green), **blocked/failing** (red), and **pending** (amber). The big number in the middle is how many are passing out of the total ŌĆö for example **5 / 7**. One glance tells you how close you are without reading every row.

The verdict next to it is one honest sentence:

- **"Ready to ship"** ŌĆö every gate is passing. Nothing is in your way.
- **"Blocked from ship"** ŌĆö one or more gates are failing or blocked. These must be resolved first.
- **"Awaiting checks"** ŌĆö nothing is failing, but some gates still need a human to review and sign off.

Below that is a quiet count line: how many are **green**, **amber**, **red**, **blocked**, and the total.

### Reading the gates

Each gate row shows:

- A **status icon and badge** ŌĆö **Passing**, **Pending** (needs review), **Failing** (blocked), or **Waiting**.
- The **gate's name** in plain words.
- The **file** it points at (its "artifact" ŌĆö the evidence behind the check, like a test report).
- A **What this means** sentence explaining the gate in beginner terms, plus a blocker note if something is wrong.
- A **Show file** link (and in Operator, a **View** button) that opens that evidence file.

> [!INFO] **The four gate states, in plain words**
> - **Passing (green)** ŌĆö this check is satisfied. Nothing for you to do.
> - **Pending / Needs review (amber)** ŌĆö the work is done, but a person still has to confirm and sign off before the release can go out.
> - **Failing (red)** ŌĆö something is actually broken. Fix the underlying problem before shipping.
> - **Blocked / Waiting (grey)** ŌĆö this is waiting on something outside your build (e.g. an external approval). Check back later.

Guided mode focuses you on the **top few gates that need attention** so you're not staring at a wall of green checks ŌĆö it surfaces the things between you and shipping.

> [!INFO] **Why is "Cut release" greyed out?**
> Console can *tell* you a build is ready, but actually tagging and publishing the release ("Cut release") and "Run all checks" are marked **Planned** ŌĆö not wired up yet. For now, Console is your dashboard and second opinion; you cut the tag yourself with your normal tools. The panel is honest about this rather than showing a button that does nothing.

### Environment variables

Release also lists the **variable names** found in your local `.env` files (only the names, never the secret values).

> [!INFO] **What is an environment variable?**
> An environment variable is a setting your app reads at runtime ŌĆö things like `DATABASE_URL` or `STRIPE_KEY`. They live in a `.env` file locally and are kept *out* of your code so secrets don't end up on GitHub. Before a release, it's worth checking that every variable your app needs locally is also set on the server you deploy to ŌĆö a missing one is a classic "works on my machine, breaks in production" bug. Console lists the names here so you have a checklist; once you connect Vercel, it can compare them against the host.

> [!INFO] **Privacy note**
> Console reads only the *names* of your variables (the left side of the `=`), never the values. Your actual secrets never leave the safe part of the app and are never shown back to you or sent to the AI assistant.

---

## Deploying to Vercel (zero-config)

> [!INFO] **What does "deploy" mean?**
> Deploying means taking the code on your machine and putting a running copy of it on the internet so other people can use it at a real URL. **Vercel** is a hosting service that's popular for web apps. "Zero-config" means you don't have to write any setup files ŌĆö Console figures out the settings for you.

Deploying lives in the **Services** panel (under **Connect & deploy**), because you first connect your Vercel account there. Here's the flow:

1. **Connect Vercel.** In Services, paste a Vercel access token. Console stores it safely (encrypted, in your operating system's keychain) and never shows it back to you.
2. **Press Deploy.** Console inspects your project and *detects* the right settings on its own ŌĆö what framework you're using, what the build command is, and which folder holds the finished site.

   > [!INFO] **What is "the framework" and why detect it?**
   > A framework is the toolkit your app is built with (React/Next.js, Vite, plain HTML, etc.). Vercel needs to know which one so it can build the project correctly. Normally you'd write a config file to tell it; Console reads your project and works it out, so you don't have to. If there's no build step at all, it deploys your files as a plain static site.

3. **Review the dialog.** A small window shows the **project name** (you can edit it) and the **detected framework and output folder**. No `vercel.json` needed.
4. **Deploy and watch.** Console uploads your project to Vercel and then **polls** the build every few seconds, updating the status (*UploadingŌĆ” ŌåÆ BuildingŌĆ” ŌåÆ READY*).

   > [!INFO] **What is "polling"?**
   > Building a site takes a little time. Rather than freeze, Console keeps quietly asking Vercel "are you done yet?" every few seconds and updates the status line each time. When Vercel reports **READY**, your site is live and Console shows you the URL to click.

5. **Done.** When the build reaches **READY**, Console saves this as your project's last deploy and shows the live URL. That same URL then appears on the **Overview** panel's deployment cell and on the Services card, so you can always jump back to your live site.

> [!INFO] **If a deploy fails**
> If Vercel reports `ERROR` or `CANCELED`, Console tells you with a toast and stops. Open the Vercel dashboard to read the build logs ŌĆö the same way you'd debug a failing CI run. A failed deploy never silently looks like a success.

---

## How Pipeline and Release fit together

Think of it as a pipeline of trust:

1. You **push code** ŌåÆ **Pipeline** (CI) automatically checks it.
2. Those checks (plus human sign-offs and security scans) become **Release gates**.
3. When every gate is green, the readiness ring fills and the verdict reads **"Ready to ship."**
4. You **deploy to Vercel**, and the live URL shows up on your Overview.

Each panel is honest about what it knows: if there's no CI, Pipeline says so; if there are no gates, Release says so; if a status can't be trusted, Console shows a blank rather than a guess. That honesty is the point ŌĆö you're learning to ship by watching real signals, not a green light that doesn't mean anything.
