## Pipeline (`/pipeline`, `views/pipeline/`)

`PipelinePanel.tsx` dispatches to `PipelineSeasoned` (Operator) or `PipelineNewbie` (Guided). Header icon `Cable`, title "Pipeline", subtitle "CI/CD stage graph from your workflow files" (Operator) / "CI workflows that run on every push" (Guided).

- `usePipelineDetect(projectRoot)` ŌåÆ `bridge.pipeline.detect(projectRoot)`. staleTime 60s.

### Operator view (`PipelineSeasoned`)

Header right cluster:

Stable row assignment per column. Label = `humanizeSlug(name)` wrapped to Ōēż2 lines (~34 chars/line, `<title>` = raw name); sub-line = `runs_on` (truncated 38). **No status dot is drawn** ŌĆö the `JobNode` `status` prop is never plumbed in, so job nodes render with a neutral `stroke-border` and no per-job dot (status lives only at workflow level). `data-status` defaults to `'unknown'`.

## Release (`/release`, `views/release/`)

`ReleasePanel.tsx` dispatches to `ReleaseSeasoned` (Operator) or `ReleaseNewbie` (Guided). Header icon `Rocket`, title "Release", subtitle "Compliance posture + release readiness".

### Data sources

- `useReleaseList()` ŌåÆ `bridge.release.list(root)`. Returns `ReleaseChecklist[]` summaries `{ version, status: 'in-progress'|'shipped'|'cancelled', ... }`. enabled when `root` set; staleTime 30s. Active version = `selectedVersion ?? list.data[0].version`.
- `useReleaseChecklist(version)` ŌåÆ `bridge.release.get(root)` (returns full `ReleaseChecklist` incl. `gates: ReleaseGate[]`). enabled when version+root set; staleTime 30s.
- `useReleaseSummary(version)` ŌåÆ `bridge.release.summary(root)` ŌåÆ `ReleaseSummary { version, overall_status: GateStatus, green, amber, red, blocked, gate_count }`. staleTime 30s.
- `useEnvLocalNames(projectRoot)` for the env-vars card.
- Source files: `docs/release-checklists/<version>.yaml`.
- `ReleaseGate`: `{ id, label, artifact, status: GateStatus, notes?, blocker? }`. `GateStatus = 'green'|'amber'|'red'|'blocked'`.

### Status taxonomy

| `GateStatus` | Operator badge | Guided badge | Icon |
|---|---|---|---|
| `green` | Passing (success) | Passing (success) | `CheckCircle2` |
| `amber` | Pending (warning) | Needs review (warning) | `AlertCircle` |
| `red` | Failing (danger) | Blocked (danger) | `XCircle` |
| `blocked` | Blocked (secondary) | Waiting (secondary) | `MinusCircle` |

`aggregateGateStatus`: any red/blocked ŌåÆ `blocked`; else any amber ŌåÆ `pending`; else `green`. Empty gates ŌåÆ `pending`.

## Deploy to Vercel (zero-config)

Initiated from **Services** (`views/services/ServicesPanel.tsx`, Vercel `ConnectionCard`), not from Pipeline/Release. Tokens are encrypted in the OS keychain and never returned to the renderer; only boolean/redacted status crosses the bridge.

Flow:
1. **Connect** ŌĆö paste a Vercel token ŌåÆ `bridge.connections.vercel.connect(token)`. Card then shows `Connected as <user> ┬Ę N projects` and exposes **Deploy** / **Disconnect**. **Deploy** is disabled until a project is open (`!projectPath`).
2. **Detect** ŌĆö `openDeploy()` ŌåÆ `bridge.connections.vercel.detectDeploy(projectRoot)` ŌåÆ `{ ok, settings?: VercelDetectedSettings, error? }`.
   - `VercelDetectedSettings`: `{ framework, buildCommand, outputDirectory, installCommand, hasPackageJson, hasBuildScript, isStatic, suggestedName }`.
   - On failure: toast `error`. On success: opens dialog (`vercel-deploy-dialog`), seeds editable **Project name** from `suggestedName`.
3. **Dialog** ŌĆö description branches on `detected.isStatic` ("No build step detected ŌĆö Console will deploy this as a static site.") vs `"Detected <framework> ŌĆö Vercel will build and deploy it. No vercel.json needed."`. Shows `Framework: <framework|static> ┬Ę Output: <outputDirectory|(auto)>`. Dialog is non-dismissable while `deploying`.
4. **Deploy** ŌĆö `runDeploy()` ŌåÆ `bridge.connections.vercel.deploy(projectRoot, { name, framework, buildCommand, outputDirectory, installCommand, target: 'production' })` ŌåÆ `{ ok, deployment?: VercelDeployResult { id, url, inspectorUrl, state }, error? }`. Sets status "UploadingŌĆ”" ŌåÆ "BuildingŌĆ”". On failure: toast + reset.
5. **Poll** ŌĆö `pollState(id)` ŌåÆ `bridge.connections.vercel.deployState(id)` every **3000ms** ŌåÆ `{ ok, state?, url? }`.
   - Updates status line with `state`; surfaces `https://<url>` as a clickable link (`bridge.openExternal`).
   - `state === 'READY'` ŌåÆ `setLastDeploy(projectPath, { url, name, id, at: Date.now() })` (localStorage key `refringence-console-deploy:<projectPath>`), toast success, stop, `onChanged()`.
   - `state === 'ERROR' | 'CANCELED'` ŌåÆ toast error, stop.
6. **Surfacing** ŌĆö `getLastDeploy(projectPath)` (per-project, from `deployStore.ts`) feeds the **Overview** Deployment cell (state badge + live URL) and the Services Vercel card. Console shows only what *it* deployed for that project ŌĆö never the account's global latest.

`bridge.connections.vercel` also exposes `projects`, `deployments`, `redeploy` (used elsewhere on Services/Overview).

---

## Cross-references

- Overview vitals: **Build** cell reads CI status (`metrics.ci`) and links to `/pipeline`; **Deployment** cell appears only when Vercel connected. Sidebar warning dot on `/release` when the release summary aggregates to blocked/red.
- Pipeline Deploy node and Services deploy both key off `vercel.json` / `netlify.toml` detection (`vercelDetected` / `netlifyDetected`).
- Live CI run status and the workflow Trigger both require the GitHub CLI (`gh`) installed and authenticated; absence degrades to shapes-only (Pipeline) or a disabled Trigger.

# Services, connections and deploy

This page is about the **Services** panel ŌĆö the place where you plug Console into the outside tools your project already uses (or will use), and where you can push your project live to the internet.

You'll learn what a "service connection" actually is, why Console handles your secret keys the careful way it does, and how to deploy your project to Vercel without configuring anything by hand.

> [!INFO] **What is a "service"?**
> Your project rarely lives alone. The code is on **GitHub**. It might be hosted on **Vercel**. Crashes get reported to **Sentry**. Your team talks in **Slack**. Each of those is a separate company with its own website and its own login. A *service connection* is just Console proving to one of those companies, "I'm allowed to read this account," so it can pull your data in and show it next to your code. Nothing more magical than that.

---

## Where to find it

Open **Connect & deploy** in the sidebar (Operator mode calls the same panel **Services**). In Guided mode you see a short, curated list of four common starters: **GitHub**, **Vercel**, **Sentry**, and **Stripe**. If you want the full grid of every supported service, click **See all services** at the bottom ŌĆö that flips you into Operator mode where the complete catalog lives.

---

## The four starters

Each card explains, in one line, what connecting it gets you:

- **GitHub** ŌĆö connect your repo so Console can show issues, commits, and pull requests.
- **Vercel** ŌĆö see your deploy status and projects, and deploy your project, without leaving Console.
- **Sentry** ŌĆö surface the latest errors on your Workboard so nothing slips through.
- **Stripe** ŌĆö show live revenue and subscription state alongside the rest of your project.

A green **Connected** badge appears on a card once it's wired up. That badge is never decorative ŌĆö it reflects the real connection state that Console checks each time you open the panel.

---

## Connecting GitHub

GitHub is the easiest one, and it's the one to do first, because most of Console (your Workboard, your release checks, your repo report) is built on top of it.

Click **Connect** on the GitHub card. Console doesn't ask you to paste anything. Instead it reuses a tool you may already have installed: the **GitHub CLI** (`gh`).

> [!INFO] **What is the GitHub CLI?**
> `gh` is a small program from GitHub that you run in a terminal. Once you log into it once (with `gh auth login`), it remembers who you are. Lots of developer tools lean on that saved login instead of inventing their own. Console does exactly this: when you click Connect, it asks `gh` "who is logged in?" and records the answer. **Console never stores a GitHub password or token of its own** ŌĆö it's borrowing the login `gh` already has.

If you haven't logged into `gh` yet, Console will tell you: **Run: gh auth login**. Open a terminal, run that command, follow the prompts, then come back and click Connect again. Once connected, the card shows **Connected as `<your-username>`**.

Disconnecting just makes Console forget ŌĆö your `gh` login stays exactly as it was.

---

## Connecting Vercel

Vercel is where you'll host your project (more on deploying below). Connecting it needs one thing GitHub didn't: a **token**.

> [!INFO] **What is a token?**
> A token is a long, random string of characters that acts like a password *for programs* instead of for humans. You generate it on the service's website, hand it to a tool, and the tool uses it to act on your behalf. The advantage over a real password: you can create several, give each a name, and revoke any one of them later without changing your actual login. Think of it as a single-purpose key you can cut copies of and throw away.

Click **Connect** on the Vercel card. A dialog opens asking for an **Access token**. There's a **Create a token** link that opens the right page on Vercel's site. Generate one there, copy it, paste it into the dialog, and press **Connect**.

Console immediately checks the token against Vercel to confirm it's real, then shows **Connected as `<your-username>` ┬Ę N projects**.

> [!INFO] **Where does the token go? (This matters.)**
> The token does **not** sit in a plain text file, and it is **never sent back to the screen** or to any AI. Console hands it to your operating system's built-in secret vault ŌĆö the **Keychain** on macOS, **DPAPI** on Windows, the system keyring on Linux ŌĆö which encrypts it before it ever touches your disk. From then on the secret lives only inside Console's background process: it's pulled out to talk to Vercel and put straight back. The part of Console you're looking at (the window) only ever learns *yes/no, connected* and your username. This split ŌĆö secrets in the locked drawer, the UI only sees a green light ŌĆö is the single most important idea on this page.

---

## Connecting Sentry

Sentry watches your *running* app and records crashes. Connecting it puts those real production errors right on your Workboard and Observability panel, so a bug your users hit shows up next to the code.

Sentry needs **two** things:

1. An **auth token** (same idea as Vercel's ŌĆö generate it on Sentry, there's a **Create a token** link).
2. Your **organization slug** ŌĆö the short name for your Sentry org, the bit that appears in your Sentry URL (e.g. `my-org`).

> [!INFO] **Why a "slug" and not the full name?**
> A *slug* is the URL-safe version of a name: lowercase, no spaces, dashes instead. Sentry can host many organizations, so the token alone isn't enough ŌĆö Console needs to know *which* org's errors to fetch. The slug is how you point at it.

Fill both in, press **Connect**, and Console verifies the token with Sentry before saving. Once connected, your latest unresolved errors from the last 24 hours show up on the **Observability** panel, and a red dot appears in the sidebar when new errors arrive. The token is stored in your OS secret vault, exactly like Vercel's.

---

## Connecting Slack (Operator grid)

If your team reports bugs in Slack, you can pull those messages in as Workboard items. This card lives in the full Operator grid (**See all services**).

Slack needs a **bot token**, which starts with `xoxb-`. You create it by making a small Slack "app" and installing it to your workspace ŌĆö the dialog links you to the page. The bot needs permission to read channels and their history. Once connected, a **Map channels** button lets you tag each channel as **Tech**, **Non-tech**, or **Test** so the right messages land on your Workboard. (Console only sees channels the bot has been invited to.)

---

## A faster path: connect straight from your `.env`

Most projects keep their secret keys in a file called `.env`. If Console finds keys it recognizes there, it offers a shortcut at the top of the panel under **From your .env**.

> [!INFO] **What is a `.env` file?**
> `.env` is a plain text file in your project where you store secrets and settings as `NAME=value` lines ŌĆö like `VERCEL_TOKEN=abc123`. Your code reads from it at runtime. It's kept out of version control (it should be in your `.gitignore`) precisely *because* it holds secrets. It's the standard place a project stashes the very keys Console wants to connect with.

Console scans your `.env` and lists any services it can wire up ŌĆö for example a `VERCEL_TOKEN` or `SENTRY_AUTH_TOKEN` it spotted. Each row shows the service name and the **key name** it found. Click **Link** and Console connects that service for you.

> [!INFO] **It reads the names, not the values ŌĆö until you ask.**
> When Console *scans*, it only ever looks at the **names** of your keys (the `VERCEL_TOKEN` part), never the secret value after the `=`. Only the names cross into the window. The moment you click **Link**, the background process reads that one value just long enough to test it and lock it in the OS vault, then drops it. It's never shown back to you and never sent to AI. The on-screen note says exactly this ŌĆö it's a promise the code keeps.

This works for OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google AI keys (which power the Assistant) as well as Vercel, Sentry, and Slack.

---

## Deploying your project to Vercel

Here's the part that turns "code on my laptop" into "a live website anyone can visit." Once Vercel is connected, the Vercel card grows a **Deploy** button.

> [!INFO] **What does "deploy" mean?**
> Deploying is taking your project's files, putting them on a server somewhere on the internet, and getting back a public web address (a URL) where your app now runs. Before deploying, only you can see your project. After, the whole world can. "Zero-config" means you don't have to write any setup files or answer a list of questions first ŌĆö Console and Vercel figure out the sensible defaults for you.

### How it works, step by step

1. **Click Deploy.** Console reads your project and tries to recognize what it is ŌĆö a Next.js app, a Vite app, an Astro site, a plain static site, and so on. It does this by peeking at your `package.json` (the file that lists your project's tools).

   > [!INFO] **What is `package.json`?**
   > It's the manifest for a JavaScript/TypeScript project: its name, and the list of libraries it depends on, and the commands to build and run it. Console reads the dependency list to guess your **framework** ŌĆö if it sees `next`, it knows it's a Next.js app; if it sees `vite`, a Vite app ŌĆö so it can tell Vercel how to build it.

2. **A dialog opens** showing what Console detected: a **Project name** (you can edit it), the **framework**, and the **output directory** (where your built files will land). If Console found no build step at all, it says it'll deploy your project as a plain static site ŌĆö that's fine, lots of projects are.

3. **Press "Deploy to production."** Console uploads your project's source files to Vercel.

   > [!INFO] **What does Console *not* upload?**
   > It deliberately skips things that shouldn't travel: your `node_modules` folder (the downloaded libraries ŌĆö Vercel re-downloads them fresh), any build output (Vercel rebuilds it), your `.git` history, and ŌĆö importantly ŌĆö your **`.env` files and any keys or certificates**. Your secrets stay on your machine. This skip-list is built into the deploy, not something you have to remember.

4. **Watch the status.** The dialog shows the deploy moving through stages ŌĆö *Uploading ŌåÆ Building ŌåÆ READY*. Console checks Vercel every few seconds and updates the line for you. When it reaches **READY**, you get a clickable public URL. Click it to see your project live.

The first time you deploy, Vercel **creates the project for you** from the name you chose ŌĆö so a single Deploy click handles both "make a new project" and "ship it." Console also remembers this deploy for *this specific project folder*, so its live URL shows up on your **Overview** afterward.

> [!INFO] **Why does the URL sometimes look different from the build URL?**
> Vercel gives every deploy a long unique address, but it also assigns a short, clean public one (like `your-project.vercel.app`). Some of those long unique addresses are locked behind a login wall by Vercel's protection settings. Console prefers the clean public URL so the link you click actually opens for everyone.

---

## The privacy rule, in one sentence

Every secret you give Console ŌĆö every token, every key ŌĆö is encrypted by your operating system's vault and lives only in Console's background process. The window you look at, and any AI you chat with, only ever learn **connected: yes/no** and harmless labels like your username. That's the whole design.

---

## Quick reference

| Service | What you provide | What it powers |
|---|---|---|
| **GitHub** | Nothing ŌĆö reuses your `gh` CLI login | Workboard, releases, repo report |
| **Vercel** | Access token (in a dialog) | Project list, deploy status, one-click deploy |
| **Sentry** | Auth token + org slug | Live production errors on Workboard/Observability |
| **Slack** | Bot token (`xoxb-...`) | Channel messages pulled into the Workboard |
| **From `.env`** | Just click **Link** | Fast-connects any of the above it finds |
| **PostHog, Stripe, others** | ŌĆö | "Open docs" for now ŌĆö real connection coming later |

> [!INFO] **A note on the "Open docs" services**
> Some cards (PostHog, Plausible, Netlify, Fly.io, Railway, Supabase, Stripe) don't have a real Console connection yet ŌĆö clicking them simply opens that provider's setup page in your browser. They're placeholders for connections being built. GitHub, Vercel, Sentry, and Slack are the four that genuinely wire in today.
operator:
# Services, connections and deploy

Reference for the **Services** panel (`/services`, `views/services/`). Connects Console to external providers and runs zero-config Vercel deploys. Persona-branched: `ServicesSeasoned` (Operator) and `ServicesNewbie` (Guided). Sidebar label is **Services** (Operator) / **Connect & deploy** (Guided). Default persona = `seasoned`.

All connection state flows through the `connections` bridge namespace (`window.refringenceConsole.connections.*`) and the `env` namespace. **Tokens never cross the IPC boundary.** The renderer receives only metadata: `{ connected: boolean, login?/user?/org?/team? }`. Backed by `console-electron/src/main/ipc/connections.ts`, `connections.ts` (store), `envConnect.ts`, and `ipc/vercelDeploy.ts`.

---

## 1. Real vs. docs-only services

Four providers have a real connection backend. Everything else is a **DocsCard** that opens the provider's token page via `bridge.openExternal` and never stores anything.

| Provider | Backend | Token type | Stored as |
|---|---|---|---|
| **GitHub** | `gh` CLI auth reuse | none stored | meta flag only |
| **Vercel** | Vercel REST API | personal access token | safeStorage |
| **Sentry** | Sentry REST API | auth token + org slug | safeStorage |
| **Slack** | Slack Web API | bot token `xoxb-` | safeStorage |
| Netlify, Fly.io, Railway, PostHog, Plausible, Supabase, Stripe | none | ŌĆö | **DocsCard only** |

**PostHog is docs-only.** Its card (`connectUrl: https://app.posthog.com/me/settings`) opens PostHog's settings page in the browser on click and toggles a local `opened` flag; there is no PostHog IPC, no stored token, no status check. The same is true of Plausible, Netlify, Fly.io, Railway, Supabase, and Stripe. The Overview's "live activity counters" copy for PostHog is aspirational ŌĆö not wired.

---

## 2. Operator layout (`ServicesSeasoned`)

`PanelHeader` (icon `Plug`, title "Services", subtitle "Connect hosting, observability, payments, and storage"). Header actions:
- **CompareServicesButton** (`ServiceComparison.tsx`) ŌĆö capability + 2026-pricing comparison, budget cap from `localStorage['refringence-console-budget-cap']`.
- **Add MCP server** ŌĆö `disabled`, `title="Coming soon"`.

Body order:
1. **GitHub hero** (`services-hero`) ŌĆö rendered only when `githubStatus === 'disconnected'`. One-click **Connect GitHub** (`services-hero-connect`) ŌåÆ `bridge.connections.github.connect()` ŌåÆ invalidates `['connections']`.
2. **EnvConnectCard** (`From your .env`) ŌĆö see ┬¦6.
3. **Category sections** from `CATEGORIES`: Hosting (`host`), Observability, Messaging, Analytics, Database, Payment, Auth. Each renders the matching `SERVICES` cards in a 1/2/3-col grid.

`SERVICES` catalog (id ┬Ę category ┬Ę blurb): `github` (host), `vercel` (host), `netlify` (host), `fly` (host), `railway` (host), `sentry` (observability), `slack` (messaging), `posthog` (analytics), `plausible` (analytics), `supabase` (database), `stripe` (payment). Card dispatch: `github`ŌåÆ`GithubCard`, `vercel`ŌåÆ`VercelCard`, `sentry`ŌåÆ`SentryCard`, `slack`ŌåÆ`SlackCard`, all othersŌåÆ`DocsCard`.

### Status resolution (`ConnStatus`)
`statusFor(connected)` collapses the `useConnections()` query into `loading | error | connected | disconnected`:
- `connections.isError` ŌåÆ `error` ŌåÆ badge **"Couldn't check connections"**.
- `isLoading || data === undefined` ŌåÆ `loading` ŌåÆ badge **"CheckingŌĆ”"**.
- settled ŌåÆ `connected ? 'connected' : 'disconnected'` ŌåÆ **Connected** (green, check icon) / **Not connected**.

This deliberately avoids flashing "Not connected" before the query settles and surfaces backend failures honestly. `ConnectionCard` adds a `border-ring` highlight while connected.

### Data reads
- `useConnections()` ŌåÆ `bridge.connections.list()`, queryKey `['connections','list']`, `staleTime 30s`. Returns `ConnectionMeta { github, vercel, sentry, slack }`, each `{ connected, login?/user?/org?/team?, connectedAt? }`.
- `useVercelProjects(enabled)` ŌåÆ `bridge.connections.vercel.projects()`, queryKey `['connections','vercel','projects']`, `staleTime 60s`, enabled only when `vercel.connected`. Gives the project count on the Vercel card.
- `useActiveProject()` ŌåÆ `project.path` (the deploy target).

---

## 3. GitHub card

`bridge.connections.github.connect()` / `.disconnect()`. **No token is stored.** The main handler runs `gh auth status`; on non-zero exit returns `{ ok:false, error:'Run: gh auth login' }`. On success it runs `gh api user --jq .login`, records `GithubMeta { connected:true, login, connectedAt }` in `connections.json`. Disconnect clears only the Console-side meta ŌĆö the `gh` login is untouched.

- Connected state: "Connected as `<login>`" + **Disconnect**.
- Disconnected state: **Connect** (busy label "ConnectingŌĆ”").
- On any result ŌåÆ `onChanged()` invalidates `['connections']`; sonner toast on success/error.

Same gh-reuse pattern as `ipc/issues.ts`. 8s spawn timeout per gh call.

---

## 4. Vercel card

### Connect
**Connect** opens `vercel-connect-dialog`: password `Input` for the access token (Enter submits), a **Create a token** link ŌåÆ `https://vercel.com/account/tokens`. `bridge.connections.vercel.connect(token.trim())`:
- Main validates against `GET https://api.vercel.com/v2/user`. On non-2xx ŌåÆ `{ ok:false, error }` (message lifted from the Vercel error body). On success extracts `user.username || user.name`, calls `setToken('vercel', token)` (safeStorage), saves `VercelMeta { connected, user, connectedAt }`.
- Renderer shows toast, clears the token field, closes dialog, invalidates `['connections']`.

Connected state shows "Connected as `<user>` ┬Ę N projects" (count from `useVercelProjects`), plus **Deploy** and **Disconnect**. Disconnect ŌåÆ `clearToken('vercel')` + `clearMeta('vercel')`.

### Deploy (zero-config) ŌĆö `VercelCard` deploy flow
`Deploy` is disabled unless `projectPath` is set (`title` flips to "Open a project first").

1. **`openDeploy()`** ŌåÆ `bridge.connections.vercel.detectDeploy(projectRoot)` ŌåÆ `VercelDetectedSettings`. Opens `vercel-deploy-dialog`, seeds `deployName` with `settings.suggestedName`.
2. Dialog shows: editable **Project name** `Input`; a line "Framework: `<framework>` ┬Ę Output: `<outputDirectory ?? (auto)>`"; the description differs for `isStatic` ("No build step detected - Console will deploy this as a static site") vs detected framework ("Detected `<framework>` - Vercel will build and deploy it. No vercel.json needed.").
3. **Deploy to production** ŌåÆ `runDeploy()` ŌåÆ `bridge.connections.vercel.deploy(projectRoot, settings)` with `{ name, framework, buildCommand, outputDirectory, installCommand, target:'production' }`. Status text walks `UploadingŌĆ” ŌåÆ BuildingŌĆ”`.
4. **`pollState(id)`** loops `bridge.connections.vercel.deployState(id)` every 3000 ms. Updates `deployStatus`; sets `deployedUrl = https://<url>` when present. Terminal handling: `READY` ŌåÆ `setLastDeploy(projectPath, {url,name,id,at})` + success toast + `onChanged()`; `ERROR`/`CANCELED` ŌåÆ error toast, stops.
5. Dialog cannot be dismissed (`onOpenChange` guarded) while `deploying`. The resolved URL renders as an accent link ŌåÆ `bridge.openExternal`.

### Detection logic (`detectDeploy`, main)
Reads `package.json`, merges `dependencies` + `devDependencies`. Framework inference (first match): `next`ŌåÆ`nextjs`; `@sveltejs/kit`ŌåÆ`sveltekit`; `nuxt`ŌåÆ`nuxtjs`; `astro`ŌåÆ`astro` (out `dist`); `@angular/core`ŌåÆ`angular`; `gatsby`ŌåÆ`gatsby`; `vite`ŌåÆ`vite` (out `dist`); `react-scripts`ŌåÆ`create-react-app` (out `build`). `buildCommand`/`installCommand` returned `null` (Vercel uses framework defaults / `npm run build`). `isStatic = !pkg || !hasBuildScript`. `suggestedName = sanitizeName(pkg.name || basename(root))` (lowercase, alnum+`._-`, strips npm scope, Ōēż100 chars).

### Upload logic (`deployProject`, main)
`walkProject(root, maxFiles=4000)` SHA-1s each file. **Excluded dirs**: `node_modules .git dist build out .next .nuxt .vercel .svelte-kit .astro coverage .cache .turbo .parcel-cache .vite .output`. **Skipped files**: `.DS_Store`, `Thumbs.db`, `*.log`, `.env` and `.env.*`, `*.pem/.key/.p12/.pfx/.crt`, `id_rsa*`, `id_ed25519*`, `.npmrc`. Uploads via `POST /v2/files` (concurrency 6, `x-vercel-digest` = sha). Creates the deployment via `POST /v13/deployments` with `{ name, files, projectSettings, target }` ŌĆö **the `name` field creates the Vercel project on first deploy** (single flow = create + deploy). Errors throw the Vercel body message.

### State resolution (`deploymentState`, main)
`GET /v13/deployments/:id`. Prefers the **shortest production alias** (clean public `<project>.vercel.app`) over the deployment-specific `url`, which Vercel deployment protection may gate behind a login wall. Returns `{ state: readyState ?? state ?? 'UNKNOWN', url }`.

### deployStore (`lib/deployStore.ts`)
Per-project-path record `StoredDeploy { url, name, id, at }` in `localStorage['refringence-console-deploy:<projectPath>']`. Read by the Overview Deployment cell so a freshly opened folder shows "not deployed" rather than an unrelated project's URL. `useVercelDeployments` (queries.connections, `refetchInterval 30s`) tracks BUILDINGŌåÆREADY for the Overview live URL.

### Other Vercel bridge methods
`vercel.deployments(projectId?)` ŌåÆ `GET /v6/deployments?limit=10[&projectId]`. `vercel.redeploy(projectId, deploymentId)` ŌåÆ `POST /v13/deployments` with `{ name, deploymentId, target:'production' }` (defensive; least-certain shape).

---

## 5. Sentry & Slack cards

### Sentry (`SentryCard`)
`sentry-connect-dialog`: **Auth token** (password) + **Organization slug** `Input`s, **Create a token** ŌåÆ `https://sentry.io/settings/account/api/auth-tokens/`. Both fields required (client-side toast guards). `bridge.connections.sentry.connect(token, org)`:
- Main validates `GET https://sentry.io/api/0/` with the bearer token; on success `setToken('sentry', token)` + `SentryMeta { connected, user: org||'sentry', org, connectedAt }`.
- Connected state: "Connected: `<org ?? sentry>`" + **Disconnect**.
- `sentry.issues()` (used by Observability, not this panel) ŌåÆ `GET /api/0/organizations/<org>/issues/?query=is:unresolved&statsPeriod=24h&limit=10`; requires a stored org or returns "no organization on record; reconnect Sentry". Polled 60s via `useSentryIssues`.

### Slack (`SlackCard`)
`slack-connect-dialog`: **Bot token** (password, must start `xoxb-`), **Create a Slack app** ŌåÆ `https://api.slack.com/apps`. Description names required scopes: `channels:read`, `channels:history`, `groups:history` (private). `bridge.connections.slack.connect(token)`:
- Main rejects non-`xoxb-` before any network call, then validates `GET https://slack.com/api/auth.test`. Slack returns HTTP 200 with `{ ok:false, error }` on app errors, so the body error is the real signal. On success `setToken('slack', token)` + `SlackMeta { connected, team, user, connectedAt, channelTeams (preserved) }`.
- Connected state: "Connected: `<team>`" + **Map channels** (`slack-map-channels`) + **Disconnect**.
- **Map channels** dialog (`slack-map-dialog`): `useSlackChannels(connected)` lists channels the bot can see; each has a `Select` (`slack-channel-team-<id>`) ŌåÆ none / tech / nontech / test ŌåÆ `bridge.slack.setChannelTeam(id, value)`, invalidating `['slack','channels']` + `['slack','issues']`. "No channels visible" hint instructs inviting the bot. Channel ŌåÆ team mapping drives the Workboard's Slack source.

---

## 6. EnvConnectCard (`From your .env`)

Rendered only when an active project path exists. `useQuery(['env','connectable', root])` ŌåÆ `bridge.env.scanConnectable(root)`, `staleTime 30s`. States: scanning (`services-env-scanning`), error + Retry (`services-env-error`), populated (`services-env-connect`), or hidden when empty.

Main (`envConnect.ts`) parses `.env`, `.env.local`, `.env.development`, `.env.production` (first file wins per name; strips `export`, quotes). Connectors and key names:
- `openai` ŌåÉ `OPENAI_API_KEY`
- `anthropic` ŌåÉ `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`
- `google` ŌåÉ `GOOGLE_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `GOOGLE_GENERATIVE_AI_API_KEY`
- `vercel` ŌåÉ `VERCEL_TOKEN` / `VERCEL_API_TOKEN`
- `sentry` ŌåÉ `SENTRY_AUTH_TOKEN` (org taken from `SENTRY_ORG`)
- `slack` ŌåÉ `SLACK_BOT_TOKEN` / `SLACK_TOKEN`

**Names-only across IPC.** `scanConnectable` returns `EnvConnectable { id, name, category, keyName }` ŌĆö `keyName` is the NAME only, never the value. **Link** ŌåÆ `bridge.env.connect(root, id)`: main reads the value **transiently in-process**, routes AI providers to `setKey` (AI keystore) and Vercel/Sentry/Slack to the same `connect*` validate+store path the manual dialogs use, then drops the value. On success: toast `Linked <name> from .env (<detail>)`, invalidates `['connections']` + `['ai','keyStatus']`, refetches the scan. The on-card note states the contract: names detected, value read transiently to test+store, "never shown back, never sent to AI."

---

## 7. Token storage & encryption (`connections.ts`, store)

Two state kinds in `<userData>`:
- **Non-secret metadata** ŌåÆ `<userData>/connections.json` (plain fs + JSON, *not* electron-store ŌĆö that crashed on an ESM mismatch). Holds `{ connected, login/user/org/team, connectedAt, channelTeams }` per provider.
- **Secret tokens** ŌåÆ `<userData>/connections/<provider>.token`, one file per provider, encrypted via Electron **`safeStorage`** (OS keychain on macOS, **DPAPI on Windows**, libsecret/keyring on Linux). `chmod 0600` best-effort (no-op on Windows ACLs).

`setToken` throws if `safeStorage.isEncryptionAvailable()` is false ŌĆö the connect is aborted rather than writing plaintext (surfaced to the user as "Encryption is unavailable on this systemŌĆ”"). `getToken` returns `null` (never throws) on missing/undecryptable. Tokens are read back per-request server-side, used to call the provider API, and **never returned to the renderer or logged**. Disconnect = `clearToken` + `clearMeta`.

---

## 8. Guided layout (`ServicesNewbie`)

`PanelHeader` (icon `Plug`, title "Services"), intro "A few services to plug Console into.", single column `max-w-[820px]`, four `STARTERS`: GitHub, Vercel, Sentry, Stripe. `statusOf(id)` reads the same `useConnections()` data ŌĆö the Connected pill is real, not cosmetic.

- **GitHub** ŌĆö connects in place (`bridge.connections.github.connect/disconnect`), filled primary button, label toggles Connect/Disconnect.
- **Vercel / Sentry** ŌĆö open an in-place `ServiceTokenDialog` (`service-connect-<id>`) rather than flipping to Operator. Token paste on connect (Sentry also takes org slug); on connect-when-already-connected the dialog shows **Manage** (open provider + destructive **Disconnect**). Same `vercel.connect` / `sentry.connect` bridge calls. Label: Connect / Manage.
- **Stripe** ŌĆö no backend; **Open docs** ŌåÆ `bridge.openExternal('https://dashboard.stripe.com/apikeys')`.
- Footer **See all services** ŌåÆ `setPersona('seasoned')` (the Operator grid).

Note: Guided Services has **no Deploy and no Slack** card and **no EnvConnectCard** ŌĆö those live only in the Operator grid.

---

## 9. Bridge surface (`connections` + `env`)

```
connections.list(): ConnectionMeta
connections.github.connect(): { ok, login?, error? }
connections.github.disconnect(): { ok, error? }
connections.vercel.connect(token): { ok, user?, error? }
connections.vercel.disconnect(): { ok, error? }
connections.vercel.projects(): { ok, projects?: VercelProject[], error? }
connections.vercel.deployments(projectId?): { ok, deployments?, error? }
connections.vercel.redeploy(projectId, deploymentId): { ok, deployment?, error? }
connections.vercel.detectDeploy(projectRoot): { ok, settings?: VercelDetectedSettings, error? }
connections.vercel.deploy(projectRoot, settings): { ok, deployment?: VercelDeployResult, error? }
connections.vercel.deployState(id): { ok, state?, url?, error? }
connections.sentry.connect(token, org): { ok, user?, org?, error? }
connections.sentry.disconnect(): { ok, error? }
connections.sentry.issues(): { ok, issues?: SentryIssue[], error? }
connections.slack.connect(token): { ok, team?, user?, error? }
connections.slack.disconnect(): { ok, error? }
env.localNames(projectRoot): EnvLocalNames
env.scanConnectable(projectRoot): EnvConnectable[]   // NAMES only
env.connect(projectRoot, serviceId): { ok, detail?, error? }
slack.channels() / slack.setChannelTeam(id, team) / slack.issues()
```

In browser dev (no Electron) the `env`/`connections` namespaces are throwing stubs.

title: Observability
guided:
# Observability

Observability is where you watch your project actually *run*. Two things live here, and it helps to keep them separate in your head:

1. **Your own checks** - tests, evals, smoke checks, and CI - that you run on demand and watch stream live.
2. **What happens after you ship** - real errors hitting real users, pulled in from Sentry once you connect it.

The first half answers "is my code correct *before* I ship?" The second answers "is my code OK *now that people are using it*?"

> [!INFO] **What "observability" actually means.** In software engineering, observability is your ability to understand what a running system is doing from the outside - without attaching a debugger or guessing. It is built from three kinds of signal: **logs** (lines a program prints as it works), **metrics** (numbers counted over time, like "errors in the last 24 hours"), and **traces** (the path a single request took). This panel gives you a beginner-friendly slice of all three: live log output while a check runs, counters for runs and errors, and a feed of production errors.

Open it from the sidebar (**Monitor** in Guided mode) or press `g y` from anywhere.

---

## The big button: Run a test

At the top of the panel there is one primary button: **Run a test**.

Press it. Console runs `npm run smoke` inside your project and shows you a small status line - "Your test is running. This can take a minute." The button changes to **Running.** and is locked so you cannot start a second copy on top of the first.

> [!INFO] **What a "smoke test" is.** The name comes from electronics: power on a new circuit board and if smoke comes out, something is badly wrong - no need for finer tests yet. A software smoke test is the same idea. It is a small, fast check that the most important things work at all: the app starts, the home page loads, nothing is on fire. It is not exhaustive. It is the *first* test you run, to decide whether running the slower, deeper tests is even worth it.

> [!INFO] **What `npm run smoke` is doing.** `npm` is the tool that runs scripts defined in your project's `package.json` file. `npm run smoke` looks for a script literally named "smoke" and runs it. Console did not invent this command - it is reading the script *you* (or whoever set up the project) already defined. If your project has no `smoke` script, the test will report that the script was not found. That is normal and not a Console bug.

When the run finishes you get a small toast notification - "Test run finished." or "Test run failed." - and the result appears below as a card.

---

## Reading the last run

After a run completes, the **Last run** card tells you in one plain sentence how it went:

- "Your last test run finished cleanly." - everything passed.
- "Your last test run had 2 problems." - something failed; the number is how many failing artifacts were found.
- "Your last test run finished, but the result is unclear." - the run produced no files Console could read a pass/fail from.

It carries a colored badge - **Passing** (green), **Failed** (red), or **Unclear** (grey) - and tells you *when* it ran ("12 min ago", "3:42 PM").

> [!INFO] **Why a result can be "unclear".** Console figures out pass/fail by looking at the *files a run leaves behind* in a folder. If a run wrote no files, or wrote files Console doesn't recognise, it honestly says "unclear" rather than guessing green. This is a deliberate engineering value: a monitoring tool that lies green when it doesn't actually know is worse than useless - you would trust it and ship a broken build. "Unclear" means "go look yourself."

### Open the report

Two buttons let you go deeper:

- **See the report folder** (on the card) and **Open last test report** (below it) both open the folder on disk where the run saved its output - logs, screenshots, JSON, whatever the test produced. This opens in your file explorer, not inside Console.

> [!INFO] **What an "artifact" is.** An artifact is any file a build or test run produces and saves for you to inspect later - a log file, a screenshot of a failing page, a coverage report, a JSON results file. The word just means "a thing the run made." Console keeps every run's artifacts in its own folder so you can always go back and see exactly what happened, even weeks later.

---

## Recent runs

Below the last run, you see up to three more recent runs as a short list. Each row is one sentence - what kind of run it was, when, a status badge, and an **Open** button to reach that run's folder. This is your short-term history: "did the last few checks pass?"

If you have never run anything, you get an empty state: *"No test runs yet. Press Run a test above, or open a terminal and run `npm run smoke`."*

---

## Switching to the full Operator view

Guided mode keeps Observability deliberately simple - one button, one last-run card, a few recent runs. The Operator view adds far more: separate buttons for evals / end-to-end / smoke / CI, live streaming consoles, eval pass-rates, raw counters, the full runs table, and the production-error feed from Sentry.

Press **Switch to Operator view** in the header any time you want the full surface. You can switch back from Settings. Nothing is hidden from you - it is just paced differently.

> [!INFO] **The other kinds of checks you'll meet in Operator view.** A quick glossary so they aren't intimidating:
> - **Eval** - short for *evaluation*. When your app uses an AI model, an eval is a test for the *AI's* answers: you give it known inputs and check the outputs are good. Console runs these with a tool called promptfoo. Evals are how you stop an AI feature from silently getting worse.
> - **End-to-end (e2e)** - a test that drives the *whole* app the way a real user would: open the page, click the button, check the result appeared. "End to end" means from the very front (the click) to the very back (the database) and back again.
> - **CI** - *Continuous Integration*. A robot that runs your tests automatically every time you push code, on a clean machine in the cloud, so "works on my computer" can't hide a broken build. Console's **Run CI** button triggers that robot on demand.

---

## After you ship: production errors

The bottom of the (Operator) panel is about what happens *after* your code is live and real people are using it. This is fed by **Sentry**, a service that catches errors thrown in your running app and reports them back to you.

> [!INFO] **Why production errors are a different thing from test failures.** A test failure happens on *your* machine, before anyone sees it - you caught the problem early, which is the whole point. A production error happens on a *user's* machine, *after* you shipped - it is a problem that escaped every test and is hurting someone right now. That is why it gets its own section, its own red counter, and (in the sidebar) a red dot that pulls your attention. Both matter; they just live at different stages of the journey.

Until you connect Sentry, this section simply says *"Connect Sentry in Services to see production errors."* Go to **Connect & deploy** (Services) to wire it up. Once connected, each unresolved error shows its title, where it came from, a severity badge, how many times it has happened, and an **Open** button to view it on Sentry's own site.

> [!INFO] **The red dot in the sidebar.** When your project has errors in the last 24 hours, Console puts a small red dot on the Monitor item in the sidebar, and pops one desktop notification the first time the count crosses from zero. It is a gentle "something needs your eyes" - not an alarm. The number it watches is the same "Errors / 24h" counter you'll see in the Operator view.

---

## What to take away

- **Run a test** is the one action that matters here - it runs your project's smoke check live and tells you, in a sentence, whether it passed.
- A result can honestly be **unclear**; that is the tool refusing to fake confidence.
- Every run keeps its files in a folder you can open and inspect.
- **Before shipping**, you watch tests. **After shipping**, you watch production errors from Sentry. Same panel, two halves of the same question: *is my software actually working?*
operator:
# Observability

Route `/observability` ┬Ę sidebar `g y` ┬Ę `views/observability/`. Persona branch in `ObservabilityPanel.tsx`: `isNewbie ŌåÆ <ObservabilityNewbie/>`, else `<ObservabilitySeasoned/>`. This page documents the Operator (`seasoned`) surface; the Guided variant is summarised at the end.

Header (`PanelHeader`): icon `Activity`, title **Observability**, subtitle "QA run artifacts, counters, and trace logs". Right slot renders an **as-of** freshness stamp (`obs-freshness`) = `max(runs.dataUpdatedAt, counters.dataUpdatedAt)` formatted `HH:MM`, shown only once data has loaded.

Layout: single vertical scroll column (`observability-panel`), six `SectionDivider`-labelled sections in order: **Run ŌåÆ Evals ŌåÆ Counters ŌåÆ Production errors (Sentry) ŌåÆ Runs ŌåÆ Artifact log**.

## Data sources

| Hook | Bridge call | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `useObsCounters()` | `bridge.obs.counters(root)` | `staleTime 30s`, `refetchInterval 60s`. Enabled only when a project is active. |
| `useObsRuns()` | `bridge.obs.runs(root)` | `staleTime 30s`. Returns `RunEntry[]`. |
| `useObsRunDetail(runId)` | `bridge.obs.runDetail(root, runId)` | Lazy; enabled only when a row is expanded (`runId !== null`). |
| `useConnections()` | `connections.list` | `sentryConnected = data.sentry.connected`. |
| `useSentryIssues(connected)` | `connections.sentry.issues` | Gated on `sentryConnected`. |
| `useEvalsHealth()` | `bridge.evals.health(root)` | Used for `promptfooOutputPresent`. |
| `usePromptfooSummary()` | `bridge.evals.promptfooSummary(root)` | `PromptfooSummary \| null`. |
| `useProjectCapabilities(root)` | `bridge.repoIntrospect.capabilities` | `hasEvals` / `hasTests` / `hasCiWorkflows` gate Run buttons. |
| `usePipelineDetect(root)` | `bridge.pipeline.detect` | `workflows[0]` drives the `Run CI` invocation. |
| `useRunner()` | `runner.start/stop` + `onOutput/onComplete` subscription | Streaming process runner (see below). |

`root` is the active project's path (`useActiveProject`); all run/counter queries are no-ops with no project mounted. Runs and counters are scoped per project.

`RunEntry` = `{ runId, startedAt(ISO), artifactKinds[], totalFiles, totalBytes }`. `ObsCounters` = `{ runs, runs_last_24h, errors, errors_last_24h }`. `RunDetail` = `{ runId, files: {relPath, kind, sizeBytes}[], status: 'ok'|'failed'|'unknown', statusSource: 'manifest'|'heuristic' }`. Runs are read from `.refringence-qa/runs/<runId>/` (artifact root; the Open buttons resolve paths relative to that).

---

## 1. Run section (`obs-run-surface`)

Toolbar (`obs-run-toolbar`) of four `RunButton`s. Each owns a stable `RunKey` (`evals` | `e2e` | `smoke` | `ci`) so it can self-disable while its own run is live (single-fire guard) and label the spawned process. Buttons use `variant="secondary"`, `size="sm"`.

| Button | testid | Invocation (`runner.start`) | Enabled when | Disabled tooltip |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Run evals** (`FlaskConical`) | `obs-run-evals` | `kind:'npm'`, args `['run','eval:promptfoo']` | `capabilities.hasEvals` | "No eval config found" |
| **Run e2e** (`TestTube`) | `obs-run-e2e` | `kind:'npm'`, args `['run','qa:e2e']` | `capabilities.hasTests` | "No tests found" |
| **Run smoke** (`Zap`) | `obs-run-smoke` | `kind:'npm'`, args `['run','smoke']` | always | ŌĆö |
| **Run CI** (`GitBranch`) | `obs-run-ci` | `kind:'gh'`, args `['workflow','run',<firstWorkflow.path>]` | `hasCiWorkflows && firstWorkflow` | "No CI workflows found" / "No workflow to trigger" |

`RunnerKind` Ōłł `'npm' | 'gh' | 'playwright' | 'node'`. All four launch with `cwd = projectRoot || undefined` and `label = RUN_LABELS[key]`. A button shows `PlayCircle` (pulsing) + text **"Running."** while its run is active and is `disabled` (the guard returns early if `isActive(key)`); `Run CI` no-ops if `firstWorkflow` is undefined.

### Live output

For each `RunKey` with a tracked run, a block (`obs-run-live-<key>`) renders:
- While `status==='running'`: a **`RunningIndicator`** ŌĆö `Loader2` spinner (accent), label, live `mm:ss` elapsed ticking client-side from `startedAt`, and a **Cancel** button (`X`) calling `stop(runId)` ŌåÆ `runner.stop`.
- A **`LiveConsole`** of accumulated `lines` (stdout/stderr, timestamped) for the run ŌĆö streamed line-by-line via `runner.onOutput`.

### Runner mechanics (`useRunner`)

One renderer-wide subscription to `runner.onOutput` (appends `RunLine{line,stream,ts}` to the matching `runId`) and `runner.onComplete` (`RunnerComplete{exitCode, durationMs, killed}` ŌåÆ `RunStatus`). `start(opts)` returns a `runId`; status mapping: `killed ŌåÆ 'killed'`, `exitCode===0 ŌåÆ 'done'`, else `'failed'`.

Completion side-effects (in `RunSurface`, via a `notified` ref set so each runId fires once across re-renders):
- `done` ŌåÆ `toast.success("<label> finished.")`
- `failed` ŌåÆ `toast.error("<label> failed (exit <code>).")`
- `killed` ŌåÆ `toast.message("<label> cancelled.")`
- Always invalidates `['obs','runs']` and `['obs','counters']` so a fresh artifact run appears in the table and the counters refresh.

---

## 2. Evals section ŌĆö `EvalsSummary`

Driven by `usePromptfooSummary()` + `useEvalsHealth().promptfooOutputPresent`.

- **Empty** (`obs-evals-empty`): when no promptfoo output present or no summary. Title "No eval results yet." Subline branches on `hasEvals`: "Use Run evals above to populate this card." vs "No eval config found in this project."
- **Summary** (`obs-evals-summary`): a row with a status `Badge` ŌĆö **All passing** (`success`) when `failed===0 && errors===0`, else **Failures** (`danger`) ŌĆö then three `EvalTally` figures: `passed` (CheckCircle2, success), `failed` (XCircle, danger when >0 else muted), `errors` (AlertCircle, warning when >0 else muted). Right-aligned "Last run `<timestamp>`" when present.

`PromptfooSummary` also carries `total`, `totalCostUsd`, `durationMs`, and per-row `results[]` (not surfaced on this card). Source is `bridge.evals.promptfooSummary` reading the project's promptfoo output JSON.

---

## 3. Counters section (`obs-counters`)

Four `CounterTile`s in a `grid-cols-2 sm:grid-cols-4`, values `toLocaleString()`, `'-'` while loading, zero rendered muted:

- **Runs total** = `counters.runs`
- **Runs / 24h** = `counters.runs_last_24h`
- **Errors total** = `counters.errors`
- **Errors / 24h** = `counters.errors_last_24h`, `tone='rose'` (danger text) when `> 0`.

`Errors / 24h > 0` is the same signal that drives the sidebar red dot on `/observability` and the once-per-session OS notification (fired from the Sidebar, not this panel) when the 24h error count crosses 0ŌåÆpositive.

---

## 4. Production errors (Sentry) ŌĆö `SentryErrors`

States:
- **Not connected** (`obs-sentry-disconnected`): "Connect Sentry in Services to see production errors." (no link element; go to `/services`).
- **Loading**: "Loading Sentry issues."
- **No issues** (`obs-sentry-empty`): "No unresolved errors." / "No production errors in the last 24 hours."
- **Issues** (`obs-sentry-issues`): one `Card` per `SentryIssue` (`obs-sentry-issue-<id>`): `title`; subline `culprit || 'unknown'` + relative `lastSeen`; level `Badge` (`danger` when level is `fatal`/`error`, else `warning`); `count├Ś` (tabular); **Open** button ŌåÆ `bridge.openExternal(permalink)`, disabled when no `permalink`.

`SentryIssue` = `{ id, title, culprit, level, count, lastSeen, permalink }`. Connection + issue fetch come from `connections.sentry.*`; the token lives in the OS keychain main-side and is never returned to the renderer.

---

## 5. Runs table ŌĆö `RunsTable` / `RunRow`

Reads `useObsRuns()`. Rows are partitioned in `ObservabilitySeasoned`:
- `realRuns` = `totalBytes > 0 && totalFiles > 0`
- `emptyRuns` = `totalBytes === 0 || totalFiles === 0`

States: loading ŌåÆ "Scanning .refringence-qa/runs."; both empty ŌåÆ `obs-runs-empty` ("No QA runs recorded." + `npm run smoke` / `npm run eval:regression` hint).

Table columns: **Run ┬Ę Started ┬Ę Status ┬Ę Files (right) ┬Ę Size (right)**. Header is uppercase muted. Each `RunRow` (`obs-run-<runId[:24]>`):
- **Run** cell: chevron (expand state) + `humanizeRunLabel(run).title` (never the raw `runId`; raw id is the `title=` hover) + subline `<N> file(s)`.
- **Started**: `label.when` ŌĆö relative for recent (`just now`, `12 min ago`), clock time within 24h, `N days ago` < 7d, else `Mon D`.
- **Status**: `StatusPill` ŌĆö `ok` (success), `failed` (danger), `unknown`ŌåÆ"empty" (secondary). Status resolution: prefer manifest-backed `RunDetail.status` once a row is expanded; otherwise the `deriveStatus` heuristic = `unknown` if `totalFiles===0`, else `failed` if any `artifactKind` matches `/fail|error/i`, else `ok`. An **inferred** label (with explanatory `title=`) is shown when there is no detail yet or `statusSource==='heuristic'`.
- **Files** / **Size**: counts; `formatSize` ŌåÆ B / KB / MB.

Row click toggles expansion ŌåÆ `RunDetailFiles` (lazy `useObsRunDetail`): "Reading artifacts." while loading; "No artifact files in this run." when empty; else one row per file ŌĆö `FileText` icon, monospace `relPath`, `kind`, `formatSize(sizeBytes)`, and an **Open** button ŌåÆ `bridge.openPath('.refringence-qa/runs/<runId>/<relPath>')` (error toast on failure; `stopPropagation` so it doesn't collapse the row).

Empty runs are hidden by default behind a toggle (`obs-toggle-empty-runs`, "Show/Hide empty runs (N)"); when shown they appear under an "Empty runs (no artifacts)" sub-header. If `realRuns` is empty but empty runs exist, the body shows "No completed runs yet. Empty runs are hidden below."

---

## 6. Artifact log ŌĆö `ArtifactLog`

Up to **5** most recent `realRuns` (`realRuns.slice(0,5)`). Empty: "Artifact paths appear here once the first run completes." Each row (`Card`): `FolderOpen` icon, `humanizeRunLabel(run).title` (raw id on hover), subline `<when> : <summarizeArtifactKinds(artifactKinds)>` (lists up to 3 humanized kinds, else "N artifacts"), and **Open folder** (`obs-open-<runId[:24]>`) ŌåÆ `bridge.openPath('.refringence-qa/runs/<runId>')`.

---

## Label humanization (`humanizeRun.ts`)

Raw `runId`s are never rendered. `humanizeRunLabel` strips a trailing ISO-ish timestamp, splits on `-`/`_`, and maps lead tokens via `KIND_MAP` (`smokeŌåÆSmoke run`, `eval/evaluationŌåÆEval run`, `regressionŌåÆRegression run`, `e2eŌåÆEnd-to-end run`, `unitŌåÆUnit tests`, `perfŌåÆPerformance run`, `a11yŌåÆAccessibility run`, `visual/snapshotŌåÆVisual snapshot`, `traceŌåÆTrace capture`, etc.). Phase-style tokens (`q0ŌåÆQ0`, `e7ŌåÆE7`) and acronyms (`qa, ci, cd, e2e, a11y, pr, ui, sbom, drc`) keep case. `summarizeArtifactKinds` drops a `critique-` prefix and Title-cases kinds.

---

## Keyboard / navigation

- `g y` ŌåÆ this panel (global g-chord, `lib/useKeymap.ts`); yields while typing in inputs or while the palette is open.
- Command palette action **"Run eval"** routes here.
- No panel-local shortcuts beyond the global set.

---

## Guided variant (`ObservabilityNewbie`)

`observability-newbie`. Single 820px column. Header subtitle "Quick status of your recent test runs." with a **Switch to Operator view** button (`observability-newbie-switch-power` ŌåÆ `setPersona('seasoned')`).

- One primary **Run a test** button (`observability-newbie-run-test`) ŌåÆ `runner.start({kind:'npm', args:['run','smoke'], label:'Run a test'})`, single-fire (`running` guard), shows "Running." + a "Your test is running. This can take a minute." status line. On completion: `toast.success('Test run finished.')` / `toast.error('Test run failed.')` and invalidates `['obs','runs']` (counters are not invalidated here).
- **Last run** card (`newbie-last-run`): plain-English headline from `deriveStatus` + `countErrors` ("finished cleanly" / "had N problems" / "result is unclear"), `SimpleStatus` badge (Passing/Failed/Unclear), and **See the report folder** / **Open last test report** ŌåÆ `openPath('.refringence-qa/runs/<runId>')`.
- **Recent runs** (`newbie-recent-runs`): up to 3 `realRows` as sentence rows with status badge + **Open**.
- Empty: `newbie-runs-empty` ("No test runs yet" + `npm run smoke` hint).
- No counters, evals card, Sentry feed, runs table, artifact log, or per-run file drill ŌĆö those are Operator-only.

title: Repo, Library and Prompts
guided:
# Repo, Library and Prompts

These three panels are where Console hands you the project itself. **Repo** orients you: what the project is, how to run it, what files it's missing, and how the pieces fit together by role. **Library** lets you read the project's own writing ŌĆö its README, docs, and config files ŌĆö rendered like a book. **Prompts** is your saved-prompt drawer: reusable instructions for an AI, with fill-in-the-blank slots.

You don't have to read them in order, but this is a natural path: orient (Repo) ŌåÆ read (Library) ŌåÆ act (Prompts).

> [!INFO] What a "repo" is
> "Repo" is short for **repository** ŌĆö the folder that holds a software project and its history. When you point Console at a folder, that folder is the repo. Everything in these three panels reads from the folder you have open; nothing is uploaded anywhere.

---

## Repo ŌĆö get your bearings

Open Repo from the sidebar (or press `g r`). At the top you'll see the project's name and a one-line shape: its main language, its size, and how many separate pieces it has. Next to that:

- A **start command** chip (like `npm run dev`) when Console can tell how the project is meant to run.
- A small coloured **status dot**: green "runnable" when there's a clear way to start it, amber "no start command" when Console found code but no obvious launch command, and "reading" while it's still looking.
- A **Set up for Console** button (more on that below).

> [!INFO] What "runnable" means
> A project is *runnable* when it declares a way to start itself ŌĆö usually a "start" or "dev" script in a file like `package.json`. That command is how a developer turns source code into a running program. If Console can't find one, it doesn't mean the project is broken; it just means the launch command isn't written down where Console looks.

### The two tabs

**Structure** (the default) is the orientation view. **Files by size** is a more technical view that ranks files by how many lines they contain ŌĆö useful later, ignore it for now.

### How to run it

If the project is runnable, the Structure tab leads with the start command in a card with a **copy** button. Console can't run a terminal for you, so copying is the action ŌĆö paste it into your own terminal, in the project folder, to start the project.

### The health checks

Below that, three checks tell you whether the project is set up the way a healthy one should be. Each runs automatically and reads only your local files (the dependency check makes read-only lookups to public databases ŌĆö it never installs or changes anything).

**Project setup** lists the housekeeping files a healthy project carries, with a progress bar and a count of how many are in place:

> [!INFO] The files a healthy project keeps
> - **README** ŌĆö the front-page explanation of what the project is and how to run it.
> - **LICENSE** ŌĆö the legal terms for using the code. Without one, others technically can't reuse it.
> - **.gitignore** ŌĆö a list of files Git should *not* track (build output, secrets, junk). Keeps the history clean.
> - **CI** ŌĆö "continuous integration": an automated checker that runs your tests every time code changes. (See the Pipeline panel.)
> - **lockfile** ŌĆö pins the exact versions of every dependency so the project installs the same way on every machine.
> - **tests** ŌĆö automated checks that prove the code still works.
> - **.env.example** ŌĆö a template listing the *names* of the secret values the project needs, with no real values filled in.
>
> Each missing item shows a one-line suggestion and a **Fix** button.

**Dependency health** checks the outside code your project pulls in:

> [!INFO] What dependencies are
> A *dependency* is someone else's code your project relies on. Most projects have dozens. Console checks them two ways: against **OSV.dev** (a public database of known security holes) for *vulnerable* packages, and against the package registry for *outdated* ones (a newer version exists). "Vulnerable" is the urgent one ŌĆö it means a known weakness is sitting in your project. A **Re-check** button forces a fresh look.

**Exposed secrets** scans your source code for keys and tokens that got pasted directly into a file:

> [!INFO] Why a secret in source is dangerous
> A *secret* is a password-like value ŌĆö an API key, an access token ŌĆö that proves your project is allowed to use a paid or private service. If one is written into a normal source file and that file goes into Git history, anyone who sees the code can use your account. The fix is always the same: move it into a `.env` file (which `.gitignore` keeps out of history) and read it from there. Console only ever shows you a blurred preview of what it found, never the full secret. (It deliberately skips your `.env` files ŌĆö secrets are *supposed* to live there.)

### The Fix button

Every finding ŌĆö a missing file, a vulnerable dependency, an exposed key ŌĆö has a **Fix** button. It doesn't change your code. Instead it writes a clear, project-specific instruction you can hand to an AI coding tool. The instruction already knows your stack and your start command, so it's specific, not generic. From the dialog you can:

- **Copy** it to paste anywhere.
- **Save to library** ŌĆö stores it in the Prompts panel under a "Setup" category so you can reuse it.
- **Cursor rules** ŌĆö appends it to a file the Cursor editor reads (only shown if Cursor is installed).
- **Run in Claude Code** ŌĆö sends it straight to the Claude command-line tool (only shown if that tool is installed).

> [!INFO] What "hand it to an AI tool" means
> Console's job here is to *describe* the fix precisely; a separate coding agent (Cursor, Claude Code) does the actual editing. This split is on purpose ŌĆö you stay in control, reviewing the instruction before any code changes.

### Structure ŌĆö the project by role

The rest of the Structure tab groups the project's pieces by **what each one does**, not by name or size. The groups read top-to-bottom in a "start here" order:

- **App shells** ŌĆö the runnable entry points you actually launch.
- **User interface** ŌĆö the screens and components people see.
- **Services** ŌĆö backend or background code.
- **Libraries** ŌĆö shared code other pieces import.
- **Tool wrappers** ŌĆö typed wrappers around bundled command-line tools.
- **Build and scripts** ŌĆö programs that build, package, and release.
- **Tests** ŌĆö automated checks.
- **Docs** ŌĆö written guides and decisions.
- **Other** ŌĆö everything else.

> [!INFO] Why grouping by role helps
> A big project can have twenty folders and no obvious place to start. Grouping by role answers "where does the program actually begin?" (App shells), "where's the screen I'm looking at?" (User interface), and so on. It's a map keyed to *purpose*, which is what you need when you're new to a codebase.

On a wide screen, a side panel called the **Newcomer guide** appears on the right. It's a reading aid built from the real project:

- **Project summary** ŌĆö the project's name, description, languages, license, and run commands.
- **Hot files** ŌĆö the files changed most often in the last 30 days. Frequently-changed files are usually the ones that matter most.
- **Recommended reading order** ŌĆö the files most other files depend on, ranked. Reading these first gives you the backbone of the project fastest.

### Set up for Console

The **Set up for Console** button (top right) does the reverse favour: it writes a short guideline *into your project* telling any AI coding agent how to shape the project so Console reads it cleanly. You preview the exact text first, then choose where it lands ŌĆö **AGENTS.md** or **.cursorrules**. It writes into a clearly-marked managed block, so your own notes in those files are left untouched.

---

## Library ŌĆö read the project like a book

Open Library from the sidebar (or press `g b`). Library finds the project's *written* files ŌĆö READMEs, docs, config, workflow files, the license, the changelog ŌĆö and lets you read them in a clean reading pane instead of a code editor.

> [!INFO] Why a separate reader
> The most valuable explanation of a project is usually already written down in it ŌĆö but it's scattered across folders and mixed in with code. Library gathers just the readable files and renders them like web pages, so you can *read* the project before you try to change it. This is the single fastest way to understand a new codebase.

### Browsing

The left sidebar is a **folder navigator**. It shows one level at a time: the folders and files directly inside wherever you are. Click a folder to drill in; the count next to each folder tells you how many readable files it holds. **Back** climbs up a level, and a breadcrumb trail across the top lets you jump straight back to any parent. You can collapse the whole sidebar to give the reading pane the full width.

### Reading

Click a file and it opens on the right:

- **Markdown** files (`.md`, like a README) render as formatted text ŌĆö headings, lists, links ŌĆö exactly as intended. A small badge shows the word count and an estimated reading time.
- **Config** files (`.json`, `.yml`, `.yaml`, `.toml`) render with syntax colouring so they're easy to scan.
- Anything else shows as plain text.

> [!INFO] What "Markdown" is
> *Markdown* is a plain-text way of writing formatted documents: `# Title` becomes a heading, `- item` becomes a bullet. Almost every project's README is written in it. Library renders it the way it's meant to look, so you read the prose, not the symbols.

Two actions sit in the header of the reading pane:

- **Copy path** ŌĆö copies the file's full location to your clipboard.
- **Open in editor** ŌĆö opens the file in VS Code (or your default editor) if you want to edit it.

> [!INFO] About the 256 KB limit
> Very large files are cut off at 256 KB with a note. This keeps the reader fast ŌĆö documentation files are almost always far smaller. If you hit the limit, **Open in editor** shows you the whole thing.

---

## Prompts ŌĆö your reusable instructions

Open Prompts from the sidebar. A **prompt** is just a piece of text you give to an AI: a question, a task, an instruction. The Prompts panel is a place to save the ones you use often, with *blanks* you fill in each time.

> [!INFO] Why save prompts at all
> When you find a wording that gets good results from an AI ŌĆö say, "explain this error in plain words" ŌĆö you don't want to retype and re-perfect it every time. A saved prompt with a blank for the error lets you reuse the good wording and just drop in today's specifics. Prompts are stored **per project**, in a `.refringence-console` folder inside the project, so each project keeps its own set.

### The layout

The left column lists your saved prompts with a search box and filter chips: **Favorites**, plus a chip per category and per tag. The right side is where you fill in and use the selected prompt.

### Filling a prompt

A prompt's text can contain **blanks** written as `{{name}}`. When you select a prompt, Console shows one input per blank and a **live preview** of the finished text that updates as you type. Blanks come in three kinds:

- **Text** ŌĆö a single line.
- **Multiline** ŌĆö a box that grows, for pasting something long like an error message.
- **Select** ŌĆö a dropdown with fixed choices.

> [!INFO] Why blanks instead of editing the text directly
> The blanks keep the *good part* of the prompt fixed and let you change only the specifics. The preview shows you the exact text that will be sent, so there's no guessing. Nothing is sent automatically ŌĆö you always see the final result first.

### Using a prompt

Once it's filled, you have two plain actions:

- **Copy** ŌĆö puts the finished text on your clipboard to paste anywhere.
- **Send to your AI** ŌĆö drops the finished text into Console's built-in AI assistant (it opens with the text waiting in the box). It does *not* send by itself ŌĆö you read it, then press send. That deliberate pause is so you're never surprised by what goes out.

In Guided mode the panel walks you through this as three calm steps ŌĆö **Pick a prompt ŌåÆ Fill the blanks ŌåÆ Copy or send** ŌĆö with a Back/Next bar and a line telling you what comes next.

### Making your own

**New prompt** (top right) opens an editor. Type your text, and anywhere you write `{{something}}` Console treats it as a blank you can then describe (give it a label, choose its kind, set options for a dropdown, set a default). Give the prompt a title, a category, and optional tags, and save. You can edit, favourite, or delete any prompt later.

### Skills

The **Skills** button opens a small catalogue of curated *agent skills* ŌĆö reusable abilities you can install into the project for a coding agent (Claude Code or Codex). Installing one writes a file inside the project's own `.claude/skills` or `.codex/skills` folder; nothing on your wider system is touched.

> [!INFO] What an "agent skill" is
> A *skill* is a packaged instruction set that teaches an AI coding agent how to do a specific job well. Installing one into your project means that, next time you use that agent here, it already knows the technique ŌĆö you don't have to explain it each time.

---

## How the three fit together

A common loop: **Repo** flags something ("missing tests", "a vulnerable dependency"), its **Fix** button turns that into a precise instruction and can **Save to library** ŌĆö which is the **Prompts** panel ŌĆö and meanwhile **Library** lets you read the project's own docs to understand what you're changing before you change it. Orient, read, act.
