On save, only currently-referenced names are emitted (stale vars dropped). Save disabled until title and body are non-empty. **Create prompt** / **Save changes** via `useCreate/UpdatePrompt`.

### Skills (`SkillsButton` ŌåÆ `SkillsDialog`, `skills-dialog`)

Curated agent-skill catalogue (`bridge.skills.list`, cached `Infinity`). Tool toggle **Claude Code** (`claude`) / **Codex** (`codex`) (`skills-tool-<t>`). Installed set per `(root, tool)` from `bridge.skills.installed`. Each skill (`skill-<id>`): name, description, tag badges; **Install** (`skill-install-<id>`) ŌåÆ `bridge.skills.install(root, id, tool)` writes `<.claude|.codex>/skills/<id>/SKILL.md` **inside the project** (nothing global), toasts the path, invalidates the installed query; already-installed shows an "installed" badge. Requires an open project.

### Cross-panel integration

Repo's `FixButton` "Save to library" lands prompts here under category **Setup** with tags `['setup', <kind>]`. "Send to AI" (both detail variants) is the only path from a prompt into the `/ai` assistant, and it always prefills-then-waits rather than auto-firing.

title: AI Assistant
guided:
# The AI Assistant

Console has a chat assistant built in. You ask it questions in plain language; it answers, and when you have a project open, it can actually read your code before it replies. This page explains what it is, where to find it, how to turn it on, what it can and cannot do, and the one idea that makes Console's AI trustworthy: it answers on top of facts Console already measured, not instead of them.

> [!INFO] What "an LLM" is
> The assistant is powered by a *large language model* (LLM) - a program trained to predict text. You give it a prompt (your question plus some context), and it produces a reply one piece at a time. It is good at explaining code, drafting text, and summarising. It is not a database and it does not "know" your project until you give it access. That last part matters, and Console handles it for you.

## Where the assistant lives

There are two doors to the same assistant:

- **The chat dock** - a panel that slides in on the right-hand side of the window. Open it with the **Assistant** button in the top bar, or with the **Open assistant** action in the command palette (press `Ctrl+K`). When it opens, Console widens the window a little to make room.
- **The assistant page** (`/ai`). In Guided mode this is a four-step wizard that walks you from "connect a provider" to "read the answer."

Both use the same engine underneath. The dock is for quick questions while you work; the wizard is for your very first time.

## Bring your own key

Console does not sell you AI access and does not have a Console account. Instead you connect your own AI provider. This is called **bring-your-own-key**.

> [!INFO] What "a provider" and "an API key" are
> A *provider* is a company that runs LLMs you can call over the internet - or a program that runs one on your own machine. An *API key* is a long secret password that provider gives you so their servers know the requests are yours (and bill you, if the provider charges). You paste that key into Console once; from then on Console uses it to talk to the provider on your behalf.

Console supports five providers:

| Provider | Needs a key? | Where it runs |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Anthropic** (Claude) | Yes | The provider's cloud |
| **OpenAI** (GPT) | Yes | The provider's cloud |
| **Google** (Gemini) | Yes | The provider's cloud |
| **Kimi** | Yes | The provider's cloud |
| **Ollama** | **No** | Your own computer |

> [!INFO] Cloud vs. on-device
> A *cloud* model runs on the provider's servers: you send your question over the internet, they run it and send the answer back. It is usually the smartest option, and it costs money per use. **Ollama** is different - it runs a model *on your own machine*. Nothing you type leaves your computer, and it is free, but it needs a capable computer and the models are generally smaller. If you have Ollama running, Console gives you a working chat with no key at all.

### How to connect (the four-step wizard)

Open the assistant page in Guided mode and you get a stepper:

1. **Connect your AI** - pick a provider. For a cloud provider, paste your key into the field and press **Save key**. Console checks the key against the provider before saving it, so a typo is caught immediately. For Ollama, press **Detect Ollama**; if it is running, you will see how many local models you have.
2. **What to do** - type what you want in plain words, or start from one of your saved prompt templates.
3. **Review** - Console shows you the exact text that will be sent. Step back if you want to change it.
4. **Send** - the answer streams in, word by word. You can **Ask again** or **Stop**.

You only do this setup once. After that, just open the dock and type.

## Where your keys are stored

This is worth understanding, because it is the question people worry about most.

> [!INFO] Why this is a security decision
> An API key is a live secret - anyone who has it can spend your money or read on your behalf. So *where* it is kept, and *who* can read it back, is the whole game. A careless app stores keys in plain text where any script can grab them. Console does not.

When you save a key, here is what happens:

- The key is handed to the **main process** (the trusted, behind-the-scenes part of the Console app), never kept in the visible web page.
- It is **encrypted** using your operating system's own secure storage - the Windows Credential Manager, the macOS Keychain, or the Linux secret service - and written to a file only your user account can open.
- The key is **never shown back to you**, **never written to a log**, and **never sent to the AI**. Console reads it back only in that trusted main process, only to make the actual call to the provider.
- The visible part of the app only ever learns one thing about your key: a yes/no **"Connected"** badge. The secret itself never travels back into the page.

So if you ever want to confirm a key is set, you look for the **Connected** badge in Settings - you will never see the key again, and that is by design. To remove a key, press **Clear**; the encrypted file is deleted.

> [!INFO] What "the model picker" shows you
> Once a key is connected, the **model picker** (top of the chat) lists the specific models you can use - grouped by provider, each with its *context window* (how much text it can consider at once, like "200k") and a one-line note on what it is good for. Console only shows models your stored keys can actually reach. If you have only an OpenAI key, you will not see Claude or Gemini in the list, so you can never pick a model that would fail. A model named like "Claude Sonnet 4.5" or "GPT-4" is just one specific model offered by that provider.

## What the assistant *can* do

When you have a project open in Console, the assistant is not guessing about your code - it can read it. Console gives the model three **read-only tools**:

- **List files** - see what files and folders are in a directory.
- **Read file** - open a specific file and read its contents (up to about 400 lines at a time).
- **Search code** - find a piece of text across the whole project.

> [!INFO] What "tools" mean here, and what you'll see
> A modern assistant can do more than reply with text - it can *call tools*: small actions the app exposes to it. When the assistant decides it needs to read `package.json` before answering, it calls the **read file** tool, and you see a little card in the chat showing the tool name and the file, with a spinner that turns into a checkmark when it finishes. This is the assistant *checking the real code* instead of making something up. Watching those cards is how you can tell its answer is grounded in your actual project.

Console also quietly tells the assistant which project you have open - its name, its description, the scripts it can run, and its top-level folders - so its very first answer already has its bearings.

Good things to ask:

- "Explain what this repo does."
- "Where is the login handled in this code?"
- "Draft a release note from the latest commits."
- "Why might this build be failing?"

## What the assistant *cannot* do

Knowing the limits keeps you out of trouble:

- **It cannot change your files.** The tools are *read-only*. The assistant can look but never edit, delete, or run anything in your project. (If you want AI that edits code, Console can hand a prompt off to a separate coding tool like Cursor or Claude Code - but that is a deliberate, separate action you take, not something the chat does on its own.)
- **It cannot read outside your project.** The file tools are locked to the folder you opened. The assistant cannot wander up into the rest of your disk.
- **It does not remember past conversations.** Right now the chat keeps **one conversation in memory** while the panel is open. Press **New chat** (or close the app) and the history is gone - there is no saved session yet. So if a thread matters, copy the useful part out.
- **It never sends without you.** When you send a prompt from the Prompts library to the assistant, it lands in the message box ready for you to read - it is *not* fired automatically. You always press Send.
- **It cannot see your keys** - the secret never reaches the model, only your messages and whatever code it reads with the tools do.

## The important idea: AI sits *on top of* facts

This is the heart of how Console is designed, and it is different from a plain chatbot.

> [!INFO] What "deterministic" means
> *Deterministic* means "the same input always gives the same output, with no guessing." When Console counts your lines of code, lists your dependencies, scans for exposed secrets, reads your CI status, or maps which package imports which - those are deterministic measurements. They are facts, repeatable and exact. An LLM is the opposite: ask it twice and you may get two different phrasings, and it can be confidently wrong.

Console measures first and lets AI talk second:

- The **Report**, **Repo**, **Architecture**, **Pipeline**, and **Observability** panels are built from real measurements of your project - no AI required. The numbers are true whether or not you ever connect a model.
- AI is then layered *over* those facts as an optional enrichment: a plain-English summary in the Report, a semantic map in Architecture, an explanation in the chat. The AI explains and narrates; the deterministic scan supplies the ground truth.
- And when you ask the chat about your project, it does not invent answers - it uses the read tools to go look at the real files first.

> [!INFO] Why this order matters to you as a beginner
> If a tool *only* had a chatbot, you could never be sure whether an answer was measured or imagined. Console flips it: trust the panels' numbers as facts, and treat the AI as a knowledgeable colleague who read those same files and is explaining them to you. When the AI says something surprising, you can always go check it against the deterministic panel that measured it. That habit - verify the story against the facts - is exactly how experienced engineers work.

## If you see "Connect an AI provider"

That empty state means no model is available yet - either no key is connected, or Ollama is not running. Press **Open Settings**, add a provider key (or start Ollama), and come back. The chat lights up as soon as one usable model exists.

## Quick recap

- Open it from the **Assistant** button in the top bar, or `Ctrl+K` ŌåÆ Open assistant.
- Connect your own provider once; the key is **encrypted in your OS keychain** and never shown back.
- With a project open, the assistant **reads your real code** (read-only) before answering, and shows you the tool cards while it does.
- It does **not** edit files, remember old chats, or send anything without you pressing Send.
- Treat the panels' measurements as facts and the AI as the colleague who explains them.
operator:
# AI Assistant

Multi-provider chat assistant, bring-your-own-key, with read-only project grounding. Same backend, two front doors: the right-hand **chat dock** (`ConsoleChatDock` ŌåÆ `ChatPanel embedded`) and the `/ai` route (`AiPanel` dispatches on persona: Operator ŌåÆ `ChatPanel`, Guided ŌåÆ `AiWizard`). Keys are held by the Electron main process; only booleans, redacted status, and model metadata cross the bridge.

## Surfaces & entry points

| Surface | Open via | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Chat dock (360px right column) | TopBar **Assistant** toggle (`topbar-ai`) ┬Ę palette **Open assistant** | `consoleLayout.chatOpen` owns state; opening also calls `bridge.window.growForDock(360)`. Header "Assistant" + close (`console-chat-dock-close`). Embeds `<ChatPanel embedded/>`. |
| `/ai` Operator | direct nav ┬Ę prefill handoff from Prompts | `ChatPanel` (non-embedded): full `PanelHeader` "Assistant" / "Chat with your AI provider". |
| `/ai` Guided | persona = Guided | `AiWizard`: 4-step stepper (Connect ŌåÆ Describe ŌåÆ Review ŌåÆ Send). |

Embedded vs. full: `embedded` drops the PanelHeader title row and renders model picker + New-chat in an 11px controls strip so the dock shows one title, not two.

## Providers

`AiProviderId = 'openai' | 'anthropic' | 'google' | 'ollama' | 'kimi'`. Registry display order (`ai/registry.ts ORDER`): `anthropic, openai, google, kimi, ollama` (cloud defaults first, local last).

| Provider | Keyed | Credential check | Models |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Anthropic | yes | `hasKey('anthropic')` | static: `claude-sonnet-4-5` (200k), `claude-opus-4-1` (200k), `claude-haiku-4-5` (200k) |
| OpenAI | yes | `hasKey('openai')` | static catalogue |
| Google | yes | `hasKey('google')` | static catalogue |
| Kimi | yes | `hasKey('kimi')` | static catalogue |
| Ollama | **no** | live probe (`localhost:11434`) | live local tags |

`KEYED_PROVIDERS = ['openai','anthropic','google','kimi']`. Ollama is local + keyless and is not part of the keystore surface; a running Ollama yields a usable chat with no key.

## Model picker (`ModelPicker.tsx`, `ai-model-picker`)

Dropdown grouped by provider (`groupModelsByProvider`, order = first model seen per provider). Per item: label + context window rendered as `Math.round(context/1000)k` (e.g. "200k") + one-line `description`. Trigger label = current model label, truncated 180px; disabled when 0 models.

- **Source of truth = available models, not raw keys.** `useAiModels` ŌåÆ `bridge.ai.availableModels()` ŌåÆ `registry.listAvailableModels()`, which contributes only providers passing `hasCredentials()`, each yielding its `listAvailableModels?.()` (catalogue Ōł® key-reachable) or `listModels()`, or live local tags. A user with only an OpenAI key never sees Claude/Gemini entries that would fail on send.
- `bridge.ai.listModels()` / `registry.listAllModels()` = full catalogue (static cloud lists + live Ollama), used internally to resolve a model id ŌåÆ provider; not what the picker shows.
- `ChatPanel` defaults `modelId` to `list[0].id` once models arrive and re-pins to a valid selection if the list changes.

## Key storage (where keys live)

Main process only. `ai/keystore.ts`:

- One encrypted file per provider at `<userData>/ai-keys/<id>.key` via Electron `safeStorage` (OS keychain ŌĆö Windows DPAPI / macOS Keychain / Linux secret service). `chmod 0o600` best-effort (no-op on Windows ACLs).
- `setKey` returns `false` (never throws) when `safeStorage.isEncryptionAvailable()` is false ŌĆö surfaces "Secure storage is unavailableŌĆ” key was not saved" rather than writing plaintext.
- `getKey` is **main-process internal only** ŌĆö never returned over IPC. Raw key never logged.
- The same encrypted store also holds non-AI service keys by string id (e.g. `langsmith`); the typed `KEYED_PROVIDERS` surface stays AI-only.

IPC (`ipc/ai.ts`):

- `console:ai.setKey` ŌĆö **validate-before-store**: `validateKey(id, key)` probes the provider; on `valid:false` returns `{ok:false, valid:false, error}` and **never lands the key on disk**. On success encrypts + stores. Returns `{ok, valid?, error?}` ŌĆö never the key.
- `console:ai.getKeyStatus` ŌåÆ `keyStatus()` = `Record<provider, boolean>` (`hasKey` per `KEYED_PROVIDERS`).
- `console:ai.clearKey` ŌĆö deletes the file; total (never throws).

Settings ŌåÆ AI write surface: masked write-only `<input>` per `CLOUD_PROVIDERS` (OpenAI `sk-ŌĆ”`, Anthropic `sk-ant-ŌĆ”`, Google `AIzaŌĆ”`), Set/Replace/Clear, Connected badge driven by `useAiKeyStatus`. **Legacy migration** (`useAiKeyMigration`, one-shot, flag `refringence-console-ai-keys-migrated`): moves localStorage `refringence-console-anthropic-key` into the keystore via `bridge.ai.setKey('anthropic',ŌĆ”)`, then removes the legacy key.

## Tier (separate from the chat backend)

Settings ŌåÆ AI **Tier** radios write localStorage `refringence-console-ai-tier`: `on-device` (default ŌĆö all AI local via Ollama, no data leaves machine) / `on-device-optional-cloud` / `cloud-first`. This drives the **on-device capability layer** (`lib/ai/registry.ts`: Transformers.js BGE-small embeddings in a worker; rule-based `suggestNext`/`explain`/`categorize`; cloud refinement stubbed "T4 not wired"), collapsed internally to `on-device-only | allow-cloud`. **The chat Assistant does not consult the tier** ŌĆö `ChatPanel` gates purely on `availableModels` and routes by model id through the main-process providers regardless of tier.

## Streaming chat (`lib/ai/useAiChat.ts`)

`bridge.ai.chat.start({ model, messages, system?, projectRoot? })` returns `{chatId}` synchronously; renderer subscribes once to `onDelta / onToolCall / onToolResult / onDone / onError`, filtering by `activeChatId`. Surface: `{ messages, streaming, error, send, stop, reset }`.

- **History across turns = role + text only**; within-turn tool detail (`ChatToolUse`) stays local to the message.
- `stop` ŌåÆ `bridge.ai.chat.cancel(chatId)` (main aborts via per-chat `AbortController`).
- `reset` (**New chat**, `ai-new-chat`) cancels any live chat + clears messages.
- One conversation in component state. **No session persistence.**
- Main side (`ipc/ai.ts`): resolves modelŌåÆprovider via `listAllModels`; unknown model ŌåÆ error+done. One `AbortController` per chat in a `chats` map; every handler total (never throws across IPC).

## Project grounding (tools + context)

Passed `projectRoot` (= `useActiveProject().project.path`) ŌćÆ assistant gets read-only tools + an injected context block. `ipc/ai.ts` builds them only when `projectRoot` is a non-empty string; failure falls back to plain chat. System prompt = `buildProjectContext(root)` prepended to any caller `system`.

**Tools** (`ai/tools.ts`, JSON-schema inputs, all scoped to root with `inside()` traversal guard ŌĆö cannot read outside the project):

| Tool | Input | Behaviour |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `list_files` | `dir` ("" = root) | dir listing, hides dotfiles (except `.github`) + SKIP set (`node_modules`, `.git`, `dist`, `build`, `.next`, `out`, `.cache`, `.venv`, `__pycache__`, `.refringence-qa`, `.refringence-console`), sorted, capped 200 |
| `read_file` | `path` | text file, **>200KB rejected**, returns Ōēż400 lines (then "ŌĆ” N more lines") |
| `search_code` | `query` | literal `git grep -F` (respects .gitignore, 10s/1MB cap); falls back to bounded JS walk (Ōēż60 hits, Ōēż3000 files scanned) on known source extensions |

**Context block** (`buildProjectContext`): "You are Console's assistantŌĆ”", project root, a `package.json` shape line (name/description/script names) or "No package.jsonŌĆ”", top-level non-dot/non-SKIP folders (Ōēż20), and an instruction to use the tools to ground answers before responding. Tool cards in the transcript (`ToolCard`) icon by name: `read_file`ŌåÆFileText, `list_files`ŌåÆFolderOpen, `search_code`ŌåÆSearch, else Wrench; show the `path`/`dir`/`query` arg + spinnerŌåÆcheck.

## Transcript & composer

- **Transcript** (`ChatMessages.tsx`, `ai-transcript`): user = right-aligned card; assistant = markdown (`marked`, `{gfm:true, breaks:true}`) with accent-dot gutter; tool cards above text. Stick-to-bottom (re-pins when within 64px of bottom), "Scroll to latest" pill when scrolled away, "Thinking" indicator (pulsing accent dot) while streaming with no assistant turn yet.
- **Composer** (`ChatComposer.tsx`, `ai-composer`): auto-grow textarea (min 40px, max 160px). **Enter sends, Shift+Enter newline.** Send (`ai-send`) disabled until non-empty + enabled; while streaming shows Stop (`ai-stop`). `disabled` mirrors `!modelId`.
- Errors render a `role="alert"` danger banner above the composer.
- **Empty states**: no models ŌåÆ `EmptyState` "Connect an AI provider" ŌåÆ **Open Settings** (`ai-empty-settings-link`); models present but no messages ŌåÆ "Ask anything".

## Prefill handoff (`lib/ai/prefill.ts`)

Prompts panel **Send to AI** stashes text in `sessionStorage` (`refringence-console-ai-prefill`), navigates to `/ai`; `ChatPanel` reads-and-clears it once into the composer `initialText`. **Never auto-fires** ŌĆö the user reviews and presses Send.

## Guided wizard (`AiWizard.tsx`, `ai-wizard`)

Steps: **Connect** (cloud key field per `openai`/`anthropic`, or Ollama detect; Next gated on `connected`) ŌåÆ **Describe** (free text or pick a saved prompt template, defaults interpolated; Next gated on non-empty) ŌåÆ **Review** (exact send text) ŌåÆ **Send** (auto-fires `chat.send(task)` once on entering step 3; **Stop** / **Ask again**). Same `useAiChat` backend, no `projectRoot` passed in the wizard path.

## Layering AI over deterministic facts (design contract)

- **Deterministic-first.** Report (`intel.profile`), Repo, Architecture (`arch.graph`), Pipeline, Observability, dependency/secrets/hygiene scans are exact measurements with no model call. They hold whether or not a provider is connected.
- **AI as optional enrichment over those facts**, never the source of truth: Report `onEnrich` (`intel.enrich`) adds tagline/narrative/one-liners on top of the deterministic profile; Architecture **Systems view** generates a semantic map but validates paths against the real repo before they open code; the chat grounds via read tools rather than recall.
- **Boundaries enforced in code**: read-only tools (no write/edit/run), root-scoped traversal guard, history limited to role+text, prefill never auto-sends, keys never reach the model or the renderer. Overview explicitly forbids a chatbot widget (deterministic `WhatsNextTile` instead).

## Bridge surface (`ai.*`)

`providers()` ┬Ę `listModels()` ┬Ę `availableModels()` ┬Ę `getKeyStatus()` ┬Ę `setKey(id,key)` ┬Ę `clearKey(id)` ┬Ę `chat.start(opts)` ┬Ę `chat.cancel(chatId)` ┬Ę `chat.onDelta/onToolCall/onToolResult/onDone/onError`. Browser-dev fallback: all stubs throw; event subscriptions are no-op unsubscribers.

## Limits / not-yet-wired

- No session persistence (one in-memory conversation; New chat / reload discards).
- Chat path ignores Tier; Tier only drives the on-device registry layer (cloud refinement stubbed).
- Tools are read-only ŌĆö code edits route out via `devhandoff.*` (Run in Claude Code / Open in Cursor), a separate explicit action, not the chat.
- `safeStorage` unavailable ŌćÆ keys cannot be saved (honest failure, no plaintext fallback).

title: Settings and shortcuts
guided:
# Settings and shortcuts

This page covers the two ways you control Console: the **Settings** screen (how Console looks and behaves, where AI runs, what services it talks to) and the **keyboard shortcuts** that let you move around without touching the mouse.

You do not have to learn any of this to use Console. But spending five minutes here will make the rest of the app feel faster and more like *yours*.

> [!INFO] **What "settings" actually are.** Almost every setting on this page is just a small piece of remembered preference. Console writes your choices into a tiny local store on your machine (the browser's `localStorage`, plus an encrypted vault for secrets) so they survive a restart. Nothing here is sent to a server. Changing a setting only changes how *your* copy of Console behaves.

---

## Opening Settings

There are three ways to get there:

1. Click **Settings** at the bottom of the left sidebar.
2. Press **`g`** then **`,`** (the comma key) anywhere in the app.
3. Press **`Ctrl + K`**, type "settings", and press **Enter**.

In **Guided mode** (where you probably are if you're reading this), Settings is deliberately short: just **Theme**, **AI mode**, and **About**. Everything else is one click away behind **See full settings**, which switches you to Operator mode and reveals the complete panel. You can switch back any time.

---

## Display: making Console look right

### Theme ŌĆö light, dark, or match your system

Pick how Console looks. **Light** is bright, **Dark** is gentler on the eyes at night, and **Match my system** follows whatever your operating system is set to (so Console goes dark when Windows goes dark in the evening, if you've set that up).

There is also a quick toggle in the top bar ŌĆö the small **sun/moon button** in the top-right corner flips between light and dark instantly. The top-bar button only flips lightŌåödark; the **Match my system** option lives only here in Settings.

> [!INFO] **Why "match my system" is usually the right pick.** Your OS already knows whether it's day or night for you (many systems switch automatically). "Match my system" means you set it up once and never think about it again. This pattern ŌĆö an app deferring to a system-wide preference instead of inventing its own ŌĆö is good engineering practice: fewer places to configure, fewer surprises.

### AI mode ŌĆö where the AI runs

This is the most important privacy choice in Console. It decides **where your code goes when you ask the AI a question.** Three options:

- **On-device only** ŌĆö every AI request runs on your own laptop using a local AI runtime (Ollama). Nothing about your code ever leaves the machine. This is the default and the most private.
- **On-device with cloud help** ŌĆö runs locally by default, but asks before sending anything to a cloud provider for harder questions.
- **Cloud first** ŌĆö uses a cloud AI provider for every request. Faster and smarter, but it uses your API key and sends your prompt to that provider.

> [!INFO] **"On-device" vs "cloud" ŌĆö what's the difference?** An AI model is a large program that has to run *somewhere*. "On-device" means that program runs on your computer's own processor ŌĆö private, free, but limited by your hardware. "Cloud" means your question is sent over the internet to a company's servers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google) which run a much bigger model and send the answer back. The trade-off is always the same: **privacy and cost vs. capability.** Console lets you choose where you sit on that line, per machine.

### About

Just shows what you're running: the app name (**Console**) and the version (**v0.1**). Useful when reporting a problem so you can say which version you're on.

### See full settings

The link at the bottom of the About card flips you into **Operator mode**, which unlocks the full Settings screen described below (density, AI keys, sidebar layout, project folder, services). Don't worry ŌĆö switching modes doesn't lose anything; it just shows more controls. You can flip back with the **mode pill** in the top bar.

---

## The full Settings screen (Operator mode)

When you switch to the full view, Settings grows a left-hand menu with five sections: **Display, AI, Sidebar, Project, Services.** Here's each one.

### Display (full)

Same **Theme** as above, plus two more controls:

- **Mode** ŌĆö switch between **Guided** (this teaching view, with explainers and roomier spacing) and **Operator** (a dense, no-explanations cockpit for experienced engineers). This is the same switch as the **mode pill** in the top bar.
- **Density** ŌĆö **Compact** packs more onto the screen; **Roomy** adds breathing room. Guided mode defaults to Roomy; Operator defaults to Compact. Purely cosmetic ŌĆö it changes spacing, not what's shown.

### AI (full)

This is where you set up cloud AI if you chose a cloud tier. Three parts:

**1. Tier** ŌĆö the same three choices as "AI mode" above (On-device only / On-device with optional cloud / Cloud-first), just with more technical wording.

**2. Provider keys** ŌĆö paste an API key for **OpenAI**, **Anthropic**, or **Google** to let Console use that provider's models.

> [!INFO] **What is an "API key"?** When you sign up with an AI provider, they give you a long secret string ŌĆö your API key ŌĆö that identifies your account and bills you for usage. It's like a password for a robot: anything that has your key can spend your money, so it must be kept secret. Console handles this carefully: when you paste a key, it goes straight to the part of Console that runs on your machine (the "main process") and is stored in your operating system's **encrypted keychain**. The key is *never shown back to you* and *never sent to the AI*. The screen only ever tells you "Connected" or "Not connected" ŌĆö a yes/no, never the secret itself. This is the correct way to handle secrets, and it's worth recognising so you can demand it from every tool you use.

Each provider row has a masked input (the characters show as dots), a **Set** button (which becomes **Replace** once a key is saved), and a **Clear** button to remove it. When you save a key, Console quickly checks it's valid before storing it.

**3. Ollama** ŌĆö for the on-device tiers. Press **Detect Ollama** and Console checks whether the local AI runtime is installed and running (on `localhost:11434`). If found, it shows the version and how many models you have installed. If not, it links you to **ollama.com** to install it.

> [!INFO] **What is Ollama?** Ollama is a free program that runs open AI models directly on your computer. Console talks to it on a local network address (`localhost:11434` ŌĆö `localhost` always means "this same machine," and `11434` is the port number Ollama listens on). Because it's local, using it costs nothing and your code never leaves your laptop ŌĆö which is exactly why it's the default for the privacy-first tiers.

**4. Eval tools ŌĆö LangSmith.** Optional. Paste a LangSmith key (starts with `lsv2_`) and Console can run a small test of your AI setup and record the results to your LangSmith account. Once connected, a **Run eval** button appears.

> [!INFO] **What is an "eval"?** An eval (short for *evaluation*) is a test for AI. Normal software tests check exact outputs ŌĆö 2 + 2 must equal 4. But AI answers vary, so you can't check them exactly; instead you run the AI over a set of example questions and *score* how good the answers are. **LangSmith** is a service that records each AI run so you can see what was asked, what came back, and how it scored. Connecting it here lets Console run one of these scored tests for you. You'll meet evals again in the Observability panel.

### Sidebar ŌĆö rearranging the left menu

This lets you make the left sidebar yours. Drag any row by its handle to **reorder** it, and click the **eye icon** to **hide** a panel you never use.

Three panels ŌĆö **Overview, Release, and Pipeline** ŌĆö show a small **lock** instead of an eye. These are core to shipping, so Console keeps them always visible.

> [!INFO] **Hidden doesn't mean gone.** Hiding a panel only removes it from the sidebar to reduce clutter. Every panel is *always* reachable from the command palette (`Ctrl + K`) ŌĆö so you can hide things aggressively and still get to them in two keystrokes. This is a common UI idea: keep the always-visible surface small, but let power-users reach anything instantly.

**Reset to default** puts the sidebar back the way it started.

### Project ŌĆö the folder Console is reading

Console always works on **one active project folder** at a time. This section shows that folder's path and lets you **Pick a folder** / **Change folder** / **Clear** it.

> [!INFO] **What "the active project" means.** Console doesn't have a database of your code; it reads a real folder on your disk live. The "active project" is simply *which folder* it's pointing at right now. Everything you see ŌĆö the architecture map, the health score, the file list ŌĆö is computed by reading that folder. Change the folder and the whole app re-reads. (You can also open a *different* project in a brand-new window from the project chip in the top bar, so you can have several open at once.)

### Services ŌĆö connecting GitHub, Vercel, and more

This section is a shortcut. The actual connecting happens in the dedicated **Services** panel ŌĆö click **Open Services** to go there. Because connecting accounts is a privacy-sensitive thing, it's worth understanding what happens:

- **GitHub** ŌĆö Console reuses the login you already have through the GitHub command-line tool (`gh`). No password or token to paste.
- **Vercel** ŌĆö paste a personal token (from *vercel.com/account/tokens*); Console can then deploy your project. The token is encrypted on your machine.
- **Sentry** ŌĆö paste a token and your organisation name to pull live production errors into the app.
- **Slack** ŌĆö paste a bot token (starts with `xoxb-`) to pull issue reports from your Slack channels.

> [!INFO] **How Console keeps your tokens safe.** A connection token is another secret ŌĆö like an API key, it lets Console act on your behalf. Every token you paste is encrypted and stored in your operating system's secure keychain, and like AI keys, **it is never sent back to the screen and never sent to the AI.** Console only ever reports a yes/no "Connected" status. There's also a neat trick on the Services panel: it can scan your project's `.env` file for service *names* (never the secret values) and offer to connect them; when you tap Link, the value is read just long enough to test the connection, then dropped.

---

## Keyboard shortcuts

Console is built so you can fly through it with the keyboard. You don't need these ŌĆö every shortcut has a button somewhere ŌĆö but they're worth learning because they make the app feel instant.

> [!INFO] **Why "g then a letter"?** Console borrows a navigation style from professional tools like Linear and Gmail: you press **`g`** (think "**go**") to start, then a second letter to choose a destination ŌĆö `g o` = "go to Overview." It's called a **chord** (two keys pressed in sequence). The advantage over a single hotkey is that you don't run out of letters: every panel gets a memorable two-key combo without clashing with normal typing.

### Jump to any panel ŌĆö press `g`, then:

| Press | Goes to |
|-------|---------|
| `g` `o` | Overview |
| `g` `w` | Workboard |
| `g` `r` | Repo |
| `g` `a` | Architecture |
| `g` `p` | Pipeline |
| `g` `s` | Services |
| `g` `b` | Library |
| `g` `l` | Release |
| `g` `y` | Observability |
| `g` `c` | Activity |
| `g` `t` | Tutorials |
| `g` `d` | Docs |
| `g` `,` | Settings |

If you press `g` and then change your mind, just wait ŌĆö the chord forgets itself after a moment and nothing happens.

### The two shortcuts to actually remember

| Press | Does |
|-------|------|
| **`Ctrl + K`** | Opens the **command palette** ŌĆö a search box for *everything*: jump to a panel, run an action (toggle theme, connect a service, deploy, run an eval), or open a doc. If you only learn one shortcut, learn this. |
| **`?`** | Opens the **keyboard shortcuts help** ŌĆö a pop-up listing every chord, so you never have to memorise this page. |

> [!INFO] **What a "command palette" is, and why every serious app has one.** A command palette is one search box that can run any command in the app, found by typing what you want. It exists because menus don't scale ŌĆö once an app has dozens of features, hunting through nested menus is slow. Typing "deploy" and pressing Enter is faster than remembering which panel the deploy button lives in. Console's palette even re-ranks results by *meaning*, not just spelling, so a rough guess usually finds the right thing.

### In the AI assistant chat

| Press | Does |
|-------|------|
| **`Enter`** | Sends your message |
| **`Shift + Enter`** | Adds a new line *without* sending (for writing a longer, multi-line question) |

### When shortcuts politely step aside

The `g`-chords and `?` **don't fire while you're typing** in a text box, or while the command palette is open. That's on purpose ŌĆö you should be able to type the letter "g" into a search field or a chat message without Console teleporting you somewhere. `Ctrl + K` always works, because no one types `Ctrl + K` by accident.

---

## A quick tour of the layout presets

One more thing worth knowing, reachable from the command palette (`Ctrl + K` ŌåÆ type "layout"): Console has three **layout presets** that rearrange the whole window in one click.

- **Standard** ŌĆö full sidebar, no chat. The calm default.
- **Focus** ŌĆö sidebar shrinks to a thin icon rail so your content fills the screen.
- **Chat** ŌĆö icon rail plus the AI assistant docked on the right, for working alongside the AI.

> [!INFO] **Why presets instead of fiddly toggles.** Rather than make you adjust the sidebar and the chat dock separately every time, presets bundle "the way I like to work right now" into one choice. It's the same idea as a "reading mode" in a browser: a named bundle of settings you switch between, instead of ten individual knobs. You can still toggle the rail or the chat individually (`Ctrl + K` ŌåÆ "toggle sidebar rail" / "open assistant") when you want fine control.

# Settings and shortcuts

Complete reference for the Settings panel (every section, field, and storage key) and the global keyboard map. Settings branches on persona at the top: Guided renders a trimmed three-card view (`SettingsNewbie`); Operator renders the full five-section panel (`SettingsSeasoned`). The mode pill in the top bar and the **Mode** field in Display both write the same persona key ŌĆö switching is non-destructive.

Two surfaces also live outside the Settings panel and are documented here for completeness: the **theme toggle** in the top bar, and **service connections** (which the Settings ŌåÆ Services section only links to; the real controls are in the Services panel).

---

## Reaching Settings

- Sidebar ŌåÆ **Settings** (Help group; always shown, never hideable from the rail).
- Chord **`g` `,`** (global, outside text inputs / palette).
- Palette (`Ctrl+K`) ŌåÆ "Settings" panel item.

Route: `/settings`. Root testid: `settings-panel` (Operator) / `settings-newbie` (Guided).

---

## Guided settings (`SettingsNewbie`)

Three cards, no left nav. Centered, max-width 820px.

| Card | Control | Values | Storage |
|------|---------|--------|---------|
| **Theme** (`newbie-settings-theme`) | native `<select>` | Light / Dark / **Match my system** | `refringence-console-theme` |
| **AI mode** (`newbie-settings-ai`) | radio group | On-device only / On-device with cloud help / Cloud first | `refringence-console-ai-tier` |
| **About** (`newbie-settings-about`) | read-only + link | App: Console ┬Ę Version: v0.1 ┬Ę **See full settings** (`newbie-settings-see-more`) ŌåÆ sets persona `seasoned` | ŌĆö |

Theme writes apply immediately: toggles `.dark` on `<html>` (resolving `system` via `prefers-color-scheme`) and broadcasts `console-theme-change`. AI mode writes the tier key only (no side effects). On mount, both Guided and Operator AI sections run a one-shot legacy migration: any `refringence-console-anthropic-key` in localStorage is pushed into the keystore via `bridge.ai.setKey('anthropic', ŌĆ”)`, then the localStorage key is removed and `refringence-console-ai-keys-migrated=1` is set so it runs once.

Guided settings deliberately omit Density, Provider keys, Ollama, Eval tools, Sidebar editor, Project folder, and Services. "See full settings" flips to Operator to expose them.

---

## Operator settings (`SettingsSeasoned`)

Left nav (`w-40 sm:w-48`) with five sections, content pane on the right. Sections (`SECTIONS`): **Display**, **AI**, **Sidebar**, **Project**, **Services**. Default active: `display`.

### Display section

Card 1 ŌĆö three `SegmentedGroup` fields:

| Field | Options (value ŌåÆ label) | Storage / effect |
|-------|-------------------------|------------------|
| **Theme** | `light`ŌåÆLight, `dark`ŌåÆDark, `system`ŌåÆSystem | `refringence-console-theme`. `writeTheme()` sets key, toggles `.dark` via `resolveDark()`, dispatches `console-theme-change`. A `system` selection installs a `matchMedia('(prefers-color-scheme: dark)')` change listener that re-toggles `.dark`. |
| **Mode** | `newbie`ŌåÆGuided, `seasoned`ŌåÆOperator | `refringence-console-persona` via `writePersona()`. Same key the top-bar mode pill writes; broadcasts `console-persona-change`. |
| **Density** | `compact`ŌåÆCompact, `roomy`ŌåÆRoomy | `refringence-console-density` via `writeDensity()`. Initial value: existing key if set, else `roomy` when persona=`newbie`, else `compact`. Persisted on first mount so other panels read a stable value. |

Card 2 ŌĆö **About**: App = `Console`, Version = `v0.1` (static).

> Note: the top-bar `topbar-theme-toggle` only cycles lightŌåödark and persists `refringence-console-theme` as `light`/`dark` ŌĆö it cannot select `system`. The `system` option is reachable only from Settings (Operator or Guided).

### AI section

Three `Field`s plus the eval tools row.

**Tier** ŌĆö radio group, key `refringence-console-ai-tier`. Three tiers:

| id | Title | Body |
|----|-------|------|
| `on-device` | On-device only | All AI runs locally via Ollama. No data leaves the machine. **Default.** |
| `on-device-optional-cloud` | On-device with optional cloud | Local first; cloud calls allowed when you opt in per task. |
| `cloud-first` | Cloud-first | Cloud provider for every request. Requires an API key. |

**Provider keys** ŌĆö one `ProviderKeyRow` per cloud provider:

| Provider id | Name | Placeholder |
|-------------|------|-------------|
| `openai` | OpenAI | `sk-...` |
| `anthropic` | Anthropic | `sk-ant-...` |
| `google` | Google | `AIza...` |

Each row: masked input (`type=password`, eye toggle to reveal), **Set**/**Replace** button (label flips on connected state), **Clear** button (connected only). Save path: `bridge.ai.setKey(id, key)` ŌåÆ on `{ok:false}` or `{valid:false}` shows the returned error, else clears the input and invalidates key status. Clear: `bridge.ai.clearKey(id)`. Connected badge driven by `bridge.ai.getKeyStatus()` (boolean per provider) via `useAiKeyStatus()`. **The raw key never crosses back to the renderer** ŌĆö input is write-only; status is boolean only; keys live in the OS keychain (main process). Hint text states this verbatim. Note: only OpenAI/Anthropic/Google get key fields here; Ollama needs none and Kimi has no Settings field (both still selectable as chat providers elsewhere via available-models gating).

**Ollama** ŌĆö **Detect Ollama** button ŌåÆ `useOllamaDetect().refetch()`. On `data.running`: success badge "Ollama vX, N model(s) installed". On not running: muted note "Ollama not detected on localhost:11434" + link to `https://ollama.com` (via `bridge.openExternal`, window.open fallback). On error: "Detection failed."

**Eval tools ŌĆö LangSmith** (`EvalToolsRow`):
- Status via `useLangsmithStatus()` ŌåÆ Connected / Not connected badge.
- Masked input (placeholder `lsv2_...` or "Replace key" when connected) + eye toggle. **Set**/**Replace** ŌåÆ `bridge.evals.setLangsmithKey(key)`; on `{ok:false}` or `{valid:false}` shows error, else clears + invalidates. **Clear** (connected only) ŌåÆ `bridge.evals.clearLangsmithKey()`.
- When connected: **Run eval** button (`langsmith-run-eval`) ŌåÆ `useRunEval().mutate()`. Runs a small fixed dev dataset through the user's OpenAI model, traced + scored to their LangSmith account. Result renders `passed/total passed` + "view in LangSmith" link, or the error string.

### Sidebar section (`SidebarEditor`)

Drag-reorder + show/hide editor over the canonical `GROUPS` item set. Writes through `useSidebarLayout` (`refringence-console-sidebar-layout-v1`, shape `{hidden, order, collapsedGroups}`); rail updates live.

- Rows built from `GROUPS.flatMap` after `orderItems(items, layout.order)`. Each row: grip handle (`@dnd-kit` `useSortable`, 4px activation distance), icon, label + group subtext, and a trailing control.
- Trailing control: **eye / eye-off** toggle (`setHidden(to, ŌĆ”)`) for hideable panels; a **lock** glyph + "Always visible" tooltip for `LOCKED` panels = **Overview, Release, Pipeline** (never hideable).
- Drag end ŌåÆ `arrayMove` ŌåÆ `setOrder(ids)`.
- **Reset to default** (`sidebar-editor-reset`) ŌåÆ `reset()`.
- Testids: root `sidebar-editor`; per-row `sidebar-editor-row-<label>`, `sidebar-editor-toggle-<label>`, `sidebar-editor-lock-<label>`.
- Note: every panel remains reachable from the palette even when hidden; this only affects the rail.

### Project section

- Active project path rendered in a `<code>` (`settings-project-path`), truncated, `title`=full path or "No active project".
- **Pick a folder** / **Change folder** (`settings-project-pick`) ŌåÆ `bridge.project.pickFolder()`; on `{canceled:false, path}` ŌåÆ `setProject(path)` via `useActiveProject`.
- **Clear** (when a project is set) ŌåÆ `clear()`.
- If `project.pickedAt` set: "Picked <localeString>." line.
- Active project is window-scoped (`?wid=`); recent list (cap 6) is global. (Multi-project: use the top-bar chip ŌåÆ "New project in a new window".)

### Services section

Static link-out only ŌĆö no connection controls live here. Renders a description + **Open Services** button (`<Link to="/services">`). All real connection management is in the Services panel.

---

## Service connections (in the Services panel, surfaced for completeness)

Settings ŌåÆ Services routes to `/services`. Connection state comes from `bridge.connections.*` via `useConnections()`; status per card is `loading | error | connected | disconnected`. Tokens are encrypted in the OS keychain and **never returned to the renderer** ŌĆö only boolean `connected` + safe metadata (login, user, project count).

| Service | Connect mechanism | Token shape / source | Disconnect | Notes |
|---------|-------------------|----------------------|------------|-------|
| **GitHub** | `bridge.connections.github.connect()` ŌĆö reuses `gh` CLI auth | none (login reused; source `cli.github.com`) | `github.disconnect()` | Powers Workboard, Repo, Release, Pipeline. Hero banner when disconnected. |
| **Vercel** | `vercel.connect(token)` | personal token from `vercel.com/account/tokens` | yes | Shows user + project count; enables zero-config **Deploy** (`detectDeploy`ŌåÆ`deploy`ŌåÆpoll `deployState` to READY). |
| **Sentry** | `sentry.connect(token, org)` | auth token + org slug (`sentry.io/.../auth-tokens/`) | yes | Surfaces production errors on Workboard + Observability. |
| **Slack** | `slack.connect(token)` | bot token `xoxb-` (`api.slack.com/apps`) | yes | **Map channels** dialog tags channels Tech/Non-tech/Test (`slack.setChannelTeam`). |
| Netlify, Fly.io, Railway, PostHog, Plausible, Supabase, Stripe | DocsCard only | ŌĆö | ŌĆö | "Open the provider's docs" via `bridge.openExternal(connectUrl)`; no in-app connect yet. |

**`.env` auto-connect** (`EnvConnectCard`): `bridge.env.scanConnectable(root)` reads `.env` key **names only**; **Link** ŌåÆ `bridge.env.connect(root, id)` reads the value transiently main-side to test+store, then drops it. Never shown back, never sent to AI.

## Keyboard map

Implemented in `lib/useKeymap.ts` (`useKeymap({enabled, onShowHelp})`), mounted by `CommandPalette` with `enabled={!paletteOpen}`. Handler is document-level; it **yields** (returns early) when:
- the event has `metaKey | ctrlKey | altKey` (except the palette's own `Ctrl/Cmd+K`), or
- the focused target is editable (`INPUT`, `TEXTAREA`, `SELECT`, or `contenteditable`), or
- the palette is open.

## Layout presets (`lib/consoleLayout.ts`)

Two bits ŌĆö `railCollapsed`, `chatOpen` ŌĆö persisted to `refringence-console-layout-v1`; changes broadcast via `console-layout-change` and mirror across windows via the `storage` event. `useConsoleLayout` is the single owner of the rail bit.

The top-bar **Assistant** button toggles `chatOpen` and, on open only, calls `bridge.window.growForDock(360)` to widen the OS window for the 360px dock. `detectPreset()` returns `custom` when the two bits don't match a named preset.

## Storage keys (settings-relevant)

| Key | Owner | Values |
|-----|-------|--------|
| `refringence-console-theme` | Display / TopBar | `light` / `dark` / `system` |
| `refringence-console-persona` | Mode | `newbie` / `seasoned` |
| `refringence-console-density` | Density | `compact` / `roomy` |
| `refringence-console-ai-tier` | AI tier | `on-device` / `on-device-optional-cloud` / `cloud-first` |

| `refringence-console-ai-keys-migrated` | One-shot migration flag | `1` |
| `refringence-console-anthropic-key` | **Legacy** (migrated then removed) | ŌĆö |

Cloud API keys, LangSmith keys, and all service tokens are **not** in localStorage ŌĆö they live in the OS keychain via the main process and are never returned to the renderer.
